Central Europe · EU member

Poland

A country that has been erased, occupied, rebuilt, and transformed — and is still, somehow, entirely itself.

Population 38M Capital: Warsaw Language: Polish EU member since 2004 Currency: Złoty (PLN)
POLSKA

You can't really understand how Polish colleagues tick without knowing a little of what Poland has been through. That history is the backstory that explains a lot of what you'll encounter at work.

Poland spent 123 years wiped off the map, partitioned between Russia, Prussia, and Austria. Then came WWII — Poland lost around 20% of its population, one of the highest tolls of any country. Then four decades of Soviet-imposed communism, followed by an abrupt leap into capitalism in 1989 with no manual and no safety net. In a single generation, Poland went from a command economy to one of Europe's most dynamic markets — the only EU country to avoid recession in 2008–09.

That's a lot of disruption to absorb. Polish working culture sits in a interesting middle ground — more formal and hierarchical than Western Europe in some ways, more direct and pragmatic than people often expect. Geographically and historically caught between East and West, the culture reflects both, and neither fully.

Polish working culture is shaped by history as much as by any management theory. Here's what you'll likely encounter.

They'll be straight with you
Polish colleagues tend to say what they mean. If something isn't working, you'll probably hear about it — directly, without much wrapping. This can catch people off guard if they're used to more diplomatic feedback cultures, but it's worth reframing: directness here is a form of respect, not hostility. It means they're taking you and the work seriously. The absence of complaint is generally a good sign.
Trust takes time — and it's personal
Don't expect deep collaboration to switch on from day one. Polish colleagues often take a while to open up, and that's not coldness — it's just how trust gets built. The key thing is that trust tends to be personal rather than institutional — a process or a contract doesn't create it, a relationship does. Time spent on informal conversation, showing up consistently, remembering small things — that's what moves the needle.
Skepticism as a form of engagement
Don't expect a lot of vocal enthusiasm in meetings. Polish colleagues more often show investment by asking hard questions and raising objections than by expressing excitement. If someone is challenging your proposal in detail, they're probably the most engaged person in the room. Hollow positivity — "this is amazing!", "great work!" without substance — tends to land as hollow rather than encouraging.
Formality is the default starting point
Polish professional culture tends to be more formal than Scandinavian or Anglo-American workplaces, especially in older companies. Titles and seniority carry weight. That said, the post-1989 generation and the tech sector have shifted things considerably. A practical rule: start formal and follow their lead. If they move to first names, follow. If they don't, don't push it.
Hierarchy is real — but so is the pragmatism
Decisions in more traditional Polish companies can sit firmly at the top, and juniors may wait for seniors to set direction in meetings. At the same time, Polish workplaces — particularly in younger companies — can be surprisingly flat in practice. The formal structure and the actual way things get done don't always match, so it's worth paying attention to both.
Practical tips
→
Start a bit formal, let them set the pace. Pan (Mr) / Pani (Ms) + surname is the safe opening in traditional settings. In international teams it relaxes quickly — just take the cue from them.
→
Invest in the relationship before you need it. A shared coffee, remembering something they mentioned last week — that investment is how the working relationship gets built.
→
Match their directness. Plain, honest, and respectful works better than diplomatic mush.
→
Don't mistake quiet for disengagement. Restraint and focus are not the same as indifference.
→
Be on time. Poland runs on linear time. Showing up late without notice is a small but real credibility dent.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out — things that seem obvious from the inside and invisible from the outside. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

Hospitality is non-negotiable
If you visit a Polish colleague's home and decline food or drink, you'll cause real discomfort — even if you're not hungry. Accepting, even symbolically, is part of the social contract. The host's obligation is to provide; the guest's is to receive. This applies to more than food: if something is offered with genuine care — help, a connection, a favour — the appropriate response is acceptance, not a reflexive "I don't want to impose." Refusing repeatedly reads less as politeness and more as rejection.
In practice: Accept something. Even a biscuit or a glass of water signals that you're comfortable and that you respect the hospitality being offered.
History is personal, not academic
WWII, the partitions, the Soviet occupation, Katyń, Solidarity — these aren't distant events for most Polish people. They're carried across generations, talked about at family tables, and felt as personal as family history. Poland lost around a fifth of its population during WWII — a proportion unmatched by any other country. That weight shapes the culture's relationship with memory, identity, and the question of what it means to survive. Treating these events as pub-quiz material or being visibly indifferent when they come up will land badly.
In practice: You don't need to be an expert. Showing real curiosity — asking questions, listening — is enough.
The person who matters isn't always the person with the title
In Polish workplaces, the actual influence and the official hierarchy don't always match. There's often someone without a senior title who has been there longest, knows everyone, and whose opinion quietly shapes what gets decided. This person may say very little in formal meetings — but people check with them privately, and their read on a situation tends to be accurate. Pay attention to who others defer to informally, not just who chairs the meeting. Finding and building that relationship early is usually worth more than navigating the org chart.
In practice: Before an important meeting, ask your Polish contact — "is there anyone else whose input matters here?" The answer is often illuminating.
Warmth isn't given to strangers — it's earned
Unsolicited warmth from someone you don't know can read as odd or insincere in Poland. A Polish colleague who seems reserved when you first meet isn't being unfriendly — they're just treating you appropriately for the stage of the relationship, which is early. The warmth comes later, and when it does it's real, durable, and accompanied by a loyalty that people from more surface-warm cultures sometimes find surprising. Trying to accelerate warmth by being more effusive tends to have the opposite effect. Showing up consistently and following through is what moves things forward.
In practice: Don't perform friendliness — just be real. Show interest, ask real questions, follow through on things you said you'd do.
Knowing something about Poland matters
Poland has spent much of its modern history being overlooked or reduced to a handful of clichés. When someone makes the effort to know something real — a city beyond Warsaw, a historical event, a film, a musician — it signals that you see Poland as something worth knowing. It doesn't take much, but the effect is disproportionate to the effort.
Easy entry points: Chopin, Robert Lewandowski, the Solidarity movement, the film Ida, the fact that Poles cracked the Enigma code before the British.
Name days — the birthday you didn't know about
Every name has a corresponding day in the Polish calendar, and many Poles celebrate their imieniny (name day) as seriously as — sometimes more than — their birthday. Unlike a birthday, name days are predictable: the calendar is fixed and public. Remembering a colleague's name day and acknowledging it — even briefly — is a small gesture that demonstrates you paid attention to something personal. Remembering it unprompted is a small thing that lands as a large one. Looking it up takes thirty seconds; the effect is disproportionate.
In practice: A quick search for "Polish name day calendar" will tell you when your Polish colleagues' name days fall. Worth two minutes.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures isn't about bad intentions — it's about different defaults. Here's what's often going on underneath.

Polish culturemeetswarm, open-by-default cultures
"They seem cold and hard to read"
Polish warmth is reserved for people you know, not extended as a default to everyone. It's not a closed door — it's a door that opens gradually. Once it does, the relationship tends to be durable. Don't mistake the starting point for the whole picture.
What helps: Don't try to accelerate warmth by being more effusive — it tends to have the opposite effect. Show up consistently, follow through, ask real questions.
Polish culturemeetshigh-positivity cultures
"I can't tell if they're actually on board"
Polish colleagues tend to show engagement through focus, hard questions, and critical input rather than expressed excitement. A Polish colleague who says "I see two problems with this" is often more invested than one who says "sounds good."
What helps: Ask directly — "what do you think needs work here?" rather than "are you happy with this?" You'll get more honest signal.
Polish culturemeetsvery direct cultures
"They shut down after critical feedback"
Polish colleagues are direct — but directness operates within a relationship. When blunt feedback comes from someone you don't know well, it doesn't land as efficient honesty — it lands as aggression, even if the intention was good. The same observation, delivered by a trusted colleague, is taken as helpful. Delivered by someone unfamiliar, it closes down rather than opens up. Building even a basic human connection before delivering critical feedback changes its entire reception. A few genuine interactions — not warmth performed for strategic reasons — is usually enough to shift the register.
What helps: Build the relationship first. Even a few informal interactions before a difficult conversation changes how feedback is received.
Polish culturemeetsflat-hierarchy cultures
"Junior colleagues seem passive in meetings"
In more hierarchical Polish environments, juniors may wait for seniors to set direction before weighing in. The assumption is that seniority earns the right to frame the discussion first. This can make a meeting look quiet or one-sided to a colleague expecting open debate from the start. Ask specifically — "I'd like to hear your view on this" directed at a named person — and the input tends to follow. The silence is a queue, not a wall. A direct invitation usually opens it.
What helps: "I'd really like to hear your take on this" directed at specific people works better than an open invitation to the room.
Polish culturemeetsdebate cultures
"They don't push back in the meeting, then disagree afterwards"
For many Polish colleagues, raising objections publicly, especially to someone senior, feels unnecessarily confrontational. The conversation after the meeting is often the real one. If a Polish colleague asks for a word after a meeting, take it seriously.
What helps: Share the agenda and key questions in advance. Time to think before speaking makes public contribution much more likely.

Five things about Poland worth knowing — good conversation starters, better for understanding where your Polish colleagues are coming from.

1
The food is extraordinary — you just won't find it in restaurants
Polish cuisine is one of Europe's great undiscovered ones, and it's undiscovered for a specific reason: it lives at home. Pierogi, bigos, żurek, gołąbki, barszcz — these dishes are deeply tied to family kitchens, Sunday tables, and grandmothers' recipes. If a Polish colleague ever invites you for a home-cooked meal, say yes without hesitation.
Conversation opener: Ask what their family makes at Christmas or Easter. Strong opinions about whose grandmother does it best are guaranteed.
2
Chopin, Copernicus, Curie — and the endless fight to keep them
All three have been claimed or quietly appropriated by other countries over the centuries. Copernicus is claimed by Germany. Marie Curie is often listed as French in Western accounts. Chopin was half-French and lived most of his adult life in Paris. For a country whose sovereignty was erased for 123 years and whose territory and population were fought over repeatedly, the question of who gets credit for what is not trivial. For Poles, asserting their Polishness is not pedantry — it's a matter of historical record and national dignity. Knowing this context, and acknowledging it, is worth something.
Conversation opener: Ask which of the three they feel most strongly about — there's usually a favourite and always a story.
3
Sports icons and the art of celebrating against the odds
Poland is not a country that expects to dominate global sport — which makes every success feel enormous. Lewandowski carries national pride well beyond football. The volleyball team has won multiple world championships. And Adam Małysz — a ski jumper from a small mountain town — became one of the most beloved figures in modern Polish history. These aren't just athletes; they're proof of something.
Conversation opener: Mention Małysz to anyone over 35. Watch what happens.
4
Polish cinema is one of Europe's finest — and barely anyone outside Poland knows it
Andrzej Wajda and Krzysztof Kieślowski shaped world cinema in ways rarely attributed back to Poland. Kieślowski's Dekalog (1988) — a ten-part series set in a Warsaw housing estate, meditating on mortality, faith, and moral choice — is considered among the greatest achievements in the history of the medium. Pawlikowski's Ida (2013) and Cold War (2018) brought Polish cinema back to international attention. Poland has won the Academy Award for Best International Feature Film three times. Asking a Polish colleague what they think is the most important Polish film tends to produce a considered, personal answer.
Watch list: Dekalog (start with ep. 1 or 5) · Cold War (2018) · Ida (2013)
5
Poland's tech scene is quietly world-class
CD Projekt Red — the studio behind The Witcher and Cyberpunk 2077 — is one of the most acclaimed game developers in the world. Polish mathematicians cracked the Enigma cipher in 1932, years before Bletchley Park. Warsaw has grown into a serious European tech hub that consistently flies under the radar. The gap between Poland's reputation and its actual output is one of the more interesting stories in European tech.
Conversation opener: Ask a Polish colleague what they think of CD Projekt. Strong opinions guaranteed.
South Asia · Largest democracy

India

One of the world's oldest civilisations and its largest democracy — a country of profound diversity, ambition, and contradiction. A place that's been negotiating difference for three thousand years, and a workplace culture where relationships, context, and hierarchy carry more weight than most org charts suggest.

Population 1.4B Capital: New Delhi Languages: Hindi, English + 21 other official Currency: Indian Rupee (INR)
INDIA

India isn't a single culture — it's a civilisation containing multitudes. There are 22 officially recognised languages — but around 122 languages spoken by significant numbers of people, and hundreds more beyond that. Dozens of regional identities, multiple major religions, and centuries of layered history coexist within one country. A colleague from Chennai and a colleague from Chandigarh may share citizenship but live in very different cultural worlds.

What they do share is a set of broader tendencies that shape how work gets done: a deep respect for hierarchy and seniority, a strong preference for relationship-first trust, a communication style that prioritises harmony and context over bluntness, and a remarkable capacity for creative problem-solving under constraint. Understanding these patterns, and where they come from, is the foundation for working well across the difference.

India's working culture is shaped by hierarchy, relationships, and a strong tendency to find a way — even when the answer is technically no. The gap between what's said and what's meant is real, and navigating it is the core skill.

Hierarchy is real — but it's relational, not bureaucratic
Seniority matters enormously. Junior colleagues may not challenge a senior in a meeting even when they disagree, and decisions often flow from the top. But hierarchy here isn't cold command-and-control — it comes wrapped in care and loyalty. A good senior manager is expected to invest in their people personally, not just professionally. The deference flows upward; the responsibility flows both ways. Crucially: if you acknowledge the power dynamic openly and use it with care, it becomes real respect. If you exploit it, the deference continues but becomes hollow — and the chances of anyone raising a real problem with you drop to near zero the higher up you are.
In practice: If you're senior, people will defer to you by default. Explicitly invite pushback — and mean it — or you simply won't get it. The most senior person in the room speaking last helps.
"Yes" has many meanings — and "no" is rarely said directly
Indian communication is often high-context. A direct "no" can feel unnecessarily harsh or relationship-damaging. "Yes, but...", "we'll try our best", or even enthusiastic silence may be closer to "this is difficult." Preserving the relationship while managing expectations is the cultural priority here.
In practice: Get specific. "Can this be done by Friday?" is less useful than "what would need to be true for this to be done by Friday?" The second question surfaces the real constraints.
Relationships come before transactions
Business in India tends to start with getting to know each other — sharing food, asking about family, understanding who someone is before what they want. Jumping straight to agenda can feel cold or even disrespectful. The relationship isn't the prelude to the work — it is part of the work.
In practice: Build in time for real small talk. Ask about their city, their weekend, their family. It's not wasted time — it's trust-building.
Time is flexible — until it isn't
India operates on what's sometimes called polychronic time — a more fluid relationship with schedules, multiple priorities running in parallel. But this coexists with real dedication when something matters. The key variable is often whether the relationship makes the deadline feel important. Deadlines tied to a person tend to land better than deadlines tied to a process.
In practice: If a deadline is hard, explain why. "The client needs this for a board presentation on Monday" changes things. Context travels.
Jugaad: the art of making it work — and its limits
Jugaad literally means a temporary fix or workaround — improvising, adapting, making something work with what's available. It's celebrated as a cultural strength, and the creative ingenuity behind it is real. But it's worth knowing that the word itself carries a slightly double-edged quality: making something work provisionally isn't always the same as making it work properly. The instinct to find a way is an asset; the risk is when a workaround gets treated as a permanent solution.
In practice: Bring hard problems — the problem-solving instinct is real and often impressive. But for anything structural or long-term, it's worth confirming whether the solution is built to last.
Practical tips
→
Don't mistake deference for agreement. Someone nodding along may be showing respect, not committing to a course of action.
→
Reciprocate on the personal. Ask about family, remember what people told you last time, acknowledge festivals and milestones. It counts more than you'd expect.
→
Don't rely on the last-minute email for anything important. If it matters, call first. Relationships are the channel; email is the record.
→
Give critical feedback in private, in person, and with care. Ego and honour matter in Indian working culture. Feedback delivered in front of peers — even well-intentioned — can be felt as a public humiliation and damage the relationship seriously. If you want someone to change, find them one-to-one.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out — things that seem obvious from the inside and invisible from the outside. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

Food and hospitality are acts of care, not courtesy
Being offered food — especially in someone's home — is meaningful. Refusing repeatedly causes real discomfort. Diet and religion intersect here in complex ways: many people are vegetarian for religious reasons, others observe fasting periods, and dietary identity can be deeply personal. Be curious rather than surprised, and follow the lead of your host.
In practice: Accept something. If you have dietary restrictions, say so warmly — it will open a conversation, not cause awkwardness.
Family is not just background — it's a decision-making framework
Family is often the lens through which major decisions get made — career moves, relocations, big commitments. Parents and extended family often have real input. This isn't unusual; it's expected, respected, and part of how major decisions get made responsibly.
In practice: When a colleague says "I need to think about it" before a big decision, "thinking" often includes consulting people outside the office.
Religion and festivals are woven into working life
Diwali, Holi, Eid, Navratri, Onam, Pongal, Christmas — India observes an enormous range of holidays across communities and regions. These aren't just days off. They're cultural and spiritual touchpoints that shape the rhythm of the year for hundreds of millions of people. Acknowledging them signals that you see the whole person, not just the colleague.
In practice: "Happy Diwali" or "Happy Eid" to the right person goes a long way. Worth knowing which colleagues observe what.
Cricket is the closest thing to a shared national religion
Cricket cuts across class, language, and region in a way almost nothing else does in India. The Indian Premier League — a two-month cricket tournament held once a year — is followed with an intensity that combines the Super Bowl, the Champions League, and a national holiday all at once. India vs. Pakistan matches are events that stop the country. You don't need to know the game deeply; you just need to know it matters.
Conversation opener: Ask about the last IPL season or India's Test result. You will not run out of conversation.
India is not a monolith — and Indians know this better than anyone
A colleague from Tamil Nadu and a colleague from Punjab may share citizenship but speak different languages, eat different food, follow different religious traditions, and carry different cultural defaults. Treating "Indian culture" as one thing can inadvertently flatten the differences that matter most to the people living them.
In practice: Ask where someone is from. It's not intrusive — it's interesting, and the answer often opens up the conversation in unexpected ways.
Titles and forms of address carry real weight
"Sir" and "ma'am" are still common in many Indian workplaces — not as subservience, but as a form of respect that carries warmth. Using someone's first name with a senior colleague may require an explicit invitation. Formality here is not distance — it's a way of signalling that you take the person seriously.
In practice: Follow the lead of your Indian colleagues. If they introduce someone as "Ramesh sir," that's the register to match until told otherwise.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures isn't about bad intentions — it's about different defaults. Here's what's often going on underneath.

Indian culturemeetslow-context, task-driven cultures
"They agreed in the meeting, then nothing happened"
Agreement in the room may have been relational rather than operational — "I hear you and I want this to work" rather than "I commit to X by Thursday." The relationship was preserved; the execution plan wasn't established. The direction was real; the execution plan wasn't yet established. The two things are separate in this cultural grammar.
What helps: Close meetings with specific names and dates attached to actions. "So Priya, you'll send the draft by Wednesday?" works better than a summary email that nobody reads.
Indian culturemeetsvery direct cultures
"I can never tell what they actually think"
Direct disagreement — especially upward — can feel unsafe in many Indian working environments. Critical opinions often come through side channels, indirect language, or careful silence. It's not opacity — it's a different grammar of disagreement, one shaped by a culture where maintaining the relationship is a priority, not a weakness.
What helps: Create smaller settings for honest input. 1:1s and informal chats often surface what group meetings won't.
Indian culturemeetsflat-hierarchy cultures
"They seem overly formal and deferential"
Formality in India is a form of respect, not distance. The idea that a senior can be casually challenged or that everyone has an equal voice in a meeting can feel disrespectful from within an Indian cultural frame. It's not a power play — it's a different map of how respect gets expressed.
What helps: Explain your norms explicitly — don't assume them. "We really do want everyone's opinion, including yours" said clearly, and repeated, creates the permission that your flat-hierarchy default assumes but doesn't communicate.
Indian culturemeetsdeadline-driven, low-context cultures
"They over-promise and under-deliver"
Saying "yes, we'll make it happen" is partly genuine optimism, partly relationship management, and partly the jugaad belief that something can be figured out. Willingness and execution are more separated here than in low-context cultures — a different model of commitment rather than any absence of it.
What helps: Build in review points. "Let's check in on Thursday to see where this stands" removes the binary of hit/miss and creates space for course correction before it becomes a problem.
Indian culturemeetsdistributed, empowered team structures
"Everything goes through one person — it's a bottleneck"
In hierarchical organisations, authority to decide or communicate often sits with one senior person. Delegation without explicit authorisation can feel presumptuous or even insubordinate. What looks like a bottleneck from outside may look like proper order from inside.
What helps: Have the explicit conversation. "I'd like Ananya to be able to make calls on this directly — is that okay?" makes the delegation visible and gives it legitimacy.

Five things about India worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your Indian colleagues are coming from.

1
The mathematical tradition that changed the world — and barely gets credited
Zero was invented in India. So was the decimal system. Brahmagupta formalised the concept of zero in the 7th century CE. The number system we call "Arabic numerals" was actually Indian in origin, transmitted through Arab mathematicians to Europe. Modern computing, in a very real sense, runs on an Indian idea. Ramanujan — self-taught in rural Tamil Nadu, producing theorems that mathematicians are still unpacking — is possibly the most extraordinary mathematical mind of the 20th century.
Conversation opener: Ask a mathematically-minded Indian colleague about Ramanujan. The pride is real, and the story is remarkable.
2
Bollywood is bigger than Hollywood — and it's not what most people think
India produces more films per year than any other country. Bollywood (Hindi-language cinema) is the most visible internationally, but there are thriving film industries in Tamil, Telugu, Malayalam, and Kannada. RRR (2022) was one of the most spectacular action films of the decade and grossed $150M+ globally — you may never have heard of it. Indian cinema is not a niche; it's a world.
Watch list: RRR (2022) · Lagaan (2001) · Dil Chahta Hai (2001) · Masaan (2015)
3
The IT revolution wasn't an accident — it was a 30-year bet
In the 1980s, India invested in technical education at scale — the IITs, Regional Engineering Colleges, and a network of institutions — just as global demand for software talent was about to explode. What looked like coincidence was infrastructure that took a generation to build. The Indian diaspora in Silicon Valley didn't happen by chance. The most consequential industrial policy of the late 20th century may have been India's bet on engineering education.
Conversation opener: Ask your Indian colleagues whether they had family pressure to be an engineer or a doctor. The stories are often funnier than you expect.
4
The Mahabharata and Ramayana are sacred texts — not mythology
These are not the Indian equivalent of Greek myths — stories you can debate, deconstruct, or treat as intellectual material. For hundreds of millions of Hindus, they are living religious texts, as sacred as the Bible is to Christians or the Quran to Muslims. Characters like Arjuna, Rama, or Krishna are not literary figures to be analysed — they are revered. The instinct to treat them the way a Western university seminar would treat Achilles or Odysseus is an instinct to suppress. That said, if an Indian colleague brings them up themselves, it's a rich conversation — just follow their lead on register and tone.
Worth knowing: References to these texts do appear in everyday conversation and professional life — but the appropriate response is curiosity and respect, not comparative literary analysis.
5
India invented management consulting before McKinsey was born
The Arthashastra — written by Chanakya around 300 BCE — is a treatise on statecraft, economics, military strategy, and governance that reads like a surprisingly modern management manual. It covers taxation, HR, competitive intelligence, and ethical leadership with a rigour that would impress any business school. One of its more quoted lines: "A person should not be too honest. Straight trees are cut first and honest people are screwed first." Chanakya was not naive.
Conversation opener: Mention Chanakya to someone who knows the name. Strong opinions are guaranteed.
North America · Global cultural exporter

United States

The most exported culture in the world — and one of the most consistently misread. American working culture is optimistic, fast, direct, and built on a mythology of individual reinvention that is sincere and occasionally at odds with reality. Knowing the difference is the key skill.

Population 335M Capital: Washington D.C. Language: English Currency: US Dollar (USD)
USA

Here's the paradox: the United States is the most familiar foreign culture in the world, and one of the most frequently misunderstood. Films, TV shows, music, and the internet have exported American surface culture everywhere — the vocabulary, the aesthetics, the cadence of how Americans talk. What they haven't exported as cleanly is the underlying operating system: the values, assumptions, and unspoken rules that make the surface behaviour make sense.

The result is that people from other countries often arrive in American working environments thinking they already understand it — and then find that things don't quite work the way they expected. The warmth turns out to be more transactional than it seemed. The directness turns out to have its own limits. The egalitarianism turns out to coexist with serious hierarchy. What follows is an attempt to describe the patterns that hold broadly — with the acknowledgement that the US is a continent-sized country and the difference between working in New York, Houston, and Portland is not trivial.

What to expect when working with American colleagues — the patterns, defaults, and unspoken rules that shape how things actually get done.

Positivity is the professional default — and it's not quite what it seems
American professional culture runs on optimism. "Great to meet you," "amazing work," "this is so exciting" — the register is warm, energised, and relentlessly forward-leaning. To most of the world, this sounds like deep enthusiasm. It is often more like ambient social weather. Positivity is the baseline register here, not a signal of exceptional feeling. The meeting that ends with "this was fantastic!" might have been fine, good, or actually fantastic. The words alone don't tell you which.
Directness — but only in one direction
Americans are comfortable saying what they want, what they think, and what they need. Requests are made clearly, disagreement is often expressed without much softening, and debate is welcomed. What's less comfortable is delivering truly critical feedback, sitting with conflict, or saying something that might seriously upset the relationship. The directness is real; it just has a ceiling. Above a certain level of uncomfortable truth, the culture tends to go indirect, vague, or quietly avoidant.
Time is money — and they mean it
Meetings start on time. Agendas are circulated. There is a strong preference for efficiency over process — "let's just get this done" rather than "let's make sure everyone has been fully heard." Rambling, over-contextualising, or spending the first twenty minutes on pleasantries can read as wasting everyone's time. The American meeting is an execution environment, not a consensus-building ritual.
Individual ownership is a real value
Americans are raised on a mythology of individual agency — that what you achieve is down to you, that initiative is rewarded, and that waiting to be told what to do is a weakness. In practice this means colleagues often take ownership without being asked, advocate loudly for their ideas, and expect to be credited individually for their contributions. Collective credit, shared modesty, or deferring to the group can be misread as lack of ambition or investment.
The flat hierarchy — it's real, and it has limits
American workplaces often look and feel remarkably flat. First names for everyone, open-plan offices, CEOs who get their own coffee. Junior employees challenge senior ones in meetings. The culture does value ideas over rank, at least in principle. But formal authority still exists, and it tends to emerge visibly when stakes are high or someone has gone around the wrong person. The flatness is real day-to-day; the hierarchy surfaces when it matters.
Networking is a professional skill, not a social one
In many countries, mixing business and personal relationships is either natural or mildly uncomfortable. In the US, it is a structured professional practice with its own norms. LinkedIn is not optional. Keeping in touch with former colleagues, making introductions, and explicitly building your professional network are expected parts of career management. "Let's connect" is not an empty phrase — it's an actual action item.
Practical tips
→
Match the energy, at least partially. You don't have to be an evangelist — but responding to American enthusiasm with flat affect reads as disengaged or unfriendly.
→
State your point early. American communication is bottom-line-up-front. Start with what you want or think, then explain why. Context after conclusion, not before.
→
Follow up in writing. Verbal agreements can evaporate. A brief email confirming what was decided is normal practice and protects everyone.
→
Accept the small talk. "How are you?" is not a real inquiry — "good thanks, you?" is the correct response. Treating it as an invitation for reflection will confuse the interaction.
→
Volunteer your accomplishments. In many cultures, letting your work speak for itself is the right move. In the US, if you don't advocate for your own contributions, there is a real chance nobody else will.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out — things that seem obvious from the inside and invisible from the outside. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

"How are you?" is a greeting, not a question
This trips up almost every foreign visitor at least once. "How are you?", "how's it going?", "how are things?" are phatic expressions — social rituals, not genuine inquiries. The expected response is brief and positive: "good, thanks" or "great, how are you?" Launching into an honest answer about how things actually are produces a moment of social confusion that is awkward for everyone involved. The question is a handshake, not an opening.
In practice: Brief, positive, reciprocal. "Good thanks — busy but good, you?" is the entire correct answer to this question in a hallway.
Litigation-awareness shapes communication in invisible ways
The US is one of the most litigious societies in the world, and this shapes professional communication in ways almost invisible to Americans but striking to outsiders. Certain things are not said, certain commitments are not made in writing, certain topics are carefully avoided — not because of interpersonal discomfort but because of legal risk. HR conversations, performance discussions, and terminations follow scripts that have been shaped by decades of employment law. What can seem like evasion or bureaucratic strangeness is often just legal caution.
In practice: Don't interpret carefully worded HR language as personal coldness. It's institutional. The warmth or lack of it will show up in other ways.
Work is identity — more so than almost anywhere
"What do you do?" is often the second question in a social conversation in the US, asked within minutes of meeting someone. This isn't superficiality — it reflects a culture where what you.ve built, achieved, or contributed is considered central to who you are. The flip side is that unemployment, career change, or underemployment carry social weight that feels disproportionate to many outsiders.
In practice: Have a clear, confident answer to "what do you do?" ready. How you frame your work — not just what it is — shapes the impression you make in the first five minutes.
The culture is allergic to silence
Silence in an American meeting or conversation tends to be filled — quickly. A pause of more than a few seconds can feel uncomfortable, broken by someone offering something to fill it. For colleagues from cultures where silence signals thought or respect — Japanese, Finnish, or many Eastern European norms — this can feel like being constantly interrupted. It isn't personal; it's an extremely low silence-tolerance culture.
In practice: If you need a moment to think, say so explicitly — "give me a second" signals thought rather than discomfort and buys you the pause without creating anxiety in the room.
Politics has entered the workplace — and it's complicated
The US is in an era of significant political polarisation, and the workplace is not insulated from it. Some organisations are openly values-driven; others work hard to be explicitly apolitical. Topics like abortion, gun rights, and immigration can escalate unexpectedly. As a foreigner, the safest position is curious and non-committal — genuine questions are usually fine; positions, even mild ones, can land badly in ways that are hard to predict.
In practice: Read the room before the conversation, not during it. If the organisation skews strongly one way, the other way is not a safe place for casual comment — in either direction.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures isn't about bad intentions — it's about different defaults. Here's what's often going on underneath.

American culturemeetslow-affect cultures
"They're so fake — all that enthusiasm can't be real"
The enthusiasm is sincere in its own way — it's just not calibrated the same way. In American professional culture, warmth and positivity are social defaults, not exceptional signals. "This is amazing" does not mean what it would mean from a Finnish colleague. The register is higher, the baseline is warmer, and the words have less individual weight. Adjust the scale, not your assessment of the person's character.
What helps: Pay less attention to adjectives and more attention to actions. What someone does — follows up, delivers, makes an introduction — tells you more about how they actually feel than their vocabulary.
American culturemeetscultures that expect ongoing directness
"They said everything was fine, then blindsided me in a performance review"
American managers are often deeply uncomfortable delivering critical feedback in real time. The cultural instinct is to preserve the relationship, stay positive, and address problems through formal channels when they become unavoidable. This means "you're doing great" can genuinely coexist with a private view that there are significant issues — which then surface suddenly in a formal context. It's not bad faith; it's conflict-avoidance with institutional structure around it.
What helps: Ask explicitly and regularly — "is there anything I should be doing differently?" creates the opening. Most American managers, if directly asked, will give more honest feedback than they'd volunteer.
American culturemeetscultures where self-promotion is uncomfortable
"Every conversation turns into a sales pitch"
Americans are raised to advocate for themselves, their ideas, and their organisations. Pitching — making a case, building excitement, selling a vision — is a core professional skill that starts young and runs deep. What reads as bragging or exhausting over-selling in many cultures is just normal professional communication in the US. The persuasive mode is the default mode.
What helps: Try to hear the enthusiasm as information about priorities rather than personal performance. And know that a little self-advocacy from your side will land better than you expect — it's the expected register.
American culturemeetshigh-accountability cultures
"They agreed to everything, and then nothing actually changed"
American agreeableness in meetings is real — and so is the gap between in-meeting agreement and post-meeting action. Saying "yes, absolutely, let's do that" is often a social move rather than a firm commitment. It keeps the energy positive and avoids conflict in the room. Whether it results in action depends on factors — competing priorities, the importance of the relationship, whether it got written down — that aren't always visible in the moment.
What helps: End meetings with explicit named actions and send a follow-up email. Written commitments carry significantly more weight than verbal ones in American professional culture.
American culturemeetsclear-hierarchy cultures
"I can't figure out the actual hierarchy — who makes decisions here?"
The deliberately flat surface of American workplaces can make authority hard to read. Everyone is on first-name terms, everyone seems to have opinions, and nobody announces their rank. But authority is real, and decisions do ultimately sit somewhere. The flatness is a communication style, not an organisational structure.
What helps: Watch who gets the last word, whose silence makes the room wait, and who people check with before committing. The hierarchy is visible in behaviour even when it's invisible in language.

Five things about the United States worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your American colleagues are coming from.

1
The "self-made" myth is the most powerful story in American culture — and the most contested
The idea that anyone, from any background, can succeed through effort and determination is a foundational cultural belief that shapes how success is celebrated, how failure is judged, and how inequality is explained. It is also one of the most empirically contested stories a wealthy country has ever told about itself. Social mobility in the US is lower than in most comparable countries. The zip code you're born in is one of the strongest predictors of where you end up. The gap between the myth and the data is one of the central tensions in contemporary American life — visible in how people talk about poverty, and how they talk about billionaires.
Conversation opener: Ask an American colleague what the American Dream means in practice today. The range of answers — from sincere to cynical to complicated — tells you a lot about who they are.
2
The scale is disorienting — and it shapes everything
The United States is not just a large country — it is a continent-sized economy with internal variation greater than most inter-country variation in Europe. The distance from New York to Los Angeles is roughly the same as London to Baghdad. Regional cultures are distinct: the South, the Midwest, the coasts, Texas, and the Mountain West carry different values, communication styles, and relationships to work. A New Yorker and an Oklahoman may share a passport and a language and very little else. Treating "American culture" as a single thing is a useful approximation — worth holding lightly.
Conversation opener: Ask an American where they're from and what's different about it. Regional pride runs deep and the answers are usually illuminating.
3
American football makes no sense — until it suddenly makes all the sense
To most of the world, American football is baffling: the game stops every ten seconds, requires 22 specialists, and includes a position whose only job is to kick the ball. And yet the Super Bowl is the single most-watched annual television event on the planet, and the NFL is the most financially successful sports league in history. The sport is a near-perfect expression of American cultural values — obsessive about strategy and data, rewarding specialisation, combining individual athleticism with complex team coordination. College football adds another layer: in much of the South and Midwest, the state university team carries the kind of tribal identity European football clubs carry in their cities — but more intense, and wrapped in traditions that tie alumni, town, and institution into something close to civic religion.
Conversation opener: Ask an American colleague from the South or Midwest about their college team. Clear your afternoon.
4
American philanthropy and volunteerism are not what they look like from outside
The US has a weaker social safety net than most comparable wealthy democracies — lower baseline unemployment support, incomplete universal healthcare, less robust public housing. What it has instead is an extraordinary culture of private giving and voluntary community action — foundations, nonprofits, corporate social responsibility, and individual philanthropy operating at a scale without equivalent elsewhere. Bill Gates spending his retirement on global health isn't anomalous; it's the operating logic of a society where private action is expected to compensate for what government doesn't provide. Understanding this changes how you read American generosity — it's real, and it's also structural.
Conversation opener: Ask an American colleague whether they volunteer anywhere. A surprising number do, and the answer tells you a lot about how they see the relationship between individual responsibility and collective welfare.
5
America invented the 20th century's sound — and keeps reinventing it
Jazz, blues, rock and roll, hip-hop, country, soul, funk, musical theatre — an almost comical share of the defining popular music forms of the last hundred years were either invented in the US or so fundamentally shaped by American artists that their origins are inseparable from the culture. And almost all of it traces back to the African American experience — the most creatively generative cultural force of the modern era, producing art forms that were exported globally, often with the originators receiving the least credit and the least money. Jazz came from New Orleans. Blues from the Mississippi Delta. Hip-hop from the Bronx. Understanding any of these forms means understanding something real about America.
Conversation opener: Ask an American colleague what music they grew up with. The answer — and which America it comes from — tells you a lot.
South America

Brazil

A continent masquerading as a country — and a culture unlike anything else on earth.

Population 215M Capital: Brasília Language: Portuguese Currency: Real (BRL)
BRASIL

Brazil is the fifth largest country in the world by area and the sixth by population — a continent masquerading as a country, with internal variation in culture, dialect, climate, and working norms that makes generalisations riskier here than almost anywhere. A colleague from São Paulo and a colleague from Salvador, Bahia are operating from meaningfully different cultural defaults. The Amazonian north and the gaucho south carry distinct identities that Brazilians themselves take seriously.

What holds across most of this diversity is a set of values rooted in miscigenação — the blending of Indigenous, African, and European influences that produced a culture unlike any other on earth. Brazil has the largest African diaspora population outside Africa, and the cultural inheritance of that history — in music, in food, in religion, in warmth and expressiveness — runs through Brazilian life at every level. This is the living foundation of what Brazilian culture actually is.

The country also has a complicated 20th century: a military dictatorship from 1964 to 1985, followed by a democratic transition that coincided with hyperinflation, economic chaos, and eventually one of the most dramatic poverty-reduction stories in modern history. Brazilians who came of age in the 1980s and 1990s carry a particular pragmatism about institutions and a scepticism about official narratives that is entirely earned.

What to expect when working with Brazilian colleagues — the patterns, defaults, and unspoken rules that shape how things actually get done.

Warmth and relationship are the starting point — not the conclusion
Brazilian professional culture leads with warmth and personal connection in a way that can surprise colleagues from more reserved cultures. The greeting matters, the small talk is real, and the time spent establishing who someone is before discussing what you want from them is the actual infrastructure of the relationship. Colleagues who skip this and go straight to business signal — without meaning to — that they don't understand what they're building on.
The jeitinho brasileiro — finding a way
Jeitinho — literally "little way," meaning a creative workaround or improvised solution — is one of the most distinctive features of Brazilian working culture. In a country where official processes have historically been unreliable or bureaucratic, the ability to find the path that achieves the result has become a cultural skill and a source of quiet pride. Brazilian colleagues are often excellent at making things work under constraint. The counterpart is a corresponding flexibility about rules — the question is less "what does the procedure say?" than "what actually needs to happen here?"
Hierarchy exists — but the register is warm across it
Brazilian organisations tend to have real hierarchical structures, and decisions typically sit with senior people. At the same time, the social register across levels is unusually warm and personal — first names, physical affection in greetings, real interest in each other's lives. The authority is real; the distance is not. A senior person who lunches with junior colleagues, asks about their families, and treats them as full human beings is the norm, not the exception.
Time is flexible — and energy follows relationships
Brazilian professional culture has a relaxed relationship with time that varies by region (São Paulo runs tighter than Salvador) and by sector (international business runs tighter than domestic). What is more consistent is that when the relationship is strong and something genuinely matters, the energy and commitment that follows tends to be exceptional — enthusiastic, sustained, and often well beyond what was formally required.
Communication is expressive and optimistic-facing
Brazilian communication tends to run warm, positive, and expressive — physically demonstrative, emotionally present, and oriented toward maintaining a good atmosphere. Direct bad news, plain criticism, and visible conflict are all culturally uncomfortable. What you hear in a meeting tends to be more optimistic than the reality behind it. The real picture surfaces in private, in trusted relationships, when someone asks directly.
Practical tips
→
Invest in the personal opening. Ask about family, football, the city. The relationship is being built in those first minutes and it matters more than the agenda.
→
Say yes to food and social moments. Brazilians build trust at the table. Declining routinely signals indifference to the relationship.
→
Be explicit about hard deadlines. Flexibility is the default assumption. If something is immovable, say so plainly and explain why.
→
Don't schedule important things in February. Carnival effectively closes Brazil. Plan around it.
→
Follow up in writing. Warm agreement and firm operational commitment are different things. A friendly confirming message makes it concrete.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out — things that seem obvious from the inside and invisible from the outside. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

Brazil is not one culture — and Brazilians know it
The differences between São Paulo (fast, international, business-driven), Rio (warmer, more relaxed, image-conscious), the Northeast (African cultural influence strongest, warmest hospitality, different rhythm entirely), and the South (European immigration, cooler, closer to Argentine norms) are real and significant. Asking where someone is from and treating the answer as meaningful context signals attention that Brazilians notice and appreciate. Assuming Brazil is uniform will produce consistent miscalibrations.
In practice: Ask early where someone is from and what that means. The differences are real and most Brazilians are happy to explain them, usually with strong opinions about other regions.
Race is present and complicated — and Brazilians navigate it differently
Brazil has a complex relationship with race that is distinct from North American or European frameworks. The official ideology of democracia racial — racial democracy — has long been contested, and the reality of structural racism is increasingly acknowledged and debated. At the same time, identity categories, the language used to discuss race, and the social norms around the topic differ enough from Anglo-American contexts that assumptions imported from outside tend to land badly in both directions. Following the lead of Brazilian colleagues rather than imposing an external frame is the more useful approach.
In practice: Listen more than you speak on this topic. Ask questions rather than making observations. The conversation is live and nuanced in ways that outsiders rarely grasp quickly.
Carnival is not just a party
Carnival in Brazil — particularly in Rio, Salvador, and Recife — is one of the most complex cultural events on earth. The samba schools of Rio spend a year preparing a performance that lasts forty minutes and organise social life in the favelas throughout the year. The axé and frevo traditions of the Northeast are distinct. Showing real curiosity about Carnival — asking which city someone prefers, what their family does, what it means beyond the international image — opens a dimension of Brazilian life that work talk rarely reaches.
In practice: Ask a Brazilian colleague where they spend Carnival and what it means to them personally. The answer reveals a great deal about where they're from and who they are.
Football is identity at a level that's hard to overstate
Brazil has won the World Cup five times, produced Pelé, Ronaldo, Ronaldinho, and Neymar, and has a football culture woven into the fabric of daily life. Club loyalties are fierce and regional. The 2014 World Cup defeat against Germany — 7-1, at home, in the semi-final — is referred to simply as o Sete a Um and remains a cultural wound that Brazilians bring up themselves, with a mixture of dark humour and real grief. Understanding that this wasn't just a football result but a collective national trauma tells you something real about how Brazilians relate to sport.
In practice: Ask which club a Brazilian colleague supports before asking about the national team. Club loyalty runs deeper and tells you more about where someone is from.
Hospitality is serious and generous
If a Brazilian colleague invites you somewhere, the expectation is real generosity and the expectation is that you'll receive it properly. Showing up late, refusing food, treating the gesture as casual — these cause discomfort. The host gives abundantly; the guest receives with appreciation. The relationship is being deepened at the table, not just maintained.
In practice: Accept what's offered. Eat with interest. Ask about what you're eating. Bring something when invited to a home. The gesture of reception matters as much as the gesture of giving.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures isn't about bad intentions — it's about different defaults. Here's what's often going on underneath.

Brazilian culturemeetsstructured, deadline-driven cultures
"They're warm and enthusiastic and then the deadline passes"
The warmth and enthusiasm were real. What wasn't established was a firm operational commitment. Social agreement and delivery commitment are different things in a relationship-first culture, and the second depends on the strength of the personal connection and the clarity of what was actually agreed. The relationship needs to be strong enough that the commitment feels personal, not just contractual.
What helps: Confirm clearly and warmly in writing after the meeting. Make the operational commitment explicit — separate from the relational warmth.
Brazilian culturemeetsdirect-feedback cultures
"I can't tell what's actually going on — everything always sounds positive"
The positive-facing register is culturally maintained even when things are difficult. Problems get softened, concerns get deferred, and the real picture often only surfaces in private conversation. The slight hesitation, the vague answer, the optimism that's just a little too smooth — these are worth paying attention to.
What helps: Create private, low-stakes moments for honest conversation. "Just between us — what's the hardest part of this right now?" gets you much closer to reality than any formal check-in.
Brazilian culturemeetsprocess-oriented cultures
"They found a workaround that bypassed the agreed process"
The jeitinho — the creative workaround — produces results in environments where official processes are slow or unreliable. From a Brazilian perspective, the goal was achieved efficiently; from a process-oriented perspective, the agreed procedure was circumvented. Both readings are accurate; the friction comes from different assumptions about which matters more.
What helps: Be explicit about which parts of the process are non-negotiable. Brazilian colleagues will respect hard limits — they just need to know which ones are real.

Five things about Brazil worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your Brazilian colleagues are coming from.

1
Brazil is the only Portuguese-speaking country in the Americas — and it happened almost by accident
The Treaty of Tordesillas in 1494 divided the New World between Spain and Portugal along a meridian line — and Brazil fell just within the Portuguese side, almost by chance. The result is that a country of 215 million people speaks a language that connects them to Portugal and a handful of African nations rather than to any of their neighbours. Brazilian Portuguese has evolved distinctly from European Portuguese — in accent, vocabulary, and grammar — and Brazilians are rightly proud of what their language has become. The linguistic isolation from Spanish-speaking neighbours has shaped a particular Brazilian self-understanding as something distinct — not just another Latin American country but a civilisation with its own specific texture.
Conversation opener: Ask a Brazilian colleague what they think is most misunderstood about Brazil by Spanish-speaking Latin Americans. The answer is usually immediate and revealing.
2
The Amazon regulates the climate of South America
The Amazon rainforest contains approximately 10% of all species on earth, produces around 20% of the world's freshwater discharge, and generates its own rainfall system — the "flying rivers" of atmospheric moisture that water the agricultural regions of southern Brazil and neighbouring countries. Deforestation in the Amazon doesn't just affect the Amazon: the feedback loops between forest cover, rainfall, and agriculture mean that what happens in the Basin affects crop yields thousands of kilometres away. For Brazilians, the Amazon is a political, economic, and identity question that sits at the centre of the country's relationship with itself and with the world — not an abstraction.
Conversation opener: Ask a Brazilian colleague whether they've been to the Amazon and what it was like. Those who have usually describe it with a specific kind of awe.
3
Bossa nova changed jazz — and emerged from a specific moment in a specific place
In the late 1950s, a small group of musicians in the Zona Sul of Rio de Janeiro — particularly around Ipanema and Copacabana — developed a style that combined samba rhythms with jazz harmonies and a new, intimate vocal approach. João Gilberto, Tom Jobim, and Vinícius de Moraes produced The Girl from Ipanema, Corcovado, and dozens of other standards that became part of the global musical vocabulary. Bossa nova is one of the most significant Brazilian contributions to world culture, and asking a Brazilian colleague about it — particularly one from Rio — usually opens something personal and specific.
Conversation opener: Ask a Brazilian colleague from Rio which bossa nova song means most to them or which they'd play to someone who'd never heard it. The answer is usually immediate.
4
Pelé is not just a footballer — he is a national symbol of what Brazil believes about itself
Edson Arantes do Nascimento — Pelé — was born into poverty in Três Corações, Minas Gerais, and became the only player to win three FIFA World Cups (1958, 1962, 1970). He scored over 1,000 career goals and was declared a national treasure by the Brazilian government — making it illegal to transfer him abroad. For Brazilians, Pelé represents something beyond sport — the idea that Brazil at its best produces something the world has never seen. His death in December 2022 was mourned with a depth that surprised much of the world but not Brazilians.
Conversation opener: Ask a Brazilian colleague over 40 what Pelé meant to them growing up. The answers tend to be personal and specific in ways that go well beyond football.
5
Brasília was built from scratch in under four years — and UNESCO recognised it within 27
President Juscelino Kubitschek commissioned Oscar Niemeyer and Lúcio Costa to design an entire planned capital city in the cerrado interior, which was built between 1956 and 1960. The buildings — the National Congress, the Presidential Palace, the Supreme Court — are among the most distinctive examples of modernist architecture anywhere. UNESCO inscribed Brasília as a World Heritage Site in 1987, just 27 years after it was built — an almost unprecedented speed of recognition for a living city. Asking a Brazilian colleague what they think of Brasília — whether they love it, find it strange, have been there — usually produces a strong and immediate opinion.
Conversation opener: Ask a Brazilian colleague whether they think Brasília worked as an experiment. The conversation about Brazil's relationship with ambition and planning tends to follow.
South America · Andean

Peru

One of the oldest continuous civilisations on earth, a country of extraordinary geographic and cultural range, and a working culture shaped by hierarchy, warmth, and a deep pride in what Peru has produced and survived.

Population 33M Capital: Lima Language: Spanish Currency: Sol (PEN)
PERÚ

Peru is one of the oldest continuous civilisations on earth. The territory that is now Peru was home to the Caral civilisation five thousand years ago, to dozens of cultures across the millennia that followed — Chavín, Moche, Tiwanaku, Wari, Chimú — and then to the Inca Empire, the largest empire in pre-Columbian America, which by the early 16th century stretched from what is now Colombia to central Chile. The Spanish conquest that dismantled it in the 1530s was one of the most dramatic collisions in history, and Peru has been living with that collision ever since.

That history is present, contested, and part of everyday identity in ways that vary by region, background, and generation. Lima, the coastal capital and home to roughly a third of the country's population, operates in a different register from Cusco, the Inca capital in the highlands, or from the Amazonian cities of Iquitos and Pucallpa. The internal diversity — geographic, ethnic, and cultural — is the first thing to grasp. A colleague from Lima and a colleague from the Andean interior may share citizenship, but the cultural distance between them can be considerable.

What tends to run consistently is a set of values that shape the working environment across these differences: strong respect for hierarchy and seniority, a relationship-first approach to trust, warmth combined with a degree of initial reserve, and a deep pride in Peruvian culture — in the food, the history, the landscape — that is easy to engage with and worth engaging with seriously.

Peruvian working culture is hierarchical, relationship-driven, and shaped by a strong sense of regional identity. Here is what you are likely to encounter.

Hierarchy is taken seriously
Peruvian professional culture tends to be hierarchical in a way that shapes who speaks, who decides, and how disagreement gets expressed. Senior people set direction; junior colleagues tend not to challenge them directly, particularly in group settings. Decisions sit with those who have authority, and attempts to route around the hierarchy — going over someone's head, pushing for a decision before the right person is in the room — tend to backfire. Understanding the formal structure and working with it, rather than around it, is the more effective approach.
Relationships before transactions
Trust in Peruvian professional life is personal and takes time. The personal warmth of an early interaction is the beginning of a relationship, not a signal that it already exists. Investing time in knowing people before expecting things from them is how the place actually functions. Colleagues who skip this step tend to find that formal agreements produce less effort and less goodwill than they expected.
Indirect communication about difficulty
Saying no directly, flagging a problem plainly in a meeting, or delivering unwelcome news to a senior colleague are all culturally uncomfortable. The communication style tends to be harmonious and face-preserving in formal settings, which means concerns get softened, problems get deferred, and the real picture often only surfaces in private conversation once trust has been established. A mild hesitation or a careful non-answer is usually doing more work than it appears.
Regional and class differences shape the room
Peru's internal diversity — between Lima and the provinces, between the coast, the highlands, and the Amazon, between different socioeconomic backgrounds — shapes working relationships in ways that are real and not always visible to outsiders. The formal register that works in a Lima office may read as cold in other contexts; the warmth that works in the provinces may seem overfamiliar in a formal Lima setting. Paying attention to where someone is from and what that means is some of the most useful observation you can do.
Warmth builds gradually
Peruvian warmth tends to be durable once established, but it doesn't come instantly. The initial register can be reserved, polite, and somewhat formal — particularly in Lima and in professional settings. The formal hierarchy is the starting point. The informal influence map matters at least as much. The warmth that comes later, once trust is built, tends to be durable and often shapes the working relationship for years.
Practical tips
→
Start formal, follow their lead. Titles and surnames as the default opening; first names when they offer it.
→
Invest in the meal. Lunch, dinner, sharing food — this is where Peruvian relationships actually get built. Take it seriously.
→
Be explicit about hard deadlines. Flexibility is the default assumption. If something is actually firm, say so clearly and explain why.
→
Never embarrass anyone publicly. Criticism and corrections belong in private. Public correction is a serious social damage in Peruvian professional culture.
→
Show real curiosity about the culture. The food, the history, Machu Picchu and what lies beyond it — Peruvians carry enormous pride in their cultural heritage. Engaging with it sincerely opens things.

The cultural norms and unspoken rules that shape Peruvian professional life — things that are obvious from the inside and invisible from the outside.

Limeño exceptionalism — and resentment of it
Lima dominates Peru economically, politically, and culturally in ways that produce real friction with the rest of the country. Many Peruvians from outside Lima carry a complicated relationship with the capital — a recognition of its importance alongside a resentment of how it overshadows everything else. Assuming Lima equals Peru is a quick way to signal that you haven't been paying attention. Asking where someone is from, and showing real interest in what that place is like, lands very differently from treating the capital as the default.
In practice: Ask where someone is from early — and follow up with a real question about the place. The answer tells you a great deal about how they see themselves.
Food is identity, not just cuisine
Peruvian cuisine has undergone a remarkable transformation in the past two decades and become one of the most celebrated in the world, drawing on Indigenous, Spanish, Japanese, Chinese, and African influences into something entirely distinct. For Peruvians, food is a source of real national pride — not just as gastronomy but as a statement about the richness and complexity of the culture. Showing real curiosity about food, asking about regional dishes, asking where someone learned to cook — this is one of the most direct routes into a real conversation with a Peruvian colleague.
In practice: Ask a Peruvian colleague what most foreigners get wrong about Peruvian food. The answer is usually specific, enthusiastic, and opens something real.
The pre-Columbian heritage is personal, not touristic
Machu Picchu, Cusco, the Inca Trail — these are the things international visitors know. But for Peruvians, the pre-Columbian heritage is a living part of identity. The question of what was lost in the conquest, what survived, and what it means now is one that Peruvians engage with across generations, regions, and political positions. Treating it with real curiosity rather than as a list of sights signals something about the quality of attention you're bringing.
In practice: Ask which pre-Columbian culture a Peruvian colleague finds most compelling beyond the Inca. The answers go well beyond what the tourist circuit covers.
Punctuality varies significantly by context
The coastal cities, Lima in particular, run on a more fluid relationship with time than northern European norms, but the degree of flexibility shifts with context, seniority, and sector. A formal meeting with a senior client is different from a social gathering, which is different again from a business lunch. Reading the context is more useful than applying a single rule.
In practice: For formal first meetings, arrive on time regardless. For recurring social or semi-social events, read the room and follow your Peruvian colleagues' lead.

Where Peruvian working culture creates friction with other styles — and what is actually going on.

Peruvian culturemeetsstructured, deadline-driven cultures
"They agreed and then it didn't happen"
Social agreement and operational commitment are different things, and in a relationship-first, hierarchy-conscious culture, commitment tends to depend on the strength of the personal connection, the clarity of the ask, and whether the right person actually authorised it. A yes from someone who doesn't have authority, or from someone who doesn't yet trust you enough to push on your behalf, is a social yes — not an operational one.
What helps: Confirm in writing after the conversation. Make sure the right person has actually committed, not just the room. "Just to capture — you'll have X by Wednesday, confirmed?" converts the social agreement into an operational one.
Peruvian culturemeetsdirect-feedback cultures
"They never flag problems until it's too late"
Raising problems plainly, particularly upward or in a group setting, is culturally uncomfortable. The preference for harmony in formal settings means that concerns get softened or deferred, and the real picture tends to emerge in private conversation once the relationship is solid enough. The smooth surface is not an accurate read of what's underneath. Problems exist; they're just not being surfaced in the formal channel.
What helps: Build in private, informal check-ins — a coffee, a walk. "Just between us — is there anything that's making this harder than it needs to be?" surfaces what the meeting doesn't. Frame it as care about how things are going, not oversight.
Peruvian culturemeetshigh-energy, expressive cultures
"I can't tell if they're engaged — the room is very quiet"
The measured initial register — restrained, polite, somewhat formal — is the starting point, not a signal about engagement level. Enthusiasm in Peruvian professional culture tends to show up as focused preparation, reliable follow-through, and careful questions — not expressed energy. The quiet colleague who delivers exactly what was agreed on time is often the most invested person in the conversation.
What helps: Measure engagement by output and follow-through rather than room energy. Ask directly and specifically — "what do you think is the strongest part of this?" — rather than waiting for spontaneous enthusiasm.

Five things about Peru worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your Peruvian colleagues are coming from.

1
Local people knew Machu Picchu long before Hiram Bingham arrived in 1911
When Hiram Bingham reached Machu Picchu in 1911, guided there by local farmers who knew it well, he described it as a "lost city" — a framing that stuck internationally but was always inaccurate. The site had never been forgotten by the people who lived nearby; it simply wasn't known to the outside world. Built in the 15th century as a royal estate for the Inca emperor Pachacuti, Machu Picchu was abandoned around the time of the Spanish conquest and left largely undisturbed for four centuries. The gap between how it is described internationally and what it actually represents — an extraordinary example of Inca architecture and engineering, built on a mountain saddle at 2,430 metres without mortar or metal tools — is itself a useful conversation with any Peruvian colleague who knows the history.
Conversation opener: Ask a Peruvian colleague which site they think is more impressive than Machu Picchu but barely known internationally. The answers are usually specific and illuminating.
2
Peruvian cuisine is one of the great culinary transformations of the 21st century
In 1990, Peruvian food was virtually unknown outside the country. By the mid-2020s, Lima had more restaurants in the World's 50 Best list than any city outside Europe. The transformation was driven by chefs like Gastón Acurio, who turned ceviche, lomo saltado, and the extraordinary range of Peruvian ingredients — more than 3,000 varieties of potato alone, native to the Andean highlands — into a global conversation. The cuisine draws on Indigenous Andean, Spanish, Japanese (the Nikkei tradition), and Chinese (the Chifa tradition) influences. For Peruvians it is a source of pride that goes well beyond food. Asking a Peruvian colleague about their favourite regional dish, or about what most foreigners get wrong about Peruvian cuisine, usually produces something immediate and enthusiastic.
Conversation opener: Ask which Peruvian dish they think deserves more international attention than it gets. The answer is rarely ceviche.
3
The Nazca Lines are one of the most actively debated mysteries in archaeology
Etched into the Peruvian desert between roughly 500 BCE and 500 CE, the Nazca Lines are a series of enormous geoglyphs — geometric shapes, animals, plants, and humanoid figures — visible in their entirety only from the air. The largest are several kilometres across. Who made them, how, and why remains actively contested. The most widely accepted theories involve astronomical calendars and water rituals, but no explanation has been definitively established. What is not contested is the extraordinary precision and scale of the work, produced by a civilisation that had no flight and no satellite imagery — and that left something visible from the sky two thousand years later.
Conversation opener: Ask a Peruvian colleague what they think the Nazca Lines were actually for. The range of answers reflects how alive the question still is.
4
Mario Vargas Llosa is one of the giants of 20th-century literature — and a controversial figure in Peru
Mario Vargas Llosa — born in Arequipa in 1936, Nobel Prize in Literature in 2010 — is one of the defining voices in the Latin American literary tradition. His novels — Conversation in The Cathedral, The War of the End of the World, The Feast of the Goat — engage directly with power, politics, military culture, and the experience of Latin American history. He is also a contested figure in Peru: his politics shifted significantly over his career, from socialism to classical liberalism, and he ran for president in 1990, losing to Alberto Fujimori in a result that Peruvians still discuss. Asking a Peruvian colleague about Vargas Llosa usually produces a strong opinion rather than a neutral answer.
Conversation opener: Ask a Peruvian colleague whether they think Vargas Llosa's novels or his politics matter more to his legacy. Few people are indifferent.
5
Peru's biodiversity is among the highest on earth — and most of it is invisible to the outside world
Peru contains around 70% of the world's different life zones and is one of the seventeen "megadiverse" countries identified by biologists. Most of that biodiversity is in the Amazonian lowlands that cover roughly 60% of the country's territory — a region largely invisible in international representations of Peru, which tend to focus on the Andes and the coast. The Peruvian Amazon contains species of plants, birds, mammals, and fish that exist nowhere else on earth, and it is under increasing pressure from deforestation, mining, and oil extraction.
