For Peruvians from the Amazon region, this is personal and immediate — felt close to home, not as an abstract policy question. Asking a colleague from Iquitos or Pucallpa about the Amazon usually opens something that work conversation rarely reaches.
Conversation opener: Ask a Peruvian colleague which part of the country they think is most underrepresented internationally. The Amazon comes up often — and the answer is usually specific.
Oceania · Constitutional monarchy

Australia

English-speaking, culturally exported through sport and film, apparently straightforward — and carrying an operating logic that is distinctly its own. The egalitarianism is real, not performed. The informality is structural, not casual. The discomfort with pretension runs deep enough to function as a social enforcement mechanism.

Population 26M Capital: Canberra Language: English Currency: Australian Dollar (AUD)
AUSTRALIA

Australia was founded as a British penal colony in 1788 — a fact that has left a specific residue in the national character: a distrust of authority, a sympathy for the underdog, a suspicion of people who put on airs. The twentieth century produced the Anzac tradition — the experience of Gallipoli and the World Wars that forged a national identity around mateship, stoicism, and showing up for each other under pressure. And the last fifty years have produced a multicultural society: over 30% of Australians were born overseas, and the cultural makeup of Sydney and Melbourne is as diverse as almost anywhere on earth.

Two things belong in any introduction. The first is the relationship with Indigenous Australians: Australia's First Nations peoples have the oldest continuous cultures on earth — over 65,000 years — and the history of colonisation, dispossession, and the ongoing work of reconciliation is present in public life in ways that have no real equivalent in most other Western countries. The second is the land itself. Ask almost any Australian where they feel most themselves and it usually involves somewhere outside. The landscape — vast, ancient, often unforgiving, and strikingly beautiful — is the other thing Australians carry with them.

Australian working culture is egalitarian, direct, and shaped by a deeply held suspicion of pretension. Here is what you are likely to encounter.

Flat hierarchy — and it means it
Australian workplaces tend to be genuinely flat. First names are used at every level, the boss makes their own coffee, the CEO sits in the open plan, and pulling rank explicitly — or expecting deference simply because of your title — creates real friction. This is not a performance of egalitarianism; it is the actual operating logic. Capability, results, and how you treat people are the currencies that matter. Titles and credentials are noted but not deferred to, and a person who leads with their status rather than their substance tends to be assessed accordingly.
Direct, but not aggressive
Australian professional communication is direct — feedback is honest, disagreement is expressed, and the expectation that you will say what you mean rather than carefully manage what you communicate is genuine. Australian directness tends to be delivered without edge — matter-of-fact rather than confrontational, and usually accompanied by enough warmth that it doesn't land as an attack. The bluntness is not hostile. It is, in its own way, a form of respect — the assumption that you are robust enough to hear the honest version.
Tall poppy syndrome: the social cost of standing out
Tall poppy syndrome — the cultural tendency to cut down people who position themselves as superior or better than others — is one of the more important things to understand about Australian professional culture. Self-promotion is met with suspicion. Excessive formality or credential-leading creates discomfort. The appropriate register is competent, capable, and modest about it — letting the work speak, not the title. This does not mean false self-deprecation; it means the Australian instinct is to demonstrate rather than claim.
Results over process — with a preference for getting on with it
Australian workplaces tend to value practical outcomes over elaborate process. There is a cultural impatience with bureaucracy, excessive consultation, and the kind of organisational politics that prioritises internal navigation over getting things done. The instinct is to identify what needs doing and do it with the minimum friction required. This produces workplaces that can move fast and adapt well — and occasionally ones where documentation and process rigour get underweighted.
Work-life balance is taken seriously
Australia has a genuine culture around personal time, family, and life outside work. There is more social permission to leave at a reasonable hour, to take annual leave without guilt, and to treat the weekend as genuinely off. Performing overwork — staying late visibly, sending emails at midnight, making your schedule a point of pride — tends to read as either disorganised or insecure rather than impressive. The colleague who works hard and well during reasonable hours is the Australian professional ideal.
Practical tips
→
Match the informality from the start. First names, relaxed register, no need to establish credentials before a real conversation. Formality reads as stiffness and sometimes as arrogance.
→
Let the work speak. Don't lead with your CV or credentials. Demonstrate capability through what you do and let others draw their own conclusions.
→
Be direct, and expect directness back. Plain, honest, and delivered without drama is the working register.
→
Take the after-work drink seriously. The pub, the Friday afternoon beer, the team barbecue — these are where Australian workplace relationships are actually cemented.
→
Don't bring excessive corporate formality to an informal culture. Slide decks when a conversation would do, overly managed communications, meeting agendas for a quick chat — all signal that you haven't read the room.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

Self-deprecation is the approved mode of self-expression
The appropriate way to acknowledge success in Australian culture is to minimise it, make a joke at your own expense, or attribute it to luck and the people around you. Someone who accepts a compliment directly — "yes, I did do a great job on that" — creates a mild but real social friction. The self-deprecation is not false modesty; it is the register through which Australian professional culture expresses confidence without triggering the tall poppy reflex. Getting the calibration right — modest enough to be socially comfortable, not so self-deprecating that you undermine your own standing — is the actual skill.
In practice: When receiving a compliment, deflect it generously toward the team or the circumstances. When someone else does the same, read it correctly as confidence rather than insecurity.
Banter is a bonding mechanism — but it has rules
Australians use humour, teasing, and irreverence as social glue. A colleague who gives you a hard time about something small is usually signalling that they like you — the banter is a form of inclusion. The colleague treated with excessive politeness and careful formality is often the one being kept at arm's length. Learning to receive banter with good humour, and to give it back in kind, is one of the faster routes to being accepted in an Australian team. The key rules: it should punch sideways or down at the situation, not up at someone's background or identity; and it stops being banter the moment the other person isn't enjoying it.
In practice: Take it well. Give a little back when you feel the register. The relationship is usually in the teasing.
The land and outdoors are not small talk
Asking an Australian about a camping trip, a beach, a national park, or a recent hike is not making conversation about the weather. The relationship with the natural landscape is personal, specific, and often deeply felt — particularly for Australians who grew up outside the major cities. The colleague who can engage with the outdoors dimension of Australian life tends to reach a warmer register faster than one who can't. You don't need to have been there yourself; curiosity about where they've been and what it was like is enough.
In practice: Ask where they go. The answer is usually specific and enthusiastic, and tends to go somewhere more real than standard professional small talk.
The reconciliation conversation is live and ongoing
Australia is in the middle of an unresolved national conversation about its relationship with First Nations peoples — the history of colonisation, the Stolen Generations, the land rights movement, the ongoing gaps in health and life expectancy. The 2023 Voice referendum — a proposal for a constitutionally enshrined Indigenous advisory body, which was defeated — was a significant moment in this conversation. Treating reconciliation as a distant historical matter, or as a topic to navigate around, misreads where Australia is. The colleague who shows curiosity and some knowledge tends to access a more honest and interesting conversation than the one who stays on safer ground.
In practice: Read a little before you arrive. Knowing what the Voice referendum was, knowing what "Country" means in First Nations usage, knowing who the Stolen Generations were — these are not a high bar and they signal something real about whether you're paying attention.
Don't call it a shrimp on the barbie
The Paul Hogan tourism campaign from the 1980s — "I'll slip an extra shrimp on the barbie for ya" — is one of the more reliably irritating things you can reference to an Australian. They're called prawns. The broader point is that Australians are aware of the clichés — the larrikin, the endless sunshine and beaches, the Crocodile Dundee — and find most of them tiring. Coming in with curiosity about contemporary Australia — the cities, the culture, the politics — lands considerably better than recycling the postcard.
In practice: Ask about where they live, what they're following in the news, what they think about current Australian politics. The country has a rich and complicated present that is considerably more interesting than its tourism identity.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures is not about bad intentions — it is about different defaults. Here is what is often going on underneath.

Formal or hierarchical culturesmeetsAustralian culture
"They're so informal — I can't tell if they're taking this seriously"
The informality in Australian professional culture is not a sign of low stakes or low commitment. It is the operating register of a culture that has concluded that formal signals of seriousness are not actually correlated with the quality of the work. The colleague who uses your first name from the first meeting, makes a self-deprecating joke before the presentation starts, and sits with their coffee in the open plan is often extremely capable and very focused. The register is casual; the standards are not.
What helps: Assess by output and engagement, not by formality. The Australian colleague who seems relaxed may be doing their best work precisely because the environment isn't burning energy on performance.
High-context or indirect culturesmeetsAustralian culture
"They're too blunt — there's no tact and it damages relationships"
Australian directness — delivered matter-of-factly, without extensive relational wrapping — can land as harsh to colleagues from cultures where softening difficult messages is a significant part of professional communication. The intent is almost never hostile. Australian directness comes from a cultural assumption that adults can handle honest information and that managing people's feelings so carefully that the message gets obscured is a form of disrespect. Once you calibrate for the register, the feedback is usually easier to act on than more wrapped versions.
What helps: Name the gap early. "I'm used to more framing around critical feedback — can we find a way of working that suits both of us?" is a conversation most Australian colleagues will have directly and without defensiveness.
Hierarchical culturesmeetsAustralian egalitarianism
"Nobody seems to want to take charge — there's no leadership"
In a flat-hierarchy culture, leadership tends to be exercised through influence, expertise, and persuasion rather than positional authority. The person who says "I'm in charge, do it this way" without earning that authority through demonstrated capability tends to find that the formal compliance it generates is thin and quietly resisted. The Australian working assumption is that authority is demonstrated, not claimed.
What helps: Establish credibility through what you do. Ask good questions, show what you know through substance, and earn the room's confidence before you try to direct it.
Indirect culturesmeetsAustralian working culture
"They say everything is fine until suddenly it isn't"
The Australian directness has specific limits around interpersonal conflict and personal criticism within teams where social bonds are strong. Australians can be very direct about tasks and very indirect about the relationship friction underneath them — maintaining a surface of mateship and good humour while something real goes unaddressed. The problem everyone is managing around but nobody is naming tends to surface eventually, often at a moment of pressure.
What helps: Create specific space for honest check-ins on how the team is working together, not just whether the tasks are on track. The mateship culture makes this easier than it sounds — Australians generally respond well to being asked directly how they're going.

Five things about Australia worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your Australian colleagues are coming from.

1
The Anzac tradition is the emotional centre of Australian national identity
On 25 April 1915, Australian and New Zealand troops landed at Gallipoli in a catastrophically unsuccessful campaign. The losses were enormous and the strategic result was failure. What emerged was a mythology — of courage, mateship, stoic endurance, and showing up for each other under impossible conditions — that became the foundational story of Australian national identity. Anzac Day is arguably the most emotionally significant public occasion in Australia. Dawn services draw enormous crowds. The tradition is not about militarism — it is about what Australians believe about themselves when things are at their worst.
Conversation opener: Ask an Australian colleague what Anzac Day means to their family. The personal connection is usually immediate.
2
Australian Rules Football is unlike anything else and is worth understanding
AFL — Australian Rules Football — is played by 18 players per side on a large oval field, involving kicking, handballing, and spectacular marking. The AFL Grand Final is the most attended domestic sporting event on earth. In Victoria, South Australia, and Western Australia, AFL team allegiance is a genuine identity marker — passed down through families, tied to suburb and class. A colleague from Melbourne who supports Collingwood and one who supports Richmond have a rivalry that is partly comedic and entirely serious. Knowing which team someone supports and why opens a conversation that goes much deeper than sport.
Conversation opener: Ask a Melbourne or Adelaide colleague which team they follow. Clear some time.
3
The multicultural reality of contemporary Australia is largely invisible from the outside
Australia is one of the most ethnically diverse countries on earth, but its international image — beaches, bush, Australians of Anglo-Celtic background — doesn't reflect this. Over 30% of the population was born overseas. Sydney and Melbourne are cities where Chinese, Vietnamese, Indian, Lebanese, Greek, and Italian communities have shaped the culture, food, and social fabric for generations. The colleague in front of you may have a surname from Vietnam, a grandmother who speaks Cantonese, and a family gathering built around a cuisine that has nothing to do with a barbecue. Curiosity about where they're actually from — without assumption — is the right approach.
Conversation opener: Ask about their family background and where they grew up. The answer is often more interesting than the passport suggests.
4
The wildlife is as extraordinary as advertised
Kangaroos, koalas, platypuses, echidnas, wombats, cassowaries, quokkas, Tasmanian devils — Australia's fauna is the product of 50 million years of isolation and is unlike anywhere else on earth. Most Australians who grew up outside the major cities have personal wildlife stories. The quokka — a small marsupial on Rottnest Island off Perth, known for appearing to smile in selfies — has become a cultural ambassador. Asking a colleague from regional Australia about the wildlife they grew up around produces stories that are usually remarkable, occasionally terrifying, and reliably entertaining. The snakes are also real. There are a lot of them.
Conversation opener: Ask what the most alarming thing they've encountered in the wild was. The answer is almost always a good story.
5
The larrikin tradition — irreverence as a national value
The larrikin is one of Australia's most persistent archetypes: the person who refuses to take authority too seriously, deflates the pompous, and gets away with it through charm and competence. The sensibility runs from the bushranger Ned Kelly to Prime Minister Bob Hawke — who held the world record for drinking a yard of beer and wore it as a credential. The larrikin tradition is the cultural root of tall poppy syndrome, of the instinct to puncture pretension, of the banter that functions as social glue. Self-importance remains one of the least tolerated qualities in an Australian workplace.
Conversation opener: Ask an Australian colleague who they think the greatest living larrikin is. You will get a strong opinion, probably a story, and a reasonable argument about whether the tradition is a feature or a bug.
South America

Argentina

A culture of extraordinary richness shaped by European immigration, political upheaval, and a resilience built from navigating both.

Population 46M Capital: Buenos Aires Language: Spanish Currency: Peso (ARS)
ARGENTINA

Argentina's history is marked by a recurring pattern: extraordinary promise followed by profound disruption. The country was, for a period in the early 20th century, one of the wealthiest in the world — Buenos Aires was known as the Paris of South America, drawing waves of European immigrants who built a society of real cultural ambition. Then came decades of political instability, military dictatorships, economic crises, and the particular trauma of the 1976–1983 junta, which disappeared an estimated 30,000 people and left wounds that have still not fully healed.

That arc — the memory of greatness, the experience of loss, the resilience required to navigate both — shapes Argentine working culture in ways that run deep. Argentines tend to be highly educated, politically engaged, emotionally expressive, and fiercely proud of a culture they know is world-class. They are also pragmatic survivors of repeated institutional failure, with a scepticism toward official systems that is entirely earned.

The country has a European cultural DNA — predominantly Italian and Spanish immigration — grafted onto a South American geography and a River Plate identity that is distinct from both. The porteño culture of Buenos Aires and the culture of the interior provinces are meaningfully different, and paying attention to where someone is from is the first useful piece of context.

What to expect when working with Argentine colleagues — the patterns, defaults, and unspoken rules that shape how things actually get done.

Passion and expressiveness are the baseline register
Argentine professional communication is warm, direct, and emotionally engaged. Debate is normal, disagreement is expressed openly, and people invest themselves in their arguments. Silence or emotional restraint in a meeting can signal disengagement rather than thoughtfulness. If someone is pushing back hard, they're usually taking the work seriously.
Hierarchy exists but informality cuts across it
Argentine organisations tend to have real hierarchical structures, and decisions often sit with senior people. At the same time, the social register across levels is typically informal and warm. First names, open doors, real interest in colleagues as people — the authority is real; the performance of distance is not.
Relationships carry the work
The personal relationship is the infrastructure on which professional commitments are built. Trust is personal rather than institutional — a contract is a starting point, not the relationship itself. Investing time in knowing people before you need things from them is how the place actually functions. A colleague who likes and trusts you will move mountains; one who doesn't will do exactly what was formally agreed and nothing more.
Creativity and improvisation are valued
Argentina has a strong cultural norm around viveza — cleverness, the ability to find a way, to think laterally, to navigate a system that often doesn't work as advertised. This produces colleagues who are often excellent problem-solvers under constraint and poor followers of rigid processes for their own sake. The instinct is to find the best solution, not necessarily the prescribed one.
Time is flexible — and commitment is personal
Argentine professional culture has a relaxed relationship with punctuality and scheduling, particularly in social and semi-social contexts. Deadlines tend to be understood as approximate unless made explicitly otherwise. The counterpart is that when something matters — particularly when the personal relationship behind it is strong — the effort tends to be disproportionate and sustained.
Practical tips
→
Invest in the personal opening. Conversation about family, football, politics — this builds the relationship. Skip it and things move more slowly.
→
Match the directness. Argentines respect people who hold their ground with reasons. Backing down too easily or being vague to avoid conflict reads as weakness.
→
Be explicit about hard deadlines. Flexibility is the default. If something is immovable, say so plainly and explain why.
→
Don't schedule around January and February. Argentine summer. Things largely slow down. Plan around it.
→
Follow up in writing. Warm verbal agreement and firm operational commitment are different things. A friendly follow-up makes it concrete.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out — things that seem obvious from the inside and invisible from the outside. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

The 1976–1983 dictatorship is living memory
The military junta that ruled Argentina between 1976 and 1983 tortured, killed, and disappeared an estimated 30,000 people — political opponents, students, journalists, trade unionists. Many working-age Argentines have family connections to this period: relatives who were disappeared, parents who were in exile. The Madres de Plaza de Mayo, who still march every Thursday, are a living reminder. This is family memory, and it shapes the particular quality of Argentine political engagement and the deep scepticism toward state authority that runs through the culture.
In practice: If the dictatorship comes up, listen more than you speak. Ask genuine questions. This is a conversation Argentines are still having with themselves.
Psychoanalysis is part of everyday life
Argentina has one of the highest concentrations of psychoanalysts per capita in the world, and Buenos Aires has more than almost any other city on earth. Therapy is a normal part of educated urban life — discussed openly, referenced casually, integrated into how people understand themselves and their relationships. Asking a Buenos Aires colleague how their analyst would interpret something is entirely unremarkable. This cultural centrality of self-reflection shapes how Argentines communicate about work, relationships, and difficulty in ways that can surprise colleagues from cultures where therapy carries more stigma.
In practice: Conversations about inner states, motivations, and emotional dynamics are more common and more accepted here than in many professional cultures. Engage with them rather than deflecting.
Criticism of Argentina belongs to Argentines
Argentines can be sharp critics of their own country — the inflation, the politics, the corruption, the recurring crises. This is a national tradition — entering the debate as an outsider rarely goes well. A foreigner who joins in the critique will find the conversation cool quickly. The self-criticism is internal; the expected response from outside is curiosity, not validation.
In practice: If an Argentine colleague vents about Argentina, listen and ask questions. Don't volunteer your own assessment of the country's problems.
The asado is a social institution
The asado — the ritual of grilling meat over fire — is both a cooking method and a social institution. An invitation to an asado is a social honour. The asador (the person managing the grill) holds a role of some prestige and takes it seriously. Showing interest in the food, in the process, in what's being served signals that you understand what you've been invited into.
In practice: Accept the invitation. Ask about what's being cooked and how. Don't offer to help at the grill unless invited — that's the asador's domain.
Football is identity, not entertainment
Argentine football operates at a level of cultural intensity that is hard to convey to people who haven't encountered it. Club loyalty is tribal, generational, and geographically rooted. Boca Juniors and River Plate divide Buenos Aires in ways that go well beyond sport. Knowing which team a colleague supports, and roughly what that means, is useful social knowledge that opens conversations revealing more about where someone is from and who they are than almost any other topic.
In practice: Ask which team they support early. It's a welcome question and the answer tells you something real about them.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures isn't about bad intentions — it's about different defaults. Here's what's often going on underneath.

Argentine culturemeetsstructured, deadline-driven cultures
"They're energetic and impressive in conversation but hard to pin down on delivery"
The energy and intelligence in the conversation are real. What wasn't necessarily established was a firm operational commitment. In a relationship-first culture with a flexible relationship with time, social agreement and delivery commitment are different things. The personal connection needs to be strong enough that the commitment feels personal, not just contractual.
What helps: Confirm clearly and warmly after the conversation. Separate the relational warmth from the operational agreement — both need to be made explicit.
Argentine culturemeetsharmony-oriented and consensus-seeking cultures
"They argue about everything — I can't tell if they're actually on board"
Open debate, pushback, and strong opinions expressed directly are signs of engagement. A room where everyone agrees quietly tends to make Argentines suspicious. The argument is how the idea gets stress-tested — if someone is challenging yours in detail, they're invested in making it work, not trying to kill it.
What helps: Engage with the substance of the challenge. Push back with reasoning. Giving ground without argument reads as lack of conviction.
Argentine culturemeetslow-affect, process-driven cultures
"Everything is a crisis — I can't tell what's actually urgent"
Argentine communication tends to be emotionally amplified — problems are expressed with urgency, enthusiasm with intensity, and the register generally runs hotter than northern European norms. This doesn't mean every urgent-sounding thing is actually critical. Calibrate against what gets followed up, not against the initial register.
What helps: Ask directly: "On a scale of things that matter — how urgent is this actually?" Argentines usually respond to direct questions with direct answers.

Five things about Argentina worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your Argentine colleagues are coming from.

1
Argentina produced two of the greatest footballers in history
Diego Maradona and Lionel Messi are both Argentine, both widely considered among the two or three greatest players ever to play the game, and both shaped entire eras of the sport. Maradona's 1986 World Cup — in which he scored the "Hand of God" goal and, five minutes later, what is widely voted the greatest goal in World Cup history — remains one of the most discussed events in Argentine cultural memory. Messi's arc — from a kid who left for Barcelona at 13, to the greatest player of his generation, to the 2022 World Cup winner at 35 — is a story Argentines tell with deep personal pride. Asking an Argentine colleague about Maradona versus Messi is one of the most reliable conversation openers on earth, and one that will reveal strong opinions immediately.
Conversation opener: Ask which one they think was the greatest and why. Expect a considered, passionate, and probably lengthy answer.
2
Buenos Aires was shaped by European immigration on an extraordinary scale
Between roughly 1880 and 1930, Argentina received one of the largest waves of European immigration in history — Italians above all, then Spanish, Jewish, Welsh, German, Syrian, and many others. At the peak, roughly a third of the population was foreign-born. This produced a culture deeply European in its reference points — the architecture, the café culture, the emphasis on education, the psychoanalytic tradition — grafted onto a South American geography and a River Plate identity that is its own distinct thing. The Italian influence in particular is everywhere: in the food, in the cadence of Argentine Spanish, in surnames, in the warmth of family culture.
Conversation opener: Ask an Argentine colleague about their family's origins. Most have a specific European story two or three generations back, and it usually connects directly to how they understand themselves.
3
Tango is grief and desire made into movement
Tango emerged in the working-class suburbs of Buenos Aires and Montevideo in the late 19th century, from the confluence of African, European, and gaucho musical traditions. It was initially considered too sensual for polite society — it was banned in several European cities before becoming fashionable there, and Argentine elites adopted it partly because Paris had. Tango is a living tradition, danced by people of all ages in milongas across the country. Asking an Argentine colleague whether they dance, or what tango means to them, usually opens something personal and specific.
Conversation opener: Ask an Argentine colleague whether their family has any connection to tango — whether someone danced, played, or sang. The answers are often surprising.
4
The Mothers of the Plaza de Mayo changed how the world thinks about human rights
Beginning in 1977, during the height of the military dictatorship, a group of mothers whose children had been disappeared began marching silently every Thursday around the Plaza de Mayo in Buenos Aires — wearing white headscarves, carrying photographs. They did this when public protest was a potentially fatal act. Their marches continued for decades, and the organisation they built became one of the most influential human rights movements in history. The white headscarf of the Madres is now one of the most recognisable symbols of peaceful resistance in the world. Understanding this history is part of understanding what Argentines mean when they talk about memory, justice, and the relationship between citizens and the state.
Conversation opener: Ask an Argentine colleague what the Plaza de Mayo means to their family or their generation. Almost everyone has something real to say.
5
Argentine literature is one of the great traditions of the 20th century
Jorge Luis Borges invented forms of fiction that influenced every writer who came after him. Julio Cortázar bent the rules of narrative in ways that are still being absorbed. Ernesto Sabato, Manuel Puig, Ricardo Piglia — the list of major Argentine writers is long and serious. For a country of 46 million people, the literary output is extraordinary, and literature is part of educated Argentine culture in a way that is more central than in many other countries. Asking an Argentine colleague which Argentine writer they think is most underappreciated internationally usually gets a considered and immediate answer.
Conversation opener: Ask which Borges story they'd recommend to someone who had never read him. The answer is always specific and often reveals something about how they think.
South America

Colombia

A country that built something extraordinary underneath the headline — and has been quietly revealing it ever since.

Population 52M Capital: Bogotá Language: Spanish Currency: Peso (COP)
COLOMBIA

Colombia spent the better part of four decades defined internationally by its conflict — the civil war between the state, guerrilla groups, and paramilitary forces that displaced millions, killed hundreds of thousands, and made the country synonymous abroad with violence and instability. The 2016 peace agreement with the FARC ended the longest-running armed conflict in the Americas. What that moment revealed was a country that had been building something extraordinary underneath the headline: a young, entrepreneurial, creative, warmly hospitable culture with a fierce pride in what it had managed to produce despite everything.

That resilience is not incidental to how Colombians work. It shapes a culture that is resourceful, relationship-driven, emotionally expressive, and deeply committed to the people it has decided to trust. It also produces a particular relationship with uncertainty and improvisation — not as failure modes to be managed, but as normal conditions to be navigated with creativity.

Colombia is also far more internally varied than outsiders tend to assume. The culture of Bogotá — urban, formal by Latin American standards, politically engaged — is meaningfully different from the warmth and Caribbean rhythm of Cartagena or Barranquilla, the industrial energy of Medellín, or the coffee-growing tradition of the Eje Cafetero. Paying attention to where someone is from is one of the first useful things you can do.

What to expect when working with Colombian colleagues — the patterns, defaults, and unspoken rules that shape how things actually get done.

Warmth is immediate and real
Colombian professional culture leads with warmth. The personal greeting is effusive — physical closeness, eye contact, real interest in the person before the agenda. The relationship is being established from the first moment, and colleagues who skip it or treat it as delay signal that they don't understand the operating system.
Relationships are the infrastructure
Who you know and who trusts you is how things actually move. Formal channels exist; personal relationships are faster, more reliable, and more likely to produce real effort. A Colombian colleague who has decided you're trustworthy will go significantly beyond what was formally agreed. One who hasn't will comply with the letter of the arrangement and little more.
Hierarchy is respected — but warmth cuts across it
Colombian organisations tend to have clear hierarchical structures and decisions sit with senior people. At the same time, the interpersonal register across levels is warm and personal. The hierarchy shapes who decides; warmth shapes how everyone interacts along the way. Challenging upward in a group setting is uncommon; honest input tends to travel through private conversation and trusted intermediaries.
Flexibility with time is the default
Meetings may start late. Deadlines are often understood as targets rather than hard limits, particularly for internal work. The cultural priority is the quality of what's being done, not its punctual completion. When something genuinely matters and the relationship is solid, the commitment tends to be real and the effort sustained.
The positive register is maintained under pressure
Colombian communication tends to stay warm and forward-looking even when things are difficult. Problems are softened, bad news is cushioned, and the instinct to preserve a positive relational atmosphere is strong. What you hear in a meeting may be more optimistic than the reality behind it. The real picture tends to surface in private, in trusted relationships, when asked directly.
Practical tips
→
Invest in the greeting. Don't rush past the personal opening. The time spent asking about family and life pays off in everything that follows.
→
Say yes to the meal. Social moments are where real trust gets built. Declining routinely signals that you don't value the relationship.
→
Be explicit about hard deadlines. Flexibility is the default assumption. If a deadline is firm, say so plainly and early.
→
Create private moments for honest input. The real picture rarely surfaces in group settings. One-to-one, in a relaxed context, is where you find out what's actually going on.
→
Follow up in writing. Warm agreement in conversation and firm operational commitment are different things. A friendly confirming message makes it concrete.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out — things that seem obvious from the inside and invisible from the outside. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

The conflict is recent and personal
The civil conflict touched virtually every Colombian family in some way — through displacement, through loss, through proximity to violence, through the particular experience of living with insecurity as a background condition for decades. For many Colombians working internationally, being reduced to the international stereotype is a familiar and tiring experience. Asking about Colombia with real curiosity, rather than leading with the conflict or narco references, opens something very different. The country people will describe to you is almost nothing like the one the headlines suggested.
In practice: Ask about the food, the music, the region someone is from, what they miss. The conversation that follows is usually vivid and immediately warm.
Regional identity runs deep
Colombia is one of the most geographically diverse countries on earth, and its regions have distinct cultures, accents, temperaments, and identities that Colombians themselves take seriously. A costeño from Barranquilla and a cachaco from Bogotá are operating from different cultural defaults — in warmth, in formality, in relationship to time. Asking where someone is from, and treating the answer as meaningful context, signals a level of attention that is noticed and appreciated.
In practice: Ask early where someone is from and let them tell you what that means. The differences are real and most Colombians are happy to explain them.
Hospitality is a serious obligation
If a Colombian colleague invites you somewhere — to their home, to their city, to a meal — the expectation is real generosity and the expectation is that you will receive it properly. Showing up late, declining food, treating the gesture lightly — these cause real discomfort. The host gives; the guest receives with real appreciation. The relationship is being built at the table as much as anywhere else.
In practice: Accept what's offered. Eat with interest. Ask about what you're eating and where it's from. Bring something when invited to a home.
Family is the primary loyalty
Family — immediate and extended — is the central organising unit of Colombian life. Major decisions involve family input. Family obligations take real precedence. Asking about family, remembering what people have told you, showing interest in children and parents — these signal that you see the whole person, not just the colleague.
In practice: Ask about family early and with real interest. Remember what people tell you. The small act of asking about someone's child at the next meeting carries disproportionate weight.
Colombia's image abroad and Colombia as Colombians know it are very different things
Colombians are deeply aware of what the world thinks of their country. Many have navigated assumptions — in airports, in conversations, in professional settings — that Colombia equals danger or instability. The country that Colombians know — the biodiversity, the food, the music, the architecture, the literary tradition, the warmth of the people — is largely invisible in that international image. Showing curiosity about the real Colombia rather than the projected one opens warmth that almost nothing else does.
In practice: Show that you know something real about Colombia beyond the obvious. Even a passing familiarity with García Márquez, with Medellín's transformation, with vallenato, registers immediately.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures isn't about bad intentions — it's about different defaults. Here's what's often going on underneath.

Colombian culturemeetsstructured, deadline-driven cultures
"They're warm and enthusiastic and then the deadline passes"
The warmth and enthusiasm in the conversation were real. What wasn't necessarily established was a firm operational commitment. In a relationship-first culture, social agreement and delivery commitment are different things — and the second depends heavily on the strength of the personal connection and the clarity of what was actually agreed. "This sounds great" and "I commit to delivering X by Friday" are not the same statement, even from the same conversation.
What helps: Confirm clearly and warmly in writing after the meeting. Separate the relational warmth from the operational commitment — both need to be made explicit.
Colombian culturemeetsdirect-feedback cultures
"I can't tell what's actually going on — everything sounds fine"
The positive register is culturally maintained in ways that mean problems often don't surface plainly in group settings or early-stage relationships. What sounds like smooth sailing may be carrying unspoken complications. The slight hesitation, the vague answer, the topic that gets changed — these are doing more work than they appear to be.
What helps: Create private, low-stakes moments for honest conversation. A one-to-one — "just between us, how is it actually going?" — surfaces what the formal meeting often doesn't.
Colombian culturemeetslow-context, task-first cultures
"They need so much relationship time before anything gets done"
The relationship time is the foundation on which reliable professional commitment is built. Skipping it doesn't accelerate the project — it makes the project less likely to go well, because the personal connection that motivates real effort hasn't been established. The practical implication: build relationship investment into the project timeline from the start. A meal before the first formal meeting, fifteen minutes of genuine conversation before getting to business — these are preparation, not delay. The investment in trust pays direct operational dividends in the form of loyalty, flexibility, and effort when it matters most.
What helps: Build relationship time into the project plan. A meal before the first formal meeting produces better outcomes than efficiency in the short term.

Five things about Colombia worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your Colombian colleagues are coming from.

1
García Márquez put Colombia on the literary map — and the tradition has continued
One Hundred Years of Solitude, published in 1967, is one of the most widely read novels of the 20th century and the work that introduced magical realism as a literary mode to a global audience. García Márquez — universally known in Colombia as Gabo — won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1982 and remains one of the most personally beloved cultural figures in the country's history. But the tradition goes beyond him: Tomás González, Piedad Bonnett, Juan Gabriel Vásquez have built a serious contemporary literature. Asking a Colombian colleague which Colombian writer they'd recommend after Gabo usually gets a considered answer and opens a real conversation.
Conversation opener: Ask what Gabo means to them personally, or which of his books they'd start with. Almost everyone has an opinion.
2
Medellín went from the most dangerous city in the world to a global model for urban transformation
In the early 1990s, Medellín had the highest murder rate of any city on earth — over 380 homicides per 100,000 people in 1991. By the 2010s the rate had dropped by over 95%, and the city had become an internationally studied model for urban renewal: cable cars connecting hillside comunas to the city centre, library parks built in the most marginalised neighbourhoods, sustained investment in public space and civic pride. The transformation of Medellín is one of the most striking urban stories of the past thirty years, and Colombians — particularly Paisas, as people from the Medellín region are known — carry real pride in it.
Conversation opener: Ask a Paisa colleague what Medellín was like when they were growing up versus what it is now. The contrast is usually immediate and specific.
3
Colombia is one of the most biodiverse countries on earth — and Colombians know it
Colombia has coastline on both the Pacific and the Caribbean, Andean peaks, Amazon rainforest, and the vast Llanos grasslands — and is home to roughly 10% of all plant species on earth. The Caribbean coast is culturally distinct from the Andean interior, with African, Indigenous, and Spanish influences producing a warmth, musicality, and rhythm that feels more like Cuba or Venezuela than Bogotá. For Colombians, the diversity of their own country is a source of real wonder, not just a tourism talking point.
Conversation opener: Ask a Colombian colleague which part of Colombia they think most visitors never see and should. The answer is usually specific and enthusiastically delivered.
4
Colombian coffee and Colombian cycling are both world-class — and connected by geography
Colombia produces some of the world's finest arabica coffee, and the coffee-growing region — the Eje Cafetero around Manizales, Armenia, and Pereira — is a UNESCO World Cultural Landscape. The same mountain terrain that produces great coffee also produced an unexpected cycling culture of remarkable depth. Nairo Quintana, Egan Bernal (Tour de France winner in 2019), and Rigoberto Urán have made Colombia one of the most consistent producers of elite climbers in the world. The connection is the geography: the same brutal climbs that coffee pickers navigate are the roads the cyclists train on.
Conversation opener: Ask a Colombian colleague from the coffee region whether they've followed Bernal or Quintana. Most have strong feelings about it.
5
Shakira and the global reach of Colombian popular culture
Colombia's cultural exports go well beyond literature. Shakira — from Barranquilla — is one of the best-selling music artists of all time, having sold over 75 million records. Carlos Vives popularised vallenato internationally. The global spread of salsa, cumbia, and more recently reggaeton all have deep Colombian roots. The warmth, rhythm, and expressiveness that characterise Colombian working culture are the same qualities that make Colombian popular culture so exportable. Asking a Colombian colleague about the music they grew up with usually opens something immediate and personal.
Conversation opener: Ask a Colombian colleague from the coast what vallenato means to them, or which artist they think the world underestimates. Strong opinions guaranteed.
Caribbean

Dominican Republic

The island where the Americas began — and a culture shaped by music, family, and an extraordinary history of survival.

Population 11M Capital: Santo Domingo Language: Spanish Currency: Peso (DOP)
REPÚBLICA DOMINICANA

The Dominican Republic shares the island of Hispaniola with Haiti — the site of the first permanent European settlement in the Americas, established by Columbus in 1492, and the location of some of the most consequential history in the Western Hemisphere. The Haitian Revolution of 1791–1804 — the only successful slave revolt in history, which produced the first Black republic in the world — reshaped the entire island and left a complicated relationship between the two countries that persists to this day.

The Dominican Republic's own 20th century was defined largely by Rafael Trujillo, who ruled with extreme brutality from 1930 until his assassination in 1961. The dictatorship left lasting marks on institutions, on the relationship between citizens and authority, and on the culture's relationship with power. What followed was a turbulent democratic transition, waves of emigration — particularly to New York — and a gradual economic opening that has made tourism and services the backbone of the economy.

What you find in a Dominican working context is a culture that is exceptionally warm, relationship-driven, expressive, and deeply anchored in family and community. The music — merengue, bachata — is one of the ways Dominican life is experienced and understood. And the diaspora dimension means that many Dominicans you encounter in international professional settings have navigated two cultures, often since childhood, producing a particular adaptability and cultural fluency.

What to expect when working with Dominican colleagues — the patterns, defaults, and unspoken rules that shape how things actually get done.

Warmth is immediate and the relationship comes first
Dominican professional culture leads with warmth, personal connection, and real interest in who you are before any agenda is established. Greetings are warm, physical, and not perfunctory. Colleagues who skip the personal opening and go straight to business are read as cold or disrespectful — not efficient. The relationship being built in the first few minutes is the infrastructure everything else runs on.
Hierarchy is respected — and expressed through warmth rather than distance
Dominican organisations tend to have clear hierarchical structures and decisions typically sit with senior people. At the same time, the social register is warm and personal across levels. A senior person who takes real interest in junior colleagues — who asks about family, who remembers birthdays, who shows up at personal moments — commands real loyalty. The authority flows through the relationship, not in spite of it.
Time is relational, not mechanical
Dominican professional culture has a flexible relationship with time anchored in relationship rather than schedule. Meetings may start late. The conversation that matters will take as long as it takes. Trying to impose a tight clock on a relationship-building conversation tends to damage both the relationship and the outcome. When something genuinely matters and the trust is solid, the effort that follows is sustained and real.
Communication is expressive and positive-facing
Dominican communication is warm, effusive, and tends to maintain a positive atmosphere even under pressure. Direct criticism, plain bad news, and visible conflict are all uncomfortable. What you hear in a meeting is often a more optimistic version of a more complicated reality. The real picture tends to surface in private, in moments of real trust, when asked directly and warmly.
The diaspora dimension
A significant share of Dominicans in international professional settings have grown up between two cultures — the island and New York, Madrid, or Montreal. This produces colleagues who are often highly adaptable, fluent in multiple registers, and skilled at reading different cultural contexts simultaneously. Don't assume the Dominican-American colleague operates on the same defaults as someone who grew up in Santo Domingo. Ask, rather than assume.
Practical tips
→
Invest in the greeting. Don't rush past the personal opening. Time spent asking about family and life pays off in everything that follows.
→
Say yes to food and social moments. Trust gets built at the table and in relaxed social contexts. Declining consistently signals indifference to the relationship.
→
Be explicit about hard deadlines. Flexibility is the default. If something is immovable, say so plainly and early, with context.
→
Create private moments for honest input. Problems rarely surface in group settings. A relaxed one-to-one is where you find out what's actually going on.
→
Follow up in writing. Warm agreement and firm operational commitment are different things. A friendly confirming message makes it concrete.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out — things that seem obvious from the inside and invisible from the outside. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

Family is the centre of gravity
Family — immediate, extended, and the broader community of trusted people — is the primary organising unit of Dominican life. Major decisions involve family input. Family obligations take real precedence. Asking about family, remembering what people have told you, showing interest in children, parents, and siblings signals that you see the whole person. It is one of the fastest ways to build real trust.
In practice: Ask about family early and remember what you're told. Coming back to a detail someone mentioned at a previous meeting carries disproportionate weight.
Respeto — respect as a lived practice
Respect — respeto — runs through how Dominicans relate to age, seniority, and the people they care about. It shows up in forms of address, in tone, in the care taken not to cause embarrassment, and in the particular weight given to treating people as full human beings. Causing someone to lose face — particularly in front of others — is a serious social damage that is hard to repair. Difficult conversations belong in private, delivered with warmth.
In practice: Never correct or criticise someone in front of peers or reports. The same message lands completely differently depending on who else is in the room.
Buen ambiente — the social atmosphere is a shared responsibility
Maintaining a warm, positive, and harmonious social atmosphere — buen ambiente — is a collective obligation in Dominican social and professional life. Introducing tension, delivering criticism harshly, or allowing a conversation to become uncomfortable reflects on everyone present. This is why problems get softened, why conflict gets deferred, and why the room tends to stay warm even when the situation isn't. The preference for formal channels is a different understanding of what good professional behaviour looks like.
In practice: If you need to raise a difficult issue, invest in the setup. Starting warmly, framing it within the relationship, and making it feel like a collaborative problem rather than a confrontation changes how it lands entirely.
The Haitian relationship is sensitive and layered
The relationship between the Dominican Republic and Haiti is one of the most complex in the Americas — sharing an island, deeply intertwined historically and economically, and marked by significant tensions including documented discrimination against Haitians and Haitian-Dominicans. This is not a topic to weigh into casually from outside. If it comes up, listen carefully and ask questions rather than offering positions.
In practice: Follow the lead of your Dominican colleague on this topic. It's a conversation Dominicans are having among themselves — your role as an outsider is curiosity and respect, not analysis.
Dominican identity abroad carries its own specific weight
Dominicans in international settings — particularly those from the New York diaspora — often navigate a complex identity that is neither straightforwardly Caribbean nor American, and that has sometimes been misread or underestimated in both directions. Showing real curiosity about where someone is from and what their story is — rather than fitting them into a pre-existing category — opens something that generic cultural assumptions rarely reach.
In practice: Ask where someone grew up and what moving between cultures has been like. Most will have a considered and honest answer.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures isn't about bad intentions — it's about different defaults. Here's what's often going on underneath.

Dominican culturemeetsstructured, deadline-driven cultures
"They're warm and agreeable and then the deadline passes"
The warmth and agreement were real. What wasn't established was a firm operational commitment. Social agreement and delivery commitment are different things in a relationship-first culture, and the second depends on the strength of the personal connection and the clarity of what was actually agreed. "That sounds great" and "I commit to delivering X by Friday" are not the same statement, even from the same conversation.
What helps: Confirm clearly and warmly in writing after the conversation. Separate the relational warmth from the operational agreement — both need to be made explicit.
Dominican culturemeetsdirect-feedback cultures
"I can't tell what's actually happening — everything sounds positive"
The positive register is culturally maintained in ways that mean problems rarely surface plainly in group settings. What sounds like smooth progress may be carrying unspoken complications. The slight hesitation, the vague answer, the topic that gets changed — these are doing more work than they appear to be.
What helps: Create private, warm, low-stakes moments for honest conversation. "Just between us — how is it actually going?" surfaces what the formal meeting often doesn't.
Dominican culturemeetslow-context, task-first cultures
"They seem to need a lot of relationship time before anything moves"
The relationship time is the foundation on which reliable professional commitment is built. Skipping it doesn't accelerate the work — it makes the work less likely to go well, because the personal connection that motivates real effort hasn't been established. The investment in trust pays direct operational dividends when something genuinely needs to be delivered.
What helps: Build relationship time into the project plan. A meal before the first formal meeting consistently produces better outcomes than efficiency in the short term.

Five things about the Dominican Republic worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your Dominican colleagues are coming from.

1
The oldest cathedral, university, and hospital in the Americas are all in Santo Domingo
La Navidad, established from the wreckage of the Santa María in 1492, was the first European settlement in the Americas. The city of Santo Domingo, founded in 1498, became the first permanent European city in the Western Hemisphere and the administrative capital of the Spanish colonial empire. The oldest cathedral, the oldest university, and the oldest hospital in the Americas are all there. For Dominicans, this history is a source of real pride — a reminder that their island was where the modern Americas began, a fact that tends to get lost in more recent narratives about the country.
Conversation opener: Ask a Dominican colleague what most visitors to Santo Domingo miss. The colonial zone usually comes up, and the pride is immediate.
2
Merengue and bachata mean something different at home than abroad
Merengue — fast, accordion-driven, the national music — became the official music of the Trujillo dictatorship before surviving to become a genuine expression of Dominican identity and joy. Bachata — slower, guitar-driven, associated with sadness and longing — was considered lowbrow for decades and banned from radio; it became globally popular only after artists like Juan Luis Guerra and Romeo Santos brought it to international audiences. Asking a Dominican colleague which they prefer, and what each means to them — beyond the international versions — usually opens something immediate and personal. The version the world knows and the version played at a family gathering are often quite different.
Conversation opener: Ask a Dominican colleague which bachata artist they think the world underestimates. Strong opinions guaranteed.
3
Dominican independence was from Haiti — not Spain
Most countries in Latin America won independence from Spain. The Dominican Republic's founding story is different: the country declared independence in 1844 from Haiti, which had occupied the entire island since 1822. Juan Pablo Duarte — the intellectual architect of independence, who spent his life in exile and died in poverty — is revered as the purest of the founding fathers precisely because he sought nothing for himself. Understanding that Dominican national identity was forged in contrast to Haitian rule, rather than Spanish colonial rule, gives essential context to a complicated set of attitudes and sensitivities that outsiders often misread.
Conversation opener: Ask a Dominican colleague about Duarte and what he represents to them. Most have a considered view of his significance.
4
The Dominican Republic produces MLB talent at a rate that defies its size
With a population of about 11 million, the Dominican Republic produces roughly 10% of all MLB players — a concentration with no parallel in world sport relative to population size. Baseball academies run by MLB franchises are a major economic force in rural communities and a real path of social mobility. Among the players who have come through: Sammy Sosa, David Ortiz, Albert Pujols, Robinson Canó, Manny Ramírez — and Pedro Martínez, born in Manoguayabo outside Santo Domingo, widely considered one of the two or three most dominant pitchers the game has ever produced. His 1999 and 2000 seasons with the Boston Red Sox are still analysed as among the greatest individual pitching performances in history, and his Hall of Fame induction speech in 2015, delivered partly in Spanish, was watched by millions on the island. Baseball is woven into Dominican communities in ways that go back generations — it is how many families understand aspiration, effort, and what it means to represent where you come from. Asking a Dominican colleague which team they follow or who their favourite player was growing up usually opens something immediate and warm.
Conversation opener: Ask a Dominican colleague over 30 where they were when Pedro pitched his best games. The answers are specific and enthusiastic.
5
Every winter, thousands of humpback whales come to Samaná Bay
Between January and March each year, North Atlantic humpback whales migrate to the warm waters of Samaná Bay on the northeastern coast to breed and give birth. It is one of the most significant humpback breeding grounds on earth, and the concentration of whales — breaching, singing, competing for mates in waters you can reach from shore — is extraordinary. The whale watching tradition in Samaná has been running since the 1980s and is taken seriously as both conservation and tourism. For Dominicans, Samaná is a point of quiet, specific pride — a place of exceptional natural beauty that most of the world knows nothing about, which is almost the point. Asking a Dominican colleague whether they've been to Samaná usually opens something personal and often wistful.
Conversation opener: Ask a Dominican colleague which part of the country they think is most underappreciated by visitors. Samaná comes up often.
South America · Andean

Ecuador

A small country that contains extraordinary range — coast, highlands, Amazon, and the Galápagos — and a working culture shaped by warmth, hierarchy, and the particular resilience of a place that has navigated a great deal of disruption.

Population 18M Capital: Quito Language: Spanish Currency: US Dollar (USD)
ECUADOR

Ecuador sits at the centre of the world — the equator runs through it, the country is named after it — and the geography alone tells you something about the range you're dealing with. In the space of a few hundred kilometres you move from the Pacific coast through the Andean highlands to the Amazon basin, with the Galápagos Islands anchoring a fourth distinct region hundreds of kilometres offshore. Each zone has its own climate, its own ethnic and cultural character, and its own working rhythm. A colleague from Guayaquil — the country's largest city, coastal, commercially driven, fast-moving — is operating in a meaningfully different register from a colleague from Quito, the highland capital, more formal, more reserved, more conscious of social hierarchy.

That internal diversity is worth holding onto. Ecuador is a small country by South American standards — around 18 million people — but it contains multitudes: Indigenous communities that make up roughly a quarter of the population and have real political and cultural weight, a significant Afro-Ecuadorian population along the coast and northern highlands, a mestizo majority, and a recent history of political turbulence that has shaped institutional trust in specific ways. The country has had ten presidents in twenty years, including several who didn't finish their terms.

What tends to be consistent across these differences is a set of working culture values that Ecuador shares with much of the region: relationships before transactions, warmth as a baseline register, a flexible relationship with time, and a strong sense that who you are as a person matters more than your job title. Understanding where someone is from within Ecuador is often the first and most useful piece of context you can have.

Ecuadorian working culture is relationship-driven, warm, and shaped by hierarchy and a flexible sense of time. Here is what you are likely to encounter.

The relationship comes first
Ecuadorian professional culture is relationship-driven in a way that shapes everything from how meetings open to how commitments are made and kept. The personal connection — who someone is, where they're from, whether there is real warmth between you — is the foundation on which professional trust is built. Colleagues who move quickly to task and skip the personal opening tend to find that things move more slowly and with less real effort. The investment in the person is not a preamble to the work — it is part of how the work gets done.
Warmth and formality coexist
The immediate warmth of Ecuadorian social interaction can mislead people into thinking the culture is uniformly informal. It isn't. Particularly in Quito and in more traditional or public-sector organisations, hierarchy and formal titles carry real weight. The warmth is interpersonal; the structure is professional. Address people as licenciado, ingeniero, doctor until they indicate otherwise — using professional titles is a form of respect that lands well and costs nothing.
Time is a flexible concept — context determines priority
Ecuadorian working culture has a more relaxed relationship with punctuality and scheduling than northern European or North American norms. Meetings may start late, plans may shift, and deadlines tend to be understood as approximate unless explicitly flagged otherwise. This reflects a priority system where the quality of what's happening and the people involved take precedence over the clock. When a deadline is firm, say so clearly and explain why. Context travels.
Seniority and hierarchy are taken seriously
Decisions in Ecuadorian organisations tend to sit with senior people, and the distance between levels is real. Junior colleagues don't typically challenge senior ones directly, and important decisions rarely get made without the right authority present. Understanding who actually holds decision-making power — and making sure they're in the conversation — matters more than building consensus from the bottom up.
Indirect communication about problems
Saying no directly, raising a concern plainly in a meeting, or delivering critical feedback frontally are all culturally uncomfortable — particularly across hierarchical lines or in group settings. Problems tend to surface softly, obliquely, or in private rather than in formal settings. A mild expression of concern is often carrying significantly more weight than the delivery suggests.
Practical tips
→
Open with the personal. Ask about family, about where they're from, about the region — before the agenda. It's the relationship being built.
→
Use titles until invited not to. Ingeniero, doctor, licenciado — use what fits. Moving to first names is theirs to offer.
→
Flag hard deadlines explicitly. Flexibility is the default. If something is actually immovable, say so with the reason.
→
Find out who decides. Time spent understanding the real decision structure is rarely wasted.
→
Follow up in writing. Warm verbal agreement and firm operational commitment are different things. A friendly follow-up message makes the commitment concrete.

The cultural norms and unspoken rules that shape Ecuadorian professional life — things that are obvious from the inside and invisible from the outside.

Regionalism runs deep
The coastal/highland divide — costeños vs serranos — is one of the most significant cultural fault lines in Ecuador, and Ecuadorians are very aware of it. Guayaquil and Quito have a long-running rivalry that is political, economic, and cultural simultaneously. Knowing where a colleague is from, and having some awareness of what that means, signals a level of attention that is noticed.
In practice: Ask where someone is from early — it's a natural and welcome question, and the answer tells you a great deal about register, style, and what to expect.
Indigenous identity and rights carry real political weight
Ecuador's Indigenous movement — led primarily by CONAIE — has played a significant role in Ecuadorian politics, including bringing down presidents. Indigenous culture, languages (particularly Kichwa), and worldview have been incorporated into the constitution, which recognises Ecuador as a plurinational state. This is a live political reality that Ecuadorian colleagues engage with, not background colour.
In practice: Show curiosity rather than assumptions. Ecuador's Indigenous communities are not a historical footnote — they are a present political and cultural force.
Criticism of Ecuador is an Ecuadorian privilege
Ecuadorians can be sharp critics of their own institutions, their politics, their infrastructure. A foreigner who joins in will find the temperature drop immediately. The critique is internal; the expected response from outside is curiosity, not agreement.
In practice: Ask questions rather than offering opinions. "What do you think needs to change?" is a very different conversation from "I agree, the system seems broken."
Hospitality is a real obligation
Being invited to a home or to share a meal is a social commitment taken seriously on both sides. Refusing food or drink repeatedly causes real discomfort. Showing interest in what is being offered — asking about a dish, accepting a second serving — signals that you are comfortable and that you respect what has been prepared.
In practice: Accept something, even symbolically. The hospitality is an act of care and deserves to be received as one.

Where Ecuadorian working culture creates friction with other styles — and what is actually going on.

Ecuadorian culturemeetsstructured, deadline-driven cultures
"They agreed in the meeting and then nothing moved"
The agreement in the meeting was real — but in a relationship-first culture, social agreement and operational commitment are different things, and the second depends heavily on the strength of the personal connection and the clarity of what was actually agreed. "That sounds good" and "I commit to delivering X by Thursday" are not equivalent statements, even from the same conversation.
What helps: Confirm clearly and warmly in writing after the conversation. Separate the relational warmth from the operational agreement — both need to be made explicit.
Ecuadorian culturemeetsdirect-feedback cultures
"I can't get a clear answer on whether there's a problem"
Bad news, concerns, and disagreements tend not to surface plainly in formal settings or across hierarchical lines. The positive register is culturally maintained even when the situation is complicated. What sounds like smooth sailing may be carrying significant unspoken reservations. The slight hesitation, the vague answer, the conversation that changes subject — these are doing more work than they appear to.
What helps: Create private, low-stakes moments for honest conversation. "Just between us, how is it actually going?" surfaces what the formal meeting often doesn't.
Ecuadorian culturemeetslinear-time cultures
"Everything runs late — I can't build any momentum"
The flexible relationship with time reflects a priority system where the person in front of you and the quality of the interaction take precedence over the schedule. Starting a meeting on time with the wrong energy is considered worse than starting it late with the right one. The lateness is a feature of the priority system, not a signal about your importance.
What helps: Build buffer into timelines, be explicit about which deadlines are hard, and frame punctuality as a practical need rather than a moral position — it lands considerably better.

Five things about Ecuador worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your Ecuadorian colleagues are coming from.

1
The Galápagos Islands shaped modern science
When Charles Darwin arrived in the Galápagos in 1835 aboard HMS Beagle, he encountered animals that had evolved in complete isolation — finches with beaks adapted to different food sources, giant tortoises specific to individual islands, marine iguanas unlike anything elsewhere on earth. The observations he made there contributed directly to the theory of natural selection, published in On the Origin of Species in 1859. The islands are still among the most biologically extraordinary places on earth — a UNESCO World Heritage Site and an active living laboratory. For Ecuadorians, the Galápagos are a source of enormous pride — one of the most significant pieces of natural heritage belonging to any country on earth.
Conversation opener: Ask an Ecuadorian colleague whether they've visited the Galápagos. Many haven't — the islands are expensive to reach even from the mainland — and that fact usually opens a conversation of its own.
2
Quito's historic centre was the first UNESCO World Heritage city
Quito sits at 2,850 metres above sea level in an Andean valley, surrounded by volcanoes, and its historic centre — designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1978, the first city to receive the designation — contains one of the most intact colonial urban landscapes in Latin America. Churches, convents, and civic buildings dating from the 16th and 17th centuries crowd the centre in a density that has survived earthquakes and modernisation. Walking through Quito's old city is an exercise in reading five centuries of history in stone, and Ecuadorians from Quito tend to know it and talk about it with specific pride.
Conversation opener: Ask a Quiteño colleague which part of the historic centre they think visitors always miss. The answers usually go well beyond the obvious.
3
Ecuador is the world's largest exporter of bananas — and has been for decades
Ecuador has held the top position in global banana exports for most of the past seventy years, accounting for roughly a quarter of all bananas traded internationally. The banana industry shaped the country's economic and political history in the 20th century — and the influence of US fruit companies in that history is part of the broader story of how Ecuador relates to foreign economic power. The banana is not a trivial fact — it is a thread that runs through land ownership, labour rights, environmental politics, and the country's relationship with global commodity markets.
Conversation opener: Ask an Ecuadorian colleague what they think most people abroad don't understand about the banana industry. It usually opens something more political than you'd expect.
4
Oswaldo Guayasamín painted the pain of Latin America
Oswaldo Guayasamín (1919–1999) is Ecuador's most celebrated artist and one of the most significant painters in Latin American history. Born in Quito to a mestizo father and Indigenous mother, he spent his career documenting suffering — the consequences of poverty, colonialism, and repression — in a style that blended Cubism with Indigenous and pre-Columbian influences. His monumental cycle La Edad de la Ira (The Age of Wrath) contains over 200 paintings addressing the violence of the 20th century. His home and studio in Quito, the Fundación Guayasamín, is one of the most visited cultural sites in Ecuador, and asking an Ecuadorian colleague about him usually produces something immediate and personal.
Conversation opener: Ask an Ecuadorian colleague which Guayasamín work they find most powerful. It's a question that tends to open something real.
5
Ecuador was the first country to give constitutional rights to nature
In 2008, Ecuador adopted a new constitution that included provisions granting legal rights to Pacha Mama — nature itself — including the right to exist, to be maintained, and to regenerate. This was a first in legal history anywhere in the world, drawing directly on Indigenous Kichwa concepts of the living earth. It has been used in court cases to protect specific ecosystems and rivers. Whether or not the provisions have been consistently enforced, the philosophical statement they represent — that nature has standing in law, not merely instrumental value — has influenced environmental legal movements on every continent.
Conversation opener: Ask an Ecuadorian colleague what they think the rights-of-nature provisions have actually changed in practice. The answers range from proud to sceptical, and both are interesting.
Central America

Costa Rica

The country that abolished its military in 1948 and spent the savings on schools and hospitals — and it shows.

Population 5.2M Capital: San José Language: Spanish Currency: Colón (CRC)
COSTA RICA

Costa Rica abolished its military in 1948 — a decision so unusual in the region that it became a cornerstone of national identity. The constitution that enshrined it redirected defence spending into education and healthcare, and over the following decades the country built some of the highest literacy rates, life expectancy figures, and environmental protections in Latin America. In a region defined for much of the 20th century by military governments and armed conflict, Costa Rica was quietly doing something different.

That choice shaped a culture in specific ways. The value placed on education is real and deep — ticos, as Costa Ricans call themselves, are proud of their schools, their universities, and their reputation for civic reasonableness. The relationship with the natural world is not just environmental policy but identity: Costa Rica contains around 6% of the world's biodiversity within 0.03% of its land surface, and the national culture reflects a relationship with that fact that is personal and not merely promotional.
