When she died in 1975, an estimated four million people attended her funeral in Cairo — one of the largest in modern history. She sang of love, longing, and dignity with a voice and a phrasing that her audiences found almost unbearably moving. Asking an Egyptian colleague — particularly one over forty — about Umm Kulthum tends to open something immediately personal and often very warm.
Conversation opener: Ask which song of hers they'd play to someone hearing her for the first time. There will be a considered answer.
3
The 1973 October War is remembered in Egypt very differently than in the West
What Western accounts typically frame as a war Egypt ultimately did not win is remembered in Egypt as Nast October — the October Victory. The Egyptian army crossed the Suez Canal, broke through the Bar-Lev Line (widely believed to be impenetrable), and demonstrated a military and logistical capability that forced the subsequent diplomatic process. For Egyptians of that generation, it restored something lost in 1967. Understanding this — that the same set of events carries genuinely different meanings — is a useful template for how Egyptian national history more broadly works.
Conversation opener: Ask an Egyptian colleague over 50 what the October War means to their family. The contrast with Western framings of the same events is usually striking.
4
Egyptian literature has a Nobel laureate — and he was nearly killed for it
Naguib Mahfouz won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1988 — the first Arabic-language writer to do so. His Cairo Trilogy, following three generations of a Cairo family from 1917 to 1952, is one of the great novels of the 20th century. In 1994, he was stabbed in the neck outside his home by an Islamist extremist, apparently in response to a novel he had written decades earlier. He survived, continued writing, and died in 2006 aged 94. The attack, and his response to it — continuing to write, refusing to leave, dying in Cairo — tells you something real about the cultural stakes that Egyptian literature has sometimes carried.
Watch list / read list: Cairo Trilogy (start with Palace Walk) · Midaq Alley · The Thief and the Dogs
5
Egypt's ancient heritage is still actively contested — and Egyptians have views
The Rosetta Stone is in the British Museum. The bust of Nefertiti is in Berlin. Egyptian archaeologists and cultural officials have been pressing for repatriation for decades, with limited success. For many Egyptians, this is not an abstract museum-politics debate — it's a continuing story about colonial-era extraction that is living, present, and occasionally sharp. If it comes up, knowing it's not just an academic question is useful. Asking about it with genuine curiosity tends to unlock a conversation that many Egyptian colleagues have strong and sophisticated views on.
Conversation opener: Ask an Egyptian colleague what they think should happen with the Rosetta Stone. It rarely stays theoretical for long.
Western Europe · EU member

France

A republic built on explicit principles — and a culture that has been thinking seriously about how life should be lived for a very long time.

Population 68M Capital: Paris Language: French EU founding member Currency: Euro (EUR)
FRANCE

Few countries have invested as consciously in the idea of themselves as France has. The Revolution of 1789 didn't just change a government — it produced a republic built on explicit principles, a relationship between the state and its citizens that was argued over, written down, and defended. The grandes écoles system, established in the same era, was designed to train people who could reason from first principles and run complex institutions. The Académie française has been guarding the French language since 1635. These are not coincidences — they reflect a culture that takes ideas, institutions, and intellectual rigour seriously as matters of national purpose.

The other shaping force is France's long relationship with civilisation as something to be cultivated rather than just consumed. The modern restaurant was invented in Paris. French cuisine, wine, philosophy, literature, and fashion have been exported for centuries — not as products but as a way of thinking about how life should be lived. This produces a working culture that places real value on quality, on doing things properly, and on the distinction between something well-made and something merely functional.

What that history gives you in a professional context is a culture that debates hard because it believes that is how good ideas survive, that respects intellectual preparation above most other signals, and that draws a considered line between work and the rest of life — not as an oversight, but as a position.

What to expect when working with French colleagues — the patterns, defaults, and unspoken rules that shape how things actually get done.

Debate is how thinking happens — not a sign something has gone wrong
In French professional culture, intellectual challenge is normal, expected, and not personal. A French colleague who pushes back hard on a proposal is engaging with it seriously. The underlying logic: if an idea can't survive rigorous challenge, it isn't ready. A room full of polite agreement is more suspicious than a room full of hard questions. Presenting something and expecting smooth approval tends to produce either disengagement or a barrage — and the barrage is the more useful outcome.
Preparation means reasoning, not just slides
Coming into a meeting without having thought through the logic, the edge cases, and the likely objections is noticed and remembered. What's valued is the ability to explain not just what is being proposed, but why — from first principles. "This is best practice elsewhere" opens a question. The answer requires understanding why the local approach is as it is. The intellectual work done before the meeting is the credibility brought into it.
Decisions are often made before the meeting
France has a clear hierarchy — title, seniority, and grande école background carry real weight — and decisions typically sit with senior people. But the path to a decision frequently runs through informal consultation and relationship-building that happens outside the formal meeting room. The meeting is often where a decision is presented, not where it is made. If you're planning to make your case from scratch in the room, you may already be too late.
Formality is the starting protocol, not the permanent register
French professional culture begins with vous rather than tu, surnames rather than first names, a degree of professional distance that signals mutual respect. The transition to informality is meaningful — precisely because it has to be earned rather than assumed. Follow their lead rather than accelerating it. Jumping to first names or casual register before it's been offered tends to read as presumptuous rather than warm.
Work and the rest of life are separate
The French relationship with working hours is a considered position, not a failure to keep up. Evenings and weekends are protected. Quality of life — good food, real holidays, time outside work — is not in tension with professional seriousness; it is part of what professional seriousness is for. Emailing on a Sunday and expecting a reply is a cultural mismatch, not a norm they've failed to adopt.
Practical tips
→
Do the intellectual work before the meeting. Anticipate the objections and know your reasoning, not just your conclusion. The preparation is the credibility.
→
Start with vous and surnames. Let them make the move to informality. Jumping ahead reads as presumptuous rather than friendly.
→
Engage with the challenge. When a French colleague pushes back, argue back with reasoning. Conceding too easily, or getting defensive, both land badly.
→
Take the lunch seriously. A proper sit-down lunch is part of the working day, not time away from it. It's where relationships are actually built.
→
Don't schedule important things for August. France largely closes down. Plan around it.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out — things that seem obvious from the inside and invisible from the outside. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

Intellectual standing matters as much as organisational rank
In many workplaces, the most senior person sets the terms. In French professional culture, the person with the sharpest argument often carries the most weight — regardless of title. Where you were educated, how rigorously you reason, whether you can hold your ground in a conceptual discussion: these carry real social currency, often more than the organisational chart suggests.
In practice: When meeting French colleagues for the first time, leading with your area of real expertise and depth lands better than leading with your title or years of experience.
Criticism targets the idea, not the person — but the line can blur for outsiders
French directness in intellectual debate is aimed at the argument, not the individual making it. The distinction is culturally clear and meant. A French colleague who dismantled a proposal in a meeting will often be warm and collegial immediately after — because in their frame, the argument and the relationship were always separate. For colleagues from cultures where professional and personal are less clearly partitioned, this takes adjustment in both directions.
In practice: If a meeting felt bruising, check whether the relationship itself is actually fine before drawing conclusions. Often it is — the debate was the debate, not a verdict on you.
The French state carries real cultural weight
France has a strong tradition of state-led thinking: the idea that certain things — culture, language, education, public services — are best organised nationally and protected from pure market logic. This shapes a particular scepticism toward efficiency arguments that treat everything as interchangeable. Understanding that this is a principled position, rooted in a long relationship between the French state and its citizens, makes it much easier to engage with than if it's read as simple resistance.
In practice: When proposing changes that touch on how things are structured or governed, expect principled questions about what is being lost — not just what is being gained. Engaging seriously with those questions goes further than pushing past them.
Food is not small talk — it's a different kind of conversation
A meal in France has its own arc, its own register, and its own social weight. Being a curious and appreciative guest at a French table is one of the most reliable ways to build a real relationship. Eating quickly, indifferently, or without interest in what's being shared is noticed — not as a transgression exactly, but as a signal about what someone values.
In practice: Ask about what you're eating and drinking. Show curiosity. The conversation that follows is usually warmer and more revealing than anything that happens in the meeting room.
The language question is not trivial
The French relationship with the French language is a connection between language, thought, and cultural identity. Making an effort — even briefly, even imperfectly — lands differently than defaulting immediately to English. The effort signals respect, even when the conversation quickly switches. Assuming English is the obvious default, without acknowledging the shift, can come across as entitled — particularly outside Paris.
In practice: Open in French if you have any at all — even a sentence. "Mon français n'est pas très bon, mais je vais essayer" is received warmly almost everywhere.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures isn't about bad intentions — it's about different defaults. Here's what's often going on underneath.

French culturemeetsconsensus-seeking cultures
"Everything gets argued to death — nothing ever gets decided"
The debate is the process, not an obstacle to it. Rigorous collective challenge is how an idea gets tested until it's robust enough to act on. French decision-making can be slow for this reason — but the decisions that emerge tend to be well-reasoned and durable. The arguing is quality control, not delay for its own sake.
What helps: Build time into the process for the intellectual work to happen properly. A decision forced before the debate has run its course tends to get relitigated anyway.
French culturemeetsdirect-commitment cultures
"They agreed in the meeting and then did something different"
A meeting in French professional culture is not always where commitment is made — it's often where a position is explored. What looked like agreement may have been engagement with an idea rather than commitment to a plan. "That seemed to land well" and "we have a commitment" are different things and worth treating as such.
What helps: Confirm explicitly in a follow-up. "Just to make sure we're aligned — we're going with X, delivered by Y?" makes the commitment concrete in a way the discussion in the room often didn't.
French culturemeetswarm, immediately informal cultures
"They seem cold and hard to get to know"
The formality is a starting protocol, not a permanent register. It signals that the relationship is being taken seriously — that familiarity hasn't been assumed before it's been established. The transition to real warmth is real and often quite complete once it happens. The runway is longer; the destination is the same.
What helps: Invest in the non-work moments — the lunch, the coffee, asking about the weekend with real interest. The relationship builds there more than in the meeting room.

Five things about France worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your French colleagues are coming from.

1
The grandes écoles system shapes who runs almost everything
France has two parallel higher education tracks: the standard universities and the grandes écoles — highly selective institutions (Sciences Po, HEC, École Polytechnique, ENA/INSP) that train the French elite. A disproportionate share of senior leaders in French business, government, and public life are grandes écoles graduates, and the networks formed there last entire careers. Where someone was educated is often a more reliable signal of their trajectory than their current job title — and understanding this helps explain some of the invisible hierarchy in French professional life.
Conversation opener: Ask a French colleague where they studied. The answer usually tells you a great deal — and they'll often be candid about what the system means.
2
The Revolution is still politically live
France has had five republics, two empires, and a monarchy since 1789 — and the fault lines that opened in the Revolution are still visible in contemporary politics. The concept of laïcité — strict state secularism — is a direct descendant of the Revolution's break with the Church, and shapes everything from debates about religious dress in schools to the boundary between public and private life. For a country that has been a republic for over two centuries, France has a remarkably unresolved relationship with what the republic should be — and those arguments are worth following if you want to understand the political instincts of French colleagues.
Conversation opener: Ask a French colleague what laïcité means to them personally. The answers are rarely simple and usually reveal something real.
3
France invented the restaurant
The modern restaurant — a public place where individual diners choose from a menu, sit at their own table, and eat at their own pace — was invented in Paris in the 18th century. Before that, eating out meant taverns with fixed menus and shared tables. France didn't just develop a cuisine; it developed the entire social architecture of how food is served and experienced. This is the backstory to why French food culture feels like it has rules — because it invented most of them.
Conversation opener: Ask a French colleague about a dish or region they think is underrated. Clear some time — the answer is usually specific, passionate, and worth hearing.
4
The French language is defended by law
The Académie française, founded in 1635, is the official authority on the French language. The Toubon Law of 1994 mandates the use of French in advertising, workplaces, and public communications. This reflects a real belief that language is a commons worth protecting — and that the gradual displacement of French by English in professional and cultural life is a loss worth resisting. Knowing this gives context to moments where the language question surfaces in ways that might otherwise seem disproportionate.
Conversation opener: Ask a French colleague what they think about English words entering everyday French. The debate about le weekend vs. la fin de semaine is surprisingly revealing.
5
France is the most visited country in the world — and has a complicated relationship with it
France receives more international tourists than any other country, and Paris more than almost any other city. This produces a particular ambivalence: pride in what draws people, occasional weariness with being perpetually observed and consumed, and a certain impatience with being reduced to a set of clichés. A French colleague who seems guarded about their country is often responding to decades of it being flattened into a postcard. The more interesting conversation is usually just one layer beneath the surface.
Conversation opener: Ask a French colleague where in France they'd send someone who wanted to see something other than Paris. The answer is usually immediate, specific, and quietly proud.
Central Europe · EU member

Hungary

An island of language in a sea of difference — and a culture whose depth tends to surprise people who weren't expecting it.

Population 10M Capital: Budapest Language: Hungarian EU member since 2004 Currency: Forint (HUF)
MAGYARORSZÁG

Hungary's position in Europe is unusual in ways that matter for how its working culture developed. The Hungarians — Magyars — arrived in the Carpathian Basin in 895 AD from the east, speaking a language entirely unrelated to their Slavic, Germanic, and Latin neighbours. That linguistic isolation produced a particular sense of cultural distinctiveness that is still very much alive. Hungary has always been surrounded by difference, and has always known it.

The 20th century compressed an extraordinary amount of disruption into a short space. After WWI, the Treaty of Trianon in 1920 stripped Hungary of two-thirds of its territory and roughly a third of its ethnic Hungarian population, leaving millions of Hungarians in neighbouring countries overnight. The wound has never fully closed. WWII brought occupation and catastrophic loss. Then came four decades of Soviet-imposed communism — including a 1956 uprising brutally crushed by Soviet tanks, whose memory remains raw — followed by a transition to a market economy in 1989 that required rebuilding institutions largely from scratch.

What that history leaves is a working culture that is more nuanced than its reputation suggests: formal in some contexts, warm in others, carrying a well-founded pride in Hungarian intellectual and cultural achievement, and a scepticism toward authority and official optimism that was earned the hard way.

Hungarian working culture is shaped by history as much as by any management theory. Here's what you'll likely encounter.

Formality is the opening register — not the only one
Hungarian professional culture tends to start formal, particularly in more established organisations and with people you don't know well. Titles and surnames are the safe opening. That said, the register can shift considerably once a relationship develops — and in international or tech environments it often relaxes quickly. Start formal and follow their lead. The move to first names is an offer, not an assumption.
Directness is part of how work is taken seriously
Hungarian professional communication tends to be frank about problems. If something isn't working, you're likely to hear about it directly, without extensive hedging. This is generally a sign of engagement rather than hostility — the work is being taken seriously enough to be honest about. Colleagues from higher-context or more diplomatically cushioned cultures sometimes experience this as blunt; it's worth recalibrating what the register actually means.
Hierarchy shapes the room, but expertise carries weight
Seniority is respected and decisions can sit with senior people, particularly in traditional organisations. At the same time, demonstrated competence matters independently of title. Coming in with real depth in your area — and being able to show it — lands well. The expectation tends to be that you know what you're talking about, and that showing you do earns respect that seniority alone doesn't automatically confer.
Commitment is taken literally
When a Hungarian colleague says they'll do something, they tend to mean it — and they'll notice if you don't follow through on what you said you'd do. There's a straightforwardness to professional commitments that makes them fairly reliable in both directions. Overpromising and underdelivering is a faster way to lose trust here than in cultures where stated commitments are understood to be more approximate.
Scepticism about optimistic projections is part of how careful people think
Expressing concern about risks, naming what could go wrong, or being sceptical about over-optimistic projections is a normal part of serious professional engagement. This isn't negativity for its own sake — it tends to be how careful people think: game out the failure modes before committing, so the real problems can be addressed. Don't read the risk inventory as a vote against.
Practical tips
→
Start with surnames and titles. In formal or first-meeting contexts, Úr (Mr) or Hölgy (Ms) with the surname is appropriate. Let them set the pace toward informality.
→
Note that Hungarian names are written surname first. Kovács János is János Kovács in Western name order. Getting this wrong in correspondence is a small but noticeable slip.
→
Follow through on what you commit to. Reliability is noticed and valued — and its absence is too.
→
Engage with the critique. If a Hungarian colleague raises concerns about a plan, respond substantively. Brushing past objections doesn't resolve them.
→
Don't schedule around August or major holidays. Summer holidays are taken seriously. Plan around them rather than through them.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out — things that seem obvious from the inside and invisible from the outside. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

Trianon is not distant history
The 1920 Treaty of Trianon — which reduced Hungary to a third of its pre-war territory — is not an event most Hungarians have moved past. It's a live part of national identity, still felt personally by many families who have relatives in Romania, Slovakia, Serbia, or Ukraine. Treating it as an obscure historical footnote, or being visibly unaware of it when it comes up, registers badly. You don't need to have an opinion on it — but knowing it exists and that it matters is the minimum.
In practice: If the topic comes up, listen more than you speak. Ask real questions. This is a conversation Hungarians are still having with themselves — curiosity and respect go further than conclusions.
A few words of Hungarian go a long way
Hungarian is one of the harder languages for European learners, and Hungarians know it. Any attempt to learn even a few words — köszönöm (thank you), egészségére (cheers/bless you) — is received warmly and disproportionately well. It signals that you've made an effort that most people don't bother with. The effort matters more than the result.
In practice: Learn to say thank you and cheers before you meet Hungarian colleagues in person. The reaction is almost always immediate and warm.
Hospitality, once offered, is serious
If a Hungarian colleague invites you to their home, or insists on paying, or offers food — these gestures carry real weight and aren.t casual. Refusing repeatedly or treating the offer lightly causes discomfort. Accept something, show your appreciation, and reciprocate when you can.
In practice: If you're a guest at a Hungarian table, eat with interest and ask about what you're eating. The food and the hospitality are connected — one is an expression of the other.
The intellectual tradition is a source of real pride
Hungary has produced a disproportionate number of Nobel laureates, mathematicians, physicists, and musicians relative to its size — and Hungarians are quietly proud of this in a way that surfaces in conversation. Knowing something about Hungarian intellectual or cultural achievement beyond the most obvious references is noticed and appreciated. It signals that you see Hungary as something more than a small country in Central Europe.
Easy entry points: The Martian scientists, Ferenc Puskás, Bartók and Kodály, the thermal bath culture, the architecture of Budapest's grand era.
Work and personal life tend to stay separate
There's generally a cleaner boundary between professional and personal in Hungarian working culture than in some other contexts. Colleagues may be warm and collegial without necessarily wanting to share a great deal of personal life at work. As the relationship develops, the boundaries shift — but the starting position is reserved rather than warm. As the relationship develops, the boundary tends to shift naturally.
In practice: Don't push for personal disclosure early. Show interest when things come up naturally, and let the relationship develop at its own pace.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures isn't about bad intentions — it's about different defaults. Here's what's often going on underneath.

Hungarian culturemeetsimmediately warm, open cultures
"They seem reserved and hard to read"
The professional register at the start of a relationship tends to be measured rather than effusive. Warmth tends to be earned rather than extended by default — and once earned, it's durable. Warmth tends to be earned rather than extended by default, and once it's there it tends to be durable. The starting temperature isn't the whole range.
What helps: Show up consistently, follow through on small things, ask real questions. The relationship builds through reliability and real interest rather than performed friendliness.
Hungarian culturemeetsoptimism-default cultures
"They're always pointing out what could go wrong"
Naming risks and expressing scepticism about over-optimistic plans is a normal part of how serious professional engagement looks. It tends to mean someone has thought carefully about the proposal and wants to make sure the real obstacles are on the table. Treating it as an attitude problem misses what's actually being offered.
What helps: Invite the risk list explicitly — "what are the main things we'd need to watch out for?" turns the instinct into a structural contribution rather than an undirected concern.
Hungarian culturemeetssay-it-in-the-room cultures
"They agreed and then didn't raise issues until it was too late"
Raising concerns publicly — particularly upward, or with people not well known — can feel unnecessarily confrontational. The real conversation sometimes happens after the meeting, or one-to-one, rather than in the formal setting. If a Hungarian colleague asks for a word after a meeting, take it seriously — that's often where the actual position is.
What helps: Create low-stakes, private moments for honest input — a coffee, a follow-up message. "Is there anything you'd want to flag that didn't come up?" opens the door without requiring a public stand.

Five things about Hungary worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your Hungarian colleagues are coming from.

1
The "Martians" — Hungary's extraordinary generation of scientists
In the mid-20th century, a cluster of Hungarian-born scientists reshaped modern physics, computing, and engineering to a degree that prompted a running joke among their colleagues: they must not actually be human. Leo Szilárd — who first conceived the nuclear chain reaction — had a ready answer to the Fermi paradox, the question of why we find no evidence of intelligent life beyond Earth: "They are already here among us — they just call themselves Hungarians." György Marx noted that the names of Szilárd, John von Neumann, and Theodore von Kármán cannot be found on the map of Budapest — but craters bearing their names can be found on the Moon, and a crater on Mars bears Von Kármán's name. The list is remarkable: von Neumann laid the foundations of modern computing; Szilárd conceived the chain reaction; von Kármán defined supersonic aerodynamics; Edward Teller shaped nuclear weapons physics; Eugene Wigner won the Nobel in physics; Dennis Gabor invented holography and won another. Most left before or during WWII. For a country of ten million, the concentration is hard to explain — which is rather the point of the joke.
Conversation opener: Ask a Hungarian colleague if they know the Martian theory. Most do, and the pride is usually immediate.
2
Budapest was, briefly, one of the great cities of Europe
In the late 19th and early 20th centuries, Budapest was a city of real grandeur — the second capital of the Austro-Hungarian Empire, with an architectural ambition to match Vienna. The Parliament building on the Danube is one of the largest in the world. The metro system, opened in 1896, was the first on the European continent. The cafés, the literary culture, the intellectual life of that era produced writers, thinkers, and artists whose work still circulates. Understanding that Budapest was once a true imperial capital gives context to the cultural self-confidence that can surprise people who arrive expecting a small post-communist city.
Conversation opener: Ask a Hungarian colleague what they think most visitors miss about Budapest. The answers usually go well beyond the thermal baths.
3
The 1956 uprising left a mark that lasts
In October 1956, Hungarians rose against Soviet rule in a revolution that briefly succeeded — before being crushed within two weeks by Soviet tanks. Around 2,500 Hungarians were killed, and some 200,000 fled the country. The defeat was total and traumatic. For many Hungarian families, 1956 is not history — it's something a grandparent lived through, and the names of those who died or left are still in the family. Understanding this shapes a great deal about the Hungarian relationship to authority, institutional trust, and the particular quality of Hungarian resilience.
Conversation opener: Ask a Hungarian colleague over 40 whether their family has a connection to 1956. Many do, and the answers are usually both personal and illuminating.
4
Hungarian cuisine is more varied than its reputation
The international image of Hungarian food begins and ends with goulash (gulyás) — which is actually a soup in Hungary, not the stew that was exported abroad. The real range is considerably wider: lángos (fried dough), halászlé (fisherman's soup), töltött káposzta (stuffed cabbage), kürtőskalács (chimney cake), the wine regions of Tokaj and Eger, and a pastry tradition that owes as much to Vienna as to anything else. If a Hungarian colleague offers to show you what Hungarian food actually is, say yes. It's rarely what people expect.
Conversation opener: Ask a Hungarian colleague what dish they think is most misunderstood outside Hungary. Strong opinions guaranteed.
5
The golden age of Hungarian football — and what it still means
In the early 1950s, the Hungarian national team was, without serious argument, the best in the world. The Aranycsapat — the Golden Team — went 32 games unbeaten between 1950 and 1956, became the first continental side to beat England at Wembley (6–3 in 1953, a result that shook English football to its foundations), and repeated the feat 7–1 in Budapest months later. Ferenc Puskás, their captain and talisman, is still considered one of the greatest players in the history of the game. They went into the 1954 World Cup final as heavy favourites — and lost to West Germany in one of the most debated results in football history. The 1956 uprising ended the team: many players, abroad for a club tour when the Soviet tanks rolled in, never came home. The Aranycsapat dissolved into the diaspora, and Hungarian football never returned to those heights. For Hungarians of a certain generation, the Golden Team is not just sporting nostalgia — it is bound up with everything that era meant and everything that was lost.
Conversation opener: Ask a Hungarian colleague what they know about the 6–3 at Wembley. Anyone with a passing interest in football will have something to say.
Northern Europe · EU member

Ireland

A country that went from one people left to one people move to — in a single generation.

Population 5.1M Capital: Dublin Languages: Irish, English EU member since 1973 Currency: Euro (EUR)
ÉIRE

Ireland's working culture is shaped by a history that is both unusually heavy and unusually recent. Eight centuries of English rule, a famine in the 1840s that killed a million people and drove another million to emigrate within five years, a war of independence, partition, and then decades of economic stagnation that kept Ireland among the poorest countries in Western Europe well into the 1980s — this is the backdrop against which the modern Irish workplace developed. The transformation since then has been extraordinary: from a country people left to one they move to, from an agricultural economy to a hub for global technology, pharmaceuticals, and finance.

That trajectory matters for understanding what you encounter at work. The confidence is real and relatively new. The informality runs deep — partly temperamental, partly a long-standing cultural resistance to the kind of hierarchy and deference that were associated with colonial authority. The relationship with Britain remains complicated in ways that surface occasionally and deserve attention rather than avoidance.

What Ireland shares with the UK — the language, some institutions, proximity — can mislead people into thinking the working cultures are essentially the same. They're not. The warmth comes faster, the humour is different in kind, the relationship with authority is more sceptical, and the importance of the social dimension of work is harder to overstate.

Irish working culture is shaped by history, a deep social instinct, and a particular relationship with authority. Here's what you'll likely encounter.

Warmth and informality are immediate — and real
Irish professional culture is flat and informal by default. First names from the start, an easy register, real interest in who you are as a person before getting to business — these aren't performance, they're the actual starting point. Skipping the personal opening and going straight to agenda can come across as cold, even in a time-pressured context. A few minutes of real conversation first is an investment, not a delay.
Humour is a working tool, not a distraction
The Irish use of humour in professional settings — self-deprecating, dry, occasionally elaborate — is a way of building connection, defusing tension, and testing whether someone is worth knowing. Someone who can take a joke and give one back is easier to trust and easier to work with, and that social currency carries into professional relationships. Treating every meeting with unbroken seriousness tends to create distance rather than credibility.
Directness is wrapped in warmth — but it's still there
Irish communication is direct about problems, but the directness usually comes cushioned in good humour or personal warmth rather than delivered cold. The substance is frank; the delivery is relational. What you won't always get is elaborate British-style hedging — coded language and indirect signals are less common here. If something isn't working, you're likely to hear about it, just not harshly.
Hierarchy is flat in register, even when it's real in structure
Irish workplaces — even formal or large ones — tend to operate with a notably flat social register. Senior people use first names, junior colleagues speak freely, and the gap between levels tends to be expressed through responsibility rather than formality. This can mask where the real decision-making sits. The informal register doesn't mean decisions are made collectively — it means the authority is real but quietly held.
Relationships are the infrastructure
Getting things done in an Irish workplace often runs through personal relationships more than formal process. Who you know, who trusts you, who will pick up the phone when you call — this is the actual network. Investing time in the relationship before you need something from it is not just good manners; it's how the place functions.
Practical tips
→
Don't rush the small talk. The opening conversation — weekend, sport, where you're from — is not filler. It's the foundation of the working relationship.
→
Engage with the humour. You don't have to be funny, but being able to laugh at yourself and appreciate wit goes a long way. Taking yourself very seriously is mildly suspect.
→
Be direct when it matters. If there's a problem, say so plainly and warmly. Elaborate hedging tends to obscure rather than protect.
→
Show up to the social moments. After-work drinks, the team lunch, informal gatherings — these are where relationships are built and where the culture actually lives. Opting out consistently signals that you're not really present.
→
Don't confuse informality with lack of rigour. The easy register doesn't mean standards are loose. Work is taken seriously; it just isn't performed seriously.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out — things that seem obvious from the inside and invisible from the outside. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

The Troubles are not ancient history — and the border question is live
The conflict in Northern Ireland — which claimed over 3,500 lives between 1968 and 1998 — ended with the Good Friday Agreement, but it left wounds that haven't fully healed and a political border whose status remains contested. For many Irish colleagues, particularly those from border counties or families with direct connections to the conflict, this is personal in ways that aren't always visible. Treating it as a resolved historical episode, or weighing in with uninformed opinions, is a fast way to miscalibrate. Curiosity and real listening are safer and more useful than conclusions.
In practice: If the topic comes up, listen more than you speak. This is a conversation Irish people are still having among themselves — your role as an outsider is to be curious and respectful, not to offer verdicts.
The relationship with Britain is layered
Most Irish people have British friends, British family, have worked or studied in Britain, and navigate the relationship with a pragmatism that doesn't always match the political temperature. But the history is still there — and it surfaces in specific moments: Brexit (which Ireland had no vote in but was profoundly affected by), discussions of partition, the occasional colleague who treats Ireland as an extension of Britain. None of this is usually hostile; most of it just requires awareness that the relationship has a weight that "we speak the same language" doesn't capture.
In practice: Don't assume Irish and British are interchangeable. Ask where someone is from specifically, and treat the answer as meaningful.
Modesty is socially enforced
Ireland has a strong cultural norm against visible self-promotion. Talking yourself up, claiming credit loudly, or seeming to think too highly of yourself invites a particular kind of social correction — usually delivered through humour, but pointed. The term notions — as in "she's got notions about herself" — captures the cultural suspicion of anyone who seems to have risen above their station. This creates a dynamic where real achievements are often undersold, and where the most capable people in the room may be the quietest about it.
In practice: Let the work speak. Frame successes as team efforts, acknowledge luck and timing. Visible ambition is fine; performed self-importance is not.
GAA is not just sport — it's community infrastructure
The Gaelic Athletic Association — which organises hurling, Gaelic football, camogie, and handball — is one of the largest amateur sporting organisations in the world, and in many parts of Ireland it is the social backbone of local community life. Your county, your club, your team — these are primary identity markers that carry real weight. Knowing which county a colleague is from and having even a passing awareness of the GAA signals a kind of attention that lands well, especially outside the major cities.
In practice: Ask where someone is from, and if they played GAA growing up. In many cases you'll unlock a conversation that tells you more about who they are than an hour of professional small talk would.
Catholicism shaped the culture more than the practice now reflects
Ireland has secularised rapidly — but the cultural imprint of a deeply Catholic society is still present in less visible ways: a certain attitude toward community and family obligation, the instinct toward hospitality, the weight given to social solidarity. You don't need to engage with the religious history to understand it's still part of the texture of Irish social life for many people, particularly outside Dublin and among older generations.
In practice: Religious and social conservatism varies enormously by generation and geography. Don't assume uniformity in either direction.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures isn't about bad intentions — it's about different defaults. Here's what's often going on underneath.

Irish culturemeetsstructured, process-driven cultures
"They're great to be around but hard to pin down on commitments"
The warmth and easy agreement in conversation are real — but they don't always translate directly into a formal commitment in the way a more process-oriented colleague might expect. In a relationship-first culture, saying yes in the moment is partly social and partly substantive, and the distinction isn't always made explicit. "That sounds good" and "I formally commit to delivering X by Y" can mean different things.
What helps: Confirm in writing after the conversation. "Just capturing what we agreed — you'll have X to me by Thursday?" lands warmly and makes the commitment concrete without creating confrontation.
Irish culturemeetsformal, agenda-driven cultures
"The meeting was full of jokes — I couldn't tell what was actually decided"
Humour in an Irish meeting is not a sign that the work isn't being taken seriously — it's the social lubrication that makes the work go better. Important things get decided and agreed in rooms where people are also laughing. The informality of the process doesn't reflect the seriousness of the outcome.
What helps: Follow up with a brief written summary of what was decided and who owns what. A written summary of what was decided and who owns what is good practice here, and received that way.
Irish culturemeetssay-it-in-the-room cultures
"They seemed enthusiastic and then the idea quietly died"
The social norm against direct disagreement — particularly with someone senior, or in a group setting — means that reservations sometimes don't surface in the meeting. The polite enthusiasm was real in the moment; the concerns were real too, but stayed private. If something was agreed and then went quiet, the issue is usually surfaceable one-to-one rather than in the formal setting where it didn't come out.
What helps: After key decisions, check in individually with the people whose buy-in matters most. "Any concerns about the plan now we're past the meeting?" gives permission to be honest in a way the group setting sometimes doesn't.

Five things about Ireland worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your Irish colleagues are coming from.

1
The Irish diaspora is one of the largest in the world relative to the home population
An estimated 70–80 million people worldwide claim Irish ancestry — roughly ten times the population of Ireland itself. This is the legacy of centuries of emigration, accelerated catastrophically by the Famine and continued through waves of economic necessity in the 20th century. The result is that Irishness has always had a global dimension: a culture continuously exported, maintained in communities from Boston to Buenos Aires to Sydney, and reimported by returned emigrants and their descendants. For many Irish people, the diaspora is not an abstraction — it's family. Understanding this helps explain both the ease with which Irish colleagues navigate international environments and the particular attachment to home that often coexists with it.
Conversation opener: Ask an Irish colleague if they have family abroad, or whether they've lived elsewhere themselves. The answer almost always opens something real.
2
The Irish language is alive — and the relationship with it is complicated
Irish (Gaeilge) is a first official language of Ireland, taught in schools, present on road signs, and spoken as a community language in the Gaeltacht regions along the western coast. Most Irish people have studied it for years and can produce varying amounts of it. The relationship is complicated: pride in the language as a marker of cultural identity coexists with ambivalence about the way it was taught, and fluent speakers are a minority. Knowing that the language exists and that it matters — even to people who don't actively speak it — is worth understanding.
Conversation opener: Ask an Irish colleague about their experience of learning Irish at school. Strong opinions, usually delivered with affection, are essentially guaranteed.
3
The literary tradition is disproportionate to the country's size
For a country of five million people, Ireland's contribution to world literature is extraordinary. Joyce, Beckett, Yeats, Wilde, Shaw, Heaney, Edna O'Brien, John McGahern — the list spans centuries and continues. Four Irish writers have won the Nobel Prize in Literature. This isn't a coincidence — it reflects a real cultural centrality of language, storytelling, and the spoken word that shows up in how Irish people communicate professionally as well as personally. The gift for narrative and the care with how something is said are real.
Conversation opener: Ask an Irish colleague which writer they'd recommend to someone who wanted to understand Ireland. The answer is usually personal and revealing.
4
St Patrick's Day is not what Ireland exported
St Patrick's Day on 17 March is a public holiday in Ireland — but the version of it that exists in Boston, Chicago, New York, and Sydney is largely an invention of the diaspora, shaped by the particular experience of Irish immigrants building identity in a new country. In Ireland itself, the day has traditionally been quieter, more religious, and considerably less green. The gap between the global export and the domestic reality is something many Irish people find amusing, occasionally baffling, and sometimes a little uncomfortable. Asking an Irish colleague what they actually do on St Patrick's Day — rather than what you've seen in American cities — usually produces a more honest and interesting answer than you'd expect.
Conversation opener: Ask an Irish colleague what 17 March looks like for them personally. The contrast with the global image usually surfaces immediately.
5
Hurling is one of the oldest and fastest field sports in the world
Hurling has been played in Ireland for at least 3,000 years — it appears in pre-Christian mythology — and is still played at a level of skill and pace that surprises people seeing it for the first time. It is entirely amateur: the players who represent their counties at Croke Park in front of 80,000 people on All-Ireland final day have day jobs. The GAA's model — elite sport, completely amateur, rooted in parish and county identity — is unlike almost anything else in world sport, and understanding it tells you something real about how community, loyalty, and place function in Irish life.
Conversation opener: Ask an Irish colleague if they follow hurling or Gaelic football, and which county they support. In many cases you'll learn more about where they're from than any other question would tell you.
Southern Europe · EU founding member

Italy

A young state carrying a very old civilisation — and a working culture shaped by relationships, craft, and a deep investment in doing things well.

Population 59M Capital: Rome Language: Italian EU founding member Currency: Euro (EUR)
ITALIA

Italy as a unified state is surprisingly young — the country as it exists today was only unified in 1861, and the process was contested, incomplete, and left deep fault lines between north and south that are still visible in economic data, political attitudes, and cultural identity. Before unification, what is now Italy was a collection of city-states, kingdoms, and papal territories that had been competing, trading, and going to war with each other for centuries. That history of fragmentation left something durable: a strong identification with the local — the city, the region, the neighbourhood — that sits alongside and often above any sense of national identity. A colleague from Milan and a colleague from Naples share a passport and a language; the working cultures they come from are considerably less alike than that suggests.

The other shaping force is the weight of civilisation itself. Italy was the centre of the Roman Empire, the heartland of the Renaissance, and the origin of banking, opera, and much of what Europe thinks of as high culture. This isn't background noise — it produces a culture with a very settled sense of aesthetic standards, a deep investment in doing things with quality and style, and a relationship with tradition that isn't nostalgia so much as considered preference. Things have been done a certain way for a long time because that way is good.

What you get in an Italian professional context is a culture that is relationship-first, quality-conscious, regionally varied, and considerably more structured beneath its warm surface than it first appears.

What to expect when working with Italian colleagues — the patterns, defaults, and unspoken rules that shape how things actually get done.

The relationship has to come before the business
In Italian professional culture, the personal relationship is not a preamble — it is the foundation on which everything else is built. Time spent on coffee, on conversation about family and life, on establishing who someone actually is before discussing what you want from them: this is not wasted time. Colleagues who skip the relationship and go straight to the transaction are experienced as cold and untrustworthy, and they tend to find that things don't move as well as they expected.
Quality and craft are taken seriously
There is a deep cultural investment in doing things well — not just functionally, but properly, with attention to detail and pride in the result. This shows up across sectors: in how presentations are designed, how proposals are written, how meetings are run. Cutting corners for the sake of speed is a real friction point. The extra time spent on getting something right is understood as the work, not a delay to it.
Regional identity matters — Italy is not one culture
The differences between working in Milan, Rome, Bologna, and Palermo are real and significant. The north tends to be faster-paced, more internationally oriented, and closer to northern European norms in some ways; the south tends to be more relationship-driven, warmer in register, and operating on a different relationship with time. Treating Italy as a single uniform culture will produce consistent miscalibrations. Always pay attention to where someone is from.
Hierarchy is present but wrapped in warmth
Italian organisations tend to have real hierarchies, and decisions often sit with senior people. But the daily register across levels is warm, personal, and informal in ways that can disguise how much the formal structure matters. The warmth is real; the hierarchy is also real. Both operate simultaneously, and understanding which one is relevant in a given moment takes time.
Bella figura — the importance of presentation
Bella figura — literally "beautiful figure," meaning making a good impression — is a cultural value that runs through professional life. How something looks, how it is presented, how you carry yourself: these matter as signals of competence, seriousness, and respect for the people you're dealing with. Showing up under-prepared, badly dressed, or visibly rushed signals something in Italy that it might not signal elsewhere. The form is part of the content.
Practical tips
→
Invest in the personal opening. Ask about family, the city, the food — with real interest. It's not small talk; it's the relationship being established.
→
Say yes to the meal. Lunch and dinner are where real trust gets built. Declining routinely signals that you don't value the relationship.
→
Don't rush. A meeting that runs long because the conversation was good is not a failure. Building in time for things to breathe tends to produce better results than forcing pace.
→
Pay attention to presentation. How a document looks, how a meeting is set up, how you dress for a first meeting — these are read as signals and worth attending to.
→
Don't schedule important things in August. Italy largely closes. Plan around it, not through it.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out — things that seem obvious from the inside and invisible from the outside. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

North and south carry different histories
The economic and cultural gap between northern and southern Italy — the Mezzogiorno question — is one of the defining and most contested issues in Italian public life. Attitudes toward it vary enormously and can run deep. Treating the distinction as trivial, or making casual assumptions about either side, tends to surface sensitivities that weren't visible a moment before. It's a topic worth approaching with curiosity rather than conclusions.
In practice: Ask where someone is from specifically and treat the answer as meaningful. The difference between being from Torino and being from Bari is not a detail.
Food is identity, not preference
Italian food culture is specific, regional, and deeply felt. Pasta is made in particular ways for particular reasons. Coffee is prepared correctly, not approximately. The rules around what goes with what, when certain dishes are eaten, how things are done at the regional level — these are not arbitrary customs. Showing real curiosity and appreciation for what you.re eating is one of the most reliable ways to build connection. Expressing indifference, or claiming that something done differently elsewhere is just as good, is not received well.
In practice: Ask what you're eating and where it's from. The conversation that follows is usually warmer and more revealing than anything that happens in the meeting room.
The family is the real institution
In Italian culture, family — immediate and extended — operates as a primary unit of loyalty, support, and decision-making in ways that go well beyond most northern European norms. Major decisions tend to involve family input. Family obligations carry real weight in professional contexts. Understanding that a colleague's family commitments are not personal life intruding on work — but a legitimate and respected part of how life is organised — avoids a lot of friction.
In practice: Ask about family with real interest. It opens the relationship. And treat family-related scheduling needs with the same respect you'd give any other serious commitment.
Bureaucracy is navigated, not followed
Italy has a complex bureaucratic reality, and the Italian response to it has historically been to develop the skills to work around it creatively. There is a well-established distinction between the formal rule and how things actually get done. This produces colleagues who are often remarkably resourceful in getting things moving, and occasionally creates friction with colleagues who expect formal process to be the actual process.
In practice: When something is stuck in process, ask your Italian colleague how it would normally get unstuck. The answer is usually practical and immediate.
Time operates differently — and varies by region
The Italian relationship with time is more flexible than northern European or American norms, particularly in southern Italy and in smaller companies. Meetings start later, deadlines are often understood as approximate, and the quality of what's happening takes precedence over its punctual completion. The hierarchy of what matters is simply different here. The counterpart is that when something truly matters to someone, they'll give it disproportionate energy and attention.
In practice: If a deadline is hard, say so explicitly and explain why. Context travels, and a deadline attached to a real reason lands very differently from an abstract target.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures isn't about bad intentions — it's about different defaults. Here's what's often going on underneath.

Italian culturemeetstask-focused, follow-through cultures
"They're warm and agreeable and then nothing happens"
The warmth and agreement in the conversation were real. What wasn't necessarily established was a formal commitment — in a relationship-first culture, social agreement and operational commitment are different things, and the distinction isn't always made explicit in the meeting. "We'd love to do this" and "I commit to delivering X by Friday" are not the same statement, even when they come from the same conversation.
What helps: Confirm clearly and warmly in writing after the meeting. "Great — just capturing: you'll have the proposal to me by the 15th?" makes the commitment concrete without creating confrontation.
Italian culturemeetsdirect, low-context cultures
"I can't get a straight answer — everything is maybe"
Vagueness in Italian professional communication sometimes reflects real flexibility, sometimes a reluctance to commit before the relationship is fully established, and sometimes a cultural preference for keeping options open until the right moment. "We'll see how it develops" can mean several different things depending on the relationship, the context, and what's actually driving the hesitation.
What helps: Ask specific, concrete questions in a warm register. "Just so I can plan — are we thinking before or after the summer?" usually produces a more specific answer than an open-ended prompt.
Italian culturemeetsfast-moving, low-hierarchy cultures
"Everything takes so long to decide"
Decisions in Italian organisations often require relationship-building, informal consultation, and sign-off from senior people — a process that happens partly in meetings and partly in conversations before and after them. The meeting is often where a decision is confirmed, not where it is made. Pushing for a fast decision before the relationship and the informal groundwork are in place tends to produce either delay or a yes that doesn't stick.
What helps: Invest in the informal conversations before the formal one. Understanding who needs to be comfortable before a decision can land, and speaking to those people early, removes most of the friction.

Five things about Italy worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your Italian colleagues are coming from.

1
The Roman Empire is still physically present — not as ruins, but as the city itself
Rome is unusual among world capitals in that the ancient city is not in a museum or a designated historic district — it is the city. The Pantheon, built in 125 AD, is still structurally intact and in daily use. The Forum is a ten-minute walk from government ministries. Aqueducts that supplied water to a million people two thousand years ago still stand in the suburbs. In other Italian cities the layers are different but the principle is the same: in Bologna, the medieval portico system still organises street life; in Naples, the Greek street grid from 500 BC is still the map of the city centre. For Italian colleagues, living alongside this kind of continuity shapes a particular relationship with time, craft, and the idea that things built properly last. It's worth understanding that the past here isn't something preserved — it's something inhabited.
Conversation opener: Ask an Italian colleague from Rome what it's like to live in a city that old. The answers are usually personal and specific.
2
The Renaissance was not a metaphor — it happened in specific buildings, in specific cities
Florence in the 15th century was one of the most intellectually and artistically generative places in human history. The Medici family's patronage funded Brunelleschi, Botticelli, Leonardo, Michelangelo, and Machiavelli — often simultaneously, often in competition with each other. For Italians, this heritage is not abstract national pride — it is local. A Florentine colleague's relationship to the Uffizi, or a Roman colleague's relationship to the Pantheon, is the relationship of someone whose family has lived near these things for generations. The beauty is personal, not touristic.
Conversation opener: Ask an Italian colleague which city they think produced the most important art. The answer is almost always local and always defended passionately.
3
The Italian food system is a protected cultural infrastructure
Italy has more UNESCO-protected food products and geographical indications than any other country — Parmigiano Reggiano can only be made in a specific area of Emilia-Romagna, Prosciutto di Parma only in Parma, Barolo only from Nebbiolo grapes in a defined zone of Piedmont. The rules codify centuries of accumulated knowledge about how specific things are made correctly. Understanding that Italian food culture is a form of living craft tradition, rather than a set of restaurant options, explains a great deal about why it's taken so seriously.
Conversation opener: Ask an Italian colleague what food product they think is most misunderstood or misrepresented outside Italy. Clear some time.
4
Football is not a hobby — it's where identity gets expressed
Italy has won the FIFA World Cup four times, but the real intensity of Italian football is at the club level, where the game is bound up with city identity, neighbourhood loyalty, and in some cases political history. AC Milan and Inter share a stadium and divide a city. Rome's derby between Roma and Lazio — the Derby della Capitale — carries social and political weight that goes well beyond sport. Juventus's dominance from Turin has for decades defined how the rest of Italy's clubs understand themselves in opposition. Knowing which team a colleague supports, and roughly what that means, is useful social knowledge — and asking about it, with real curiosity opens conversations that tell you more about where someone is from and who they are than almost any other question.
Conversation opener: Ask an Italian colleague which team they support and what the local derby means to them. Be prepared for a detailed and passionate answer.
5
Italian design changed what the modern world looks like
The Vespa, the Olivetti typewriter, the Fiat 500, the espresso machine, Armani, Versace, Ferrari — Italian design of the 20th century produced objects that became global cultural touchstones. The discipline of industrial design as a field was shaped substantially by Italian practitioners and schools. Centuries of craft tradition combined with post-war industrialisation and a culture that takes aesthetic quality seriously as a professional standard produced something distinctive. The same instinct that produces great product design also shapes how Italian professionals think about presentations, proposals, and the appearance of their work.
Conversation opener: Ask an Italian colleague which Italian design object they think has had the most influence on the world. The answer is rarely obvious and always worth hearing.
Southeast Asia · Constitutional monarchy

Malaysia

One of the most genuinely diverse countries in the world — not as an aspiration but as a daily operating reality. Malays, Chinese Malaysians, and Indian Malaysians live and work alongside each other in a country where three major religions, several languages, and distinct cultural traditions share the same office floor.

Population 33M Capital: Kuala Lumpur Languages: Bahasa Malaysia, English, Mandarin, Tamil Currency: Ringgit (MYR)
MALAYSIA

Malaysia achieved independence from Britain in 1957, and the decades since have been shaped by a deliberate nation-building project, a major inter-ethnic crisis in 1969 that restructured the political and economic settlement, and a rapid transformation that turned a commodity-exporting agricultural economy into a sophisticated middle-income one within a single generation. Kuala Lumpur is a cosmopolitan city, and Malaysian professionals working internationally often have more cross-cultural fluency than they are given credit for — they have been navigating difference at home their entire lives.

Two things matter most for working well with Malaysian colleagues. First, that the culture of harmony — avoiding open conflict, preserving face, keeping the social atmosphere smooth — is not a superficial politeness but a structural priority that shapes how disagreement, feedback, and difficult news are handled. Second, that beneath the shared Malaysian identity, your Malay, Chinese Malaysian, and Indian Malaysian colleagues may have meaningfully different cultural defaults. Treating them as interchangeable is a mistake.

Malaysian working culture is shaped by a deep commitment to harmony, a respect for hierarchy, and the practical reality of managing difference every day. Here is what you are likely to encounter.

Face governs more than most outsiders realise
The concept of face — maintaining dignity and avoiding public embarrassment for yourself or anyone else — runs through Malaysian professional life with real structural force. Direct criticism in front of others, openly contradicting a senior person, or putting a colleague in a position where they have no graceful way out are not just uncomfortable. They damage the relationship in ways that can be slow to repair. The most effective conversations about problems or performance happen one-to-one, privately, with care for the other person's dignity intact. The formal meeting is rarely the right setting for difficult truths.
Hierarchy is the default structure
Malaysian organisations tend to be clearly hierarchical, and deference to seniority is the expected default — regardless of whether the senior person is the most capable person in the room. Juniors often wait for direction before acting, avoid challenging their managers publicly, and may soften or qualify their views significantly before expressing them upward. The person whose opinion shapes the outcome is usually the most senior person present, even if the meeting is structured as an open discussion.
Communication is high-context and indirect
Malaysian professional communication — particularly around problems, disagreements, and bad news — tends to be indirect. A direct "no" is rare. More commonly, there is a hesitation, a redirection, an "I'll check and come back to you" that doesn't come back, or an agreement in the meeting that doesn't translate into action. This is a system calibrated to preserve harmony and avoid public loss of face for either party. Learning to read what is not being said is as important as listening to what is.
Relationships before transactions
Malaysian business culture rewards patience and relational investment. Before a commitment is made or a deal moves forward, both sides need to have established enough mutual trust and comfort to proceed. This is more pronounced in Malay and Indian Malaysian business contexts than in Chinese Malaysian ones, where the tempo tends to be faster — but the relational dimension is present across all three. Time spent on small talk, shared meals, and personal conversation is foundational, not incidental.
The meeting before the meeting
Significant decisions in Malaysian organisations often don't happen at the table — they happen in the individual conversations, the corridor check-ins, and the private sign-offs that take place before the formal meeting. Arriving at a key meeting expecting to make your case from a standing start is often too late. The preparation that matters most is the relational groundwork laid before you walk into the room.
Practical tips
→
Use titles and surnames until invited to do otherwise. Encik (Mr), Puan (Mrs/Ms), Dato', Dr — seniority and titles are taken seriously, and getting them right from the start costs nothing.
→
Never put a colleague in a position where they have to say no publicly. If you need a real answer to a difficult question, find a private, informal moment rather than the meeting room.
→
Build in time for the social layer. Lunch, coffee, small talk about family and the weekend — this is the actual work of relationship-building.
→
Be aware of religious and dietary differences. Halal requirements, Hindu dietary practices, and Buddhist observance all shape what people can eat and where. Check before you book the restaurant.
→
Don't mistake the smooth surface for agreement. The absence of objection in a meeting is not the same as commitment to action. Follow up in writing with clear owners and timelines.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

Racial and ethnic identity is always present
Malaysia's three main ethnic communities — Bumiputera (predominantly Malay), Chinese Malaysian, and Indian Malaysian — have distinct identities, political positions, and cultural norms that coexist in the same workplace. The New Economic Policy, introduced after the 1969 riots, gives Bumiputera Malaysians preferential treatment in certain educational and business contexts, and this shapes the social atmosphere in ways that are not always visible to outsiders. Commenting on racial dynamics — even sympathetically, even with good intentions — is one of the faster ways to create serious discomfort in a Malaysian professional setting. Observe, listen, and follow the lead of your colleagues.
In practice: Treat each colleague as an individual first. The ethnic and political dimensions are real, but they are for your Malaysian colleagues to navigate and name — not for visitors to pronounce on.
Islam shapes the Malay working day — and the calendar
For Malay colleagues, the five daily prayers structure the day, and Friday prayers restructure the Friday midday. Ramadan shifts the pace and rhythm of the month. The public holiday calendar reflects Chinese New Year, Deepavali, Hari Raya Aidilfitri, and Christmas — all potentially falling within the same working quarter. Planning ahead for this is basic professional competence in Malaysia. A meeting scheduled over Friday prayers or a lunch booked without checking dietary requirements are easily avoided mistakes that are noticed.
In practice: Keep a Malaysian public holiday calendar visible from day one. The religious calendar is not a scheduling inconvenience — it is the scheduling reality.
Receiving with two hands — and the right hand
Giving and receiving objects — a business card, a gift, a document, a plate of food — with two hands, or with the right hand supported by the left, is a sign of respect that runs across Malay, Chinese Malaysian, and Indian Malaysian culture. Using only the left hand to pass or receive is considered rude in Malay culture specifically, where the left hand carries associations of uncleanliness. These are small gestures that take no effort and are immediately noticed.
In practice: Make it a habit from your first interaction. It signals cultural awareness in a way that takes three seconds to learn and a career to benefit from.
Don't rush the food conversation — it is central
Food is a shared national obsession in Malaysia in a way that cuts across all ethnic and religious lines. Nasi lemak, char kway teow, roti canai, laksa, bak kut teh, banana leaf rice — the running argument about whose hawker stall, whose recipe, and whose version of a dish is definitive is one of the more unifying features of Malaysian life. Asking a Malaysian colleague where to find the best nasi lemak in the city is not small talk — it is an invitation to a real conversation that will tell you a great deal about who they are and where they come from.
In practice: Ask. Be prepared to receive a detailed, passionate, and strongly opinionated answer. Then ask a follow-up.
Kiasu: the spirit you need to know
Kiasu — a Hokkien Chinese term that has become part of the shared Malaysian vocabulary — means roughly "fear of losing" or "afraid to miss out." It describes the competitive, get-there-first mentality that drives everything from queuing for a popular dish to intense school exam preparation to negotiating hard on price. It is acknowledged, often self-deprecatingly, by the people it describes. Knowing the word and knowing what it refers to is useful — it explains a lot about the particular competitive intensity that can show up alongside the surface harmony of Malaysian professional culture.
In practice: If a Malaysian colleague uses the word about themselves, laugh along — it is usually affectionate self-awareness, not self-criticism.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures is not about bad intentions — it is about different defaults. Here is what is often going on underneath.

Malaysian culturemeetsdirect, task-first cultures
"They agreed in the meeting and then nothing happened"
The meeting agreement may have been entirely real in the social sense — nobody disagreed, the atmosphere was positive, everyone nodded — without constituting a firm commitment to the specific action discussed. In a high-context, harmony-oriented culture, the meeting produces a surface consensus that sometimes doesn't survive contact with the actual obstacles, the actual authority structure, or the private view that nobody expressed. What converts conversation into action is the follow-up: a written summary with clear owners, deadlines, and a check-in point. That step does the work the meeting often doesn't.
What helps: End every substantive meeting with a warm, explicit summary of who is doing what by when. Frame it as helpful clarity rather than a trust issue.
Malaysian culturemeetslow-context, direct cultures
"I can never get a straight answer — every meeting ends with 'we'll look into it'"
"We'll look into it" and "let me get back to you" are not always stalling — sometimes they genuinely are. But they are also frequently the most comfortable way to avoid committing to a position in public before the internal consensus has formed, before the senior person has been briefed, or before the real answer has been worked out privately. Pressing for a direct yes or no in a group setting often produces a yes that is not really one. The honest answer typically surfaces in a private, informal moment, one-to-one, with enough relationship behind it.
What helps: Create a private, low-stakes moment for the real conversation. "I want to make sure I'm not missing something — is there anything about this that concerns you?" is a more reliable route to the truth than any group sign-off.
Malaysian culturemeetscultures expecting open debate
"They never push back — I don't know if they actually have a view"
The absence of visible disagreement in a Malaysian meeting is not the same as the absence of disagreement. Challenging a colleague's position directly — especially if they are senior, and especially in front of others — carries real social cost. Strong personal views often exist behind the smooth surface; they just don't surface in the formal meeting, where the priority is maintaining harmony rather than establishing who is right. The candid view usually emerges in a smaller, more informal setting, once enough trust has accumulated.
What helps: Ask directly in a one-to-one. "Is there anything about this you'd change?" will often get a significantly different answer than the meeting produced.
Malaysian culturemeetscultures expecting explicit feedback
"They seem friendly but I can't tell where I actually stand"
Positive feedback in Malaysian professional culture is often warm and visible. Critical feedback tends to be carefully softened, framed obliquely, or simply withheld — because direct criticism risks damaging the relationship and the other person's face in ways that feel disproportionate to the practical issue. The silence around problems is not indifference — it is care for the relationship, expressed as withholding. To get an accurate picture of where things stand, ask specifically and privately.
What helps: "Is there anything you'd change about how I handled that?" opens the door that "what did you think?" usually leaves closed.

Five things about Malaysia worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your Malaysian colleagues are coming from.

1
Malaysia put a man in space — and had to issue special religious guidelines for it
In 2007, Sheikh Muszaphar Shukor became the first Malaysian in space, travelling to the International Space Station aboard a Russian Soyuz spacecraft. He also happened to be an orthopaedic surgeon and a part-time model. For Malaysians, the mission was a point of real national pride — a marker of how far the country had come from independence to space travel in fifty years. It also required the National Fatwa Council to issue special guidelines on how to pray towards Mecca and observe Ramadan in orbit. That combination — the ambition, the achievement, and the careful reconciliation with religious practice — captures something true about how Malaysia operates.
Conversation opener: Ask a Malaysian colleague what they remember about the space mission. The pride tends to be immediate.
2
The Petronas Twin Towers are not just architecture — they are a statement
When the Petronas Towers were completed in 1998, they were the tallest buildings in the world — and they were built by a country that had only been independent for forty years. Designed by César Pelli, the towers incorporate Islamic geometric motifs into the floor plan and were a deliberate signal to the world that Malaysia had arrived. For older Malaysian colleagues who remember the country before the skyline existed, they carry an emotional weight that pure tourism doesn't capture.
Conversation opener: Ask what KL was like before the towers went up. The before-and-after stories are usually vivid and sometimes staggering.
3
Malaysian food is one of the great unrecognised cuisines — and Malaysians will defend it fiercely
The convergence of Malay, Chinese, Indian, and indigenous culinary traditions in one place has produced a food culture of extraordinary range and depth. Nasi lemak is the national dish and simultaneously a breakfast, a late-night snack, and the subject of endless argument about whose is best.
