Penang is widely considered to have some of the finest street food in Asia, with a Chinese Malaysian cooking tradition refined over generations. Ask any Malaysian which city has better food — KL or Penang — and prepare for a considered, animated, and mildly combative answer. The strength of the opinion tells you something about how much the question matters.
Conversation opener: Ask for the definitive nasi lemak recommendation. You will not run out of conversation.
4
Bahasa Malaysia absorbed everything it touched — and it shows
The Malay language spread through maritime trade routes across Southeast Asia for centuries before European arrival, absorbing Sanskrit, Arabic, Portuguese, Dutch, and English words along the way. Which is why a Malaysian can talk about a kereta (car, from Portuguese "carreta"), hospital (unchanged), televisyen, and telefon in the same sentence without switching languages. The language is a map of the history of the region — each loan word pointing to a different wave of contact, trade, or colonisation. Knowing a few words of Bahasa is noticed and appreciated out of all proportion to the effort it takes.
Conversation opener: Ask a Malaysian colleague to teach you a Bahasa word — ideally one that doesn't translate cleanly into English. The explanation usually opens a wider conversation about culture.
5
The Malaysian diaspora carries a particular kind of dual fluency
Malaysian professionals abroad — particularly Chinese Malaysians and Indian Malaysians who grew up navigating multiple languages, religions, and cultural codes simultaneously — often arrive in international workplaces with a cross-cultural literacy that is rare. Someone who grew up code-switching between Malay, Mandarin, Tamil, and English in the same school day has been practising cultural translation since childhood. This fluency is often invisible because it doesn't announce itself. The colleague who reads the room quickly, adapts their register effortlessly, and mediates between different cultural expectations without apparent effort may well be drawing on a very specific kind of Malaysian education.
Conversation opener: Ask a Malaysian colleague how many languages they actually use in a typical week. The answer is usually surprising.
North America · LATAM cultural bridge

Mexico

The result of one of history's most dramatic cultural collisions — and a society of extraordinary richness, warmth, and complexity that rewards the effort to understand it properly.

Population 130M Capital: Mexico City Language: Spanish Currency: MXN
MÉXICO

Mexico is the result of one of the most dramatic collisions in history. The Spanish conquest of the early 16th century met a network of highly developed civilisations — Aztec, Maya, Zapotec, and dozens more — and what emerged over the following centuries was something new: a culture that is neither purely Indigenous nor purely European but a mestizo synthesis of both, layered over an African heritage brought by the slave trade and shaped by three centuries of colonial rule, a violent war of independence in 1810, revolution in 1910, and the long shadow of a powerful neighbour to the north.

That history produced a culture of extraordinary richness and complexity — in art, food, music, architecture, and literature. It also produced a particular relationship with authority, with institutions, and with the gap between official versions of things and lived reality, that shapes how Mexican professional life works in ways that aren't always visible on the surface.

Mexico City is one of the largest cities on earth and one of the great cultural capitals of the Americas. The country that produced Frida Kahlo, Octavio Paz, Carlos Fuentes, and some of the world's most celebrated cuisine is not a country short on confidence in its own culture — and working well with Mexican colleagues starts with understanding that the warmth, the relationship focus, and the particular rhythm of how things get done here all emerge from somewhere specific.

Mexican working culture is relationship-first, warm, and shaped by a long history with authority and institutions.

The relationship is the infrastructure
Mexican professional culture is relationship-first in a way that runs deeper than simple politeness. The personal connection — knowing who someone is, establishing trust, spending real time together — is the foundation on which professional commitments are built and honoured. Colleagues who skip the relationship and go straight to the transaction find that things move more slowly, with less goodwill, and with less effort on their behalf.
Hierarchy is real — and so is warmth across it
Mexican organisations tend to have clear hierarchical structures, and decisions usually sit with senior people. At the same time, the social register across levels is warm and personal. A senior person may be warmly invested in junior colleagues' lives while still being clearly in charge. Challenging a senior's position directly, especially in a group setting, sits outside the cultural comfort zone. The alternative is usually a quiet word before or after the meeting, a trusted intermediary, or framing that lets the substance land without a public disagreement. Understanding both the warmth and the structure — and navigating each in the right moment — is how things actually get done here.
Time operates differently — and context matters
Mexican professional culture has a more flexible relationship with time than northern European or American norms. Meetings may start late, deadlines may be understood as approximate. What shifts this is clarity and relationship. A deadline with a real reason behind it, delivered to someone you have confianza with, carries considerably more weight than a formal date that arrived without context. When something genuinely matters — particularly when the relationship behind it is solid — the effort that follows tends to be disproportionate. The flexibility runs in both directions.
Confianza — trust as the real operating system
The concept of confianza — trust, confidence in a person — is central to how Mexican professional relationships function. It isn't established quickly, and it isn't purely professional: it involves knowing someone, spending time together, sharing meals. It also has a practical dimension: things tend to get done through people you trust, not through processes you follow. The colleague who has confianza with the key decision-maker is often more useful than the org chart suggests. Once confianza is established, the loyalty and commitment it produces is real — and it tends to outlast the formal arrangement that first brought people together.
Indirect communication about problems and conflict
Mexican communication tends to be warm and positive-facing, particularly in group settings or with people not yet fully trusted. Saying no directly, raising a problem plainly, or delivering criticism frontally are all culturally uncomfortable. The fuller picture usually surfaces in private, in smaller groups, or after the relationship has deepened enough that honesty no longer feels like a social risk. Learning to create those private, low-stakes moments — a coffee, a walk between meetings — is how you access what the group setting doesn't show.
Practical tips
→
Invest in the personal opening. Ask about family, about the city, about food — and mean it. It's the relationship being built, not delay.
→
Say yes to the meal. Lunch is where confianza gets built. Turning it down routinely signals that you don't value the relationship.
→
Be explicit about hard deadlines. Flexibility is the default. If a deadline is firm, say so clearly and explain why.
→
Don't schedule important things around major holidays. Semana Santa, Christmas, and Día de los Muertos are serious. Plan around them.
→
Follow up in writing. Verbal warmth and formal commitment are different things. A friendly confirming message makes things concrete without creating confrontation.

The invisible cultural logic that shapes how Mexican professional relationships actually work.

Family is the primary institution
In Mexican culture, the family — immediate and extended — is the primary unit of loyalty, support, and obligation. Major decisions involve family input, and family commitments take precedence in ways that are real and not up for negotiation. This has practical implications at work: unexpected family situations will shift priorities without apology or much explanation, because no further explanation is needed within this value system. Showing interest in family — asking about children, parents, where someone is from — is how you signal that you see the whole person, not just the professional. That signal matters.
In practice: Ask about family early, and remember what you're told. It's infrastructure for the relationship.
Respeto — and what it means in practice
Respect — respeto — is a central value, expressed through form of address, tone, and the care taken not to cause embarrassment. Titles matter: licenciado, doctor, ingeniero are not formality for its own sake — they are acknowledgements of achievement that carry real meaning. Treating someone in a way that causes them to lose face — particularly in front of others — is a serious social damage that is hard to repair. Criticism and difficult conversations belong in private, delivered with warmth and genuine regard for the person.
In practice: Never correct or challenge someone directly in front of others. The same message, delivered privately, lands entirely differently.
Personalismo — the person matters more than the channel
Getting something done through the right person — someone you know, someone who trusts you — consistently outperforms going through official channels. The org chart describes what should happen; the network of personal relationships describes what does. Both matter, but the second runs faster and more reliably once it's in place. Who you know is operational infrastructure, not social decoration. Investing in relationships before you need them is what makes the system work — and trying to build them at the moment you need a favour is already too late.
In practice: Before a formal process, ask: "is there someone I should speak to first?" The answer almost always matters.
Food is a serious expression of culture and care
Mexican cuisine was inscribed by UNESCO as Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity in 2010 — the first national cuisine to receive this recognition. Food in Mexico is a carrier of regional identity, family history, and cultural pride that goes well beyond what the dish looks like on a plate. Showing real curiosity about food — asking what you're eating, where it's from, what goes into it — is one of the most reliable ways to build a real relationship with a Mexican colleague. Asking where their family is from usually comes with a specific regional food tradition attached.
In practice: Ask a Mexican colleague where their family is from — the answer usually comes with a specific food tradition attached.

Where Mexican working culture creates friction with other styles — and what's actually going on.

Mexican culturemeetsdeadline-driven, task-first cultures
"They're warm and agreeable and then the deadline passes"
The warmth and the agreement in the conversation were real. What wasn't necessarily established was a firm commitment. In a relationship-first culture, the personal connection and the operational commitment are different things — and only the second one binds. The clearest way to close that gap is a warm, explicit written follow-up: "Great — just to confirm, you'll have the proposal to me by the 15th?" makes the commitment concrete without creating friction. "We'd love to make this happen" and "I commit to delivering X by Friday" are different statements, even when they come from the same conversation.
What helps: Confirm clearly and warmly in writing after the meeting. "Great — just capturing: you'll have the proposal to me by the 15th?" makes the commitment concrete without creating confrontation.
Mexican culturemeetsdirect-feedback cultures
"I can't tell what's actually going on — everything sounds fine"
Problems and concerns tend not to surface in group meetings or in early-stage relationships. The positive register is culturally maintained in ways that mean the real picture only becomes visible later, or in private. The more established the relationship and the more private the setting, the more the real picture tends to emerge. A trusted colleague who says something is "a bit complicated" is usually signalling considerably more than the phrase suggests. Creating private, low-stakes moments for honest check-ins — one-to-one, off the record — surfaces what the formal meeting reliably doesn't.
What helps: Create private, low-stakes moments for honest conversation. "Just between us, how is it actually going?" surfaces what the formal meeting often doesn't.
Mexican culturemeetslow-context, task-first cultures
"They seem to need a lot of relationship time before anything gets done"
The relationship time is the foundation on which reliable professional commitment is built. Skipping it doesn't accelerate the project — it makes the project less likely to go well, because the personal connection that motivates real discretionary effort hasn't been established. Build relationship investment into the timeline from the start. A shared meal before the first formal meeting, real conversation before getting to business — these are the preparation, not the preamble. The investment in confianza pays direct operational dividends in the form of loyalty, flexibility, and effort when it matters most.
What helps: Build relationship time into the project timeline. A meal before the first formal meeting is infrastructure, not delay.

Five things about Mexico worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your Mexican colleagues are coming from.

1
Mexican cuisine is one of the most complex culinary traditions in the world
The UNESCO recognition in 2010 acknowledged a tradition that combines Indigenous techniques developed over three thousand years with Spanish, African, and regional influences. Mole negro can contain over thirty ingredients and take days to prepare. The range across regions — Oaxacan, Yucatecan, Veracruz, northern, central — is as wide as the difference between French and Italian cuisine. Corn, chile, and chocolate are the foundation of a civilisation, not just ingredients.
Conversation opener: Ask a Mexican colleague which regional cuisine they think is most underappreciated.
2
The Mexican muralist movement changed how art was understood politically
Diego Rivera, José Clemente Orozco, and David Alfaro Siqueiros produced in the 1920s and 30s a body of monumental public art that placed history, class struggle, and Indigenous identity on the walls of government buildings, schools, and public spaces. The muralists argued, through their work, that art belonged to everyone — that the walls of public buildings were the appropriate gallery for a people who had been denied access to private ones.
Conversation opener: Ask a Mexican colleague from Mexico City which Rivera mural visitors always miss.
3
Mexico City is built on a lake — and it's sinking
Tenochtitlan, the Aztec capital, was built on an island in Lake Texcoco. The Spanish drained the lake over several centuries. The result is that Mexico City — home to over 20 million people — sits on soft, waterlogged sediment slowly compressing under the city's weight. Parts of the city have sunk by as much as ten metres in the past century. The cathedral in the historic centre is visibly tilting.
Conversation opener: Ask a Mexico City colleague what they think the city looked like before the Spanish arrived.
4
The 1968 Tlatelolco massacre is a wound that hasn't fully healed
Ten days before the 1968 Mexico City Olympics, the government ordered the military suppression of a student protest in the Plaza de las Tres Culturas. The number of people killed remains disputed. The event was suppressed in Mexican media for years and the full truth has never been officially acknowledged. For Mexican colleagues of a certain generation, Tlatelolco is family history — a defining moment in the relationship between Mexican citizens and the state.
Conversation opener: Ask a Mexican colleague over 50 what the 1968 generation meant for Mexican political life.
5
Lucha libre is theatre and identity as much as sport
Lucha libre has been part of Mexican popular culture since the 1930s. The masked wrestlers are characters with histories and identities that their masks embody. El Santo, who wrestled for decades and starred in dozens of films, became one of the most recognisable cultural figures in Mexican history. The mask is an alter ego — a luchador unmasked against their will has lost something significant.
Conversation opener: Ask a Mexican colleague — particularly one from Mexico City — what El Santo meant to Mexican culture.
West Africa · Africa's largest economy

Nigeria

Africa's most populous country and its largest economy — a place of extraordinary diversity, ambition, and interpersonal energy. More than 250 ethnic groups, 500 languages, and a working culture where relationships, seniority, and resourcefulness carry more weight than any org chart.

Population 220M+ Capital: Abuja Languages: English + Yoruba, Igbo, Hausa & 500 others Currency: Naira (NGN)
NIGERIA

Nigeria sits at the intersection of scale and complexity in ways that make it hard to summarise. With over 220 million people, it is Africa's most populous country and its largest economy. But raw size doesn't capture what makes it distinct. Nigeria contains more than 250 ethnic groups and around 500 languages. The Yoruba, Igbo, and Hausa-Fulani are the three largest — but the Efik, Ijaw, Tiv, Kanuri, and dozens of others have their own histories, social codes, and cultural logics. A colleague from Lagos and a colleague from Kano may share citizenship, but they bring different cultural frameworks to the same room — and both know it.

The country was assembled largely by British colonial interests rather than by internal logic — the 1914 amalgamation joined the predominantly Muslim, hierarchical north with the more ethnically diverse, Christian and animist south, without particularly consulting either. The Biafran War of 1967–70, decades of military rule, the return to democratic governance in 1999, and a series of ongoing regional tensions have all shaped the working culture in ways worth understanding.

What you find, despite or perhaps because of all this, is a culture of extraordinary resilience, ambition, and interpersonal energy. Nigerians generally bring warmth and full presence to relationships. Business is personal. Rank matters. Celebration matters. And a reputation for resourcefulness — for finding solutions where the conditions seem to rule them out — runs through professional culture in ways that are observable and worth knowing.

Nigerian working culture is shaped by relationships, seniority, and a resourcefulness built in genuinely demanding environments. Here's what you'll likely encounter.

People first, agenda second
Nigerian working culture is strongly relationship-oriented. A meeting that begins precisely on the scheduled topic, without preamble or personal exchange, can feel abrupt and cold. Time spent at the start of a conversation — asking about family, acknowledging the last time you spoke, showing interest in how someone is doing — is not padding. It's the foundation that makes the work conversation possible. Colleagues who skip it often find the work conversation less productive than they'd hoped.
Seniority is visible and respected
Hierarchy is a real force in Nigerian professional culture. Senior figures expect to be addressed accordingly, and in many settings their perspective shapes what gets said in the meeting. It is uncommon for a junior colleague to openly contradict a senior in a group setting — not because the junior has no view, but because the norms around expressing it are different. The gap between what a Nigerian team member says in a group meeting and what they'll tell you one-to-one is often significant.
Directness inside warmth
Nigerian communication tends to be warm and expressive, but this doesn't mean it's indirect. Nigerians will often tell you clearly what they think — particularly one-to-one, and particularly when there's enough trust. The warmth and the directness coexist. What changes is the register: a formal setting with seniors present tends to produce more careful communication; a conversation between equals with some relationship behind it tends to be frank and fast.
The relationship is the reliability mechanism
In contexts where formal systems — contracts, processes, institutional guarantees — may not be fully trusted, personal relationships carry more of the reliability function. This is less a cultural quirk than a rational adaptation. When you need something to happen, knowing and trusting the person who can make it happen matters more than the procedure on paper. Nigerian colleagues working internationally often build trust through demonstrated follow-through rather than formal commitments — and they tend to notice who does the same.
Resourcefulness under constraint
Nigerian professionals have often built careers in environments where resources, infrastructure, and institutional support were unreliable. This produces a particular kind of adaptability — a default orientation toward making things work regardless of the conditions. In a team context, this shows up as resilience, creativity under pressure, and a low tolerance for over-planning that doesn't lead anywhere.
Practical tips
→
Don't rush to the agenda. Open with personal exchange — it's not a delay, it's how the working conversation gets set up.
→
Address seniority correctly. When in doubt about titles or forms of address, ask. Getting it right early matters.
→
One-to-one is where honesty lives. If you want real feedback, create the conditions for a private conversation. Group settings produce group performances.
→
Follow through, consistently. Nothing builds credibility faster. Nothing damages it more reliably than promising and not delivering.
→
Celebrations are not optional. Promotions, milestones, team wins — acknowledging them is a professional expectation, not a social extra.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out — things that seem obvious from the inside and invisible from the outside. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

Ubuntu lives here, in a Nigerian form
The underlying value runs across much of Nigerian culture: individual wellbeing and collective belonging are bound together, not in tension. This shows up at work in small but real ways. Sharing food, acknowledging people who contributed behind the scenes, checking in on a colleague who seems off — these aren't extras, they're the texture of how collegial relationships work.
In practice: Notice who's struggling and say something. Credit people who helped, even when it's not required. The social fabric is maintained by small, consistent acts.
Don't refuse hospitality
If a Nigerian colleague offers food, drinks, or a gift — in their home, their office, or at a shared meal — declining can land as a rejection. Accepting, even symbolically, signals that you're comfortable in the relationship and that you respect what's being offered. The effort that has gone into hospitality is not casual, and treating it casually will be noticed.
In practice: Accept something. Even a small gesture of receiving signals that you understand the social contract being extended.
Religion is present, not private
Both Christianity and Islam are lived publicly and explicitly in Nigerian professional culture. Prayers before meetings are common in many workplaces. Religious greetings are normal. At the interpersonal level, colleagues across religious lines often work closely and warmly together. Showing respect for both traditions, and not treating religious expression as something that should be kept out of the office, goes a long way.
In practice: Acknowledge religious occasions — Eid, Christmas, Easter — when they arise. The gesture of noticing is usually enough.
Names carry weight — learn them properly
Yoruba, Igbo, and Hausa naming traditions are all rich and meaningful. A name often encodes family history, circumstance of birth, hopes, or religious devotion. Making the effort to learn how to pronounce someone's name correctly — and asking if you're unsure — is a small act that lands with real weight. Defaulting to a nickname or shortened version without being invited to is a small presumption that doesn't serve you.
In practice: Ask how to say it correctly and ask what it means. The second question usually opens something warm and personal.
Time has two modes
Nigerian time and clock time are different things, and both are real. Formal professional settings tend to run on something closer to clock time. Social and informal gatherings tend to run on what Nigerians sometimes call "African time," where a start time is a general orientation rather than a precise instruction. Reading which mode you're in is a skill. When in doubt, arrive on time — it gives you flexibility rather than taking it away.
In practice: In professional settings with external clients or seniors, assume clock time. In internal or social settings, read the room.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures isn't about bad intentions — it's about different defaults. Here's what's often going on underneath.

Nigerian culturemeetstime-rigid cultures
"They're always late — it feels disrespectful"
Showing up later than the stated time in Nigerian social and informal contexts follows a different set of norms than punctuality-focused cultures expect. In many contexts — particularly social ones — arriving exactly on time can read as slightly odd, and the actual gathering coalesces later. This is a different cultural logic about when things actually start — not a statement about the value of your time. The friction comes when these different logics aren't named, and each side interprets the other through its own frame.
What helps: Be explicit about your own expectations in mixed-culture professional settings. "We're starting at 2pm and need to finish by 3" removes ambiguity without requiring anyone to change their cultural defaults.
Nigerian culturemeetsflat-hierarchy cultures
"They won't challenge the senior — we need more speaking up"
What reads as passivity in a flat-hierarchy workplace is often a deeply internalised norm about the social cost of public disagreement with a senior figure. Push for open debate in a group setting and you may get silence; create a one-to-one environment and ask directly, and you often get frank, useful assessment. The opinions are there — the setting determines whether they surface.
What helps: Ask for input explicitly and privately before decisions are finalised. The quality of the information improves dramatically when the social risk is reduced.
Nigerian culturemeetslow-context communication cultures
"I can never tell what's actually been agreed"
In relationship-first cultures, agreements are often relational as much as contractual. What was understood between two people in a conversation carries real weight — sometimes more than what's written down. From a low-context perspective, this can look like vagueness; from a high-context perspective, demanding everything in writing can look like mistrust. Both readings are understandable, and neither helps.
What helps: Frame written follow-up as your way of working, not as a signal of distrust. "I always send a quick note after conversations so I have it in one place" lands very differently from "I need this in writing."
Nigerian culturemeetshighly individualist cultures
"They spend so much time on relationships — we need to focus on the work"
The relationship investment Nigerian colleagues bring to professional life is the mechanism by which work actually gets coordinated, problems get surfaced early, and things get done when formal systems fall short. Stripping it out doesn't streamline the work — it removes the connective tissue. Colleagues who don't invest in relationships often find that the informal information flow everyone else benefits from doesn't reach them.
What helps: Treat relationship-building as a professional activity. Show up to the informal moments. Ask about people as people. It pays returns that don't show up on the project plan.
Nigerian diasporameetscredential-discounting cultures
"Their background is impressive but they're not performing at that level"
Nigerian professionals working internationally often carry qualifications and experience that are not fully recognised in their new context — because the institution isn't known, because the credential system differs, or because hiring decisions defaulted to familiar names. The gap between what someone has actually done and what the hiring context credited them for can be significant.
What helps: Ask about their full background directly and with real interest. The story of how they got here and what they've built is usually considerably more than the CV suggests.

Five things about Nigeria worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your Nigerian colleagues are coming from.

1
Nollywood is the second largest film industry in the world by output — and most people outside Africa have no idea
Nigeria's film industry produces around 2,500 films a year, placing it ahead of Hollywood by volume and behind only India's Bollywood. What began in the early 1990s with low-budget video productions sold directly from market stalls has grown into a global cultural force. Nollywood films are watched across the African continent and in diaspora communities worldwide, and a new wave of higher-budget productions — led by platforms including Netflix Africa — is reaching international audiences for the first time. Asking a Nigerian colleague about their favourite Nollywood film is a reliable conversation opener, and the recommendations are usually worth following.
Conversation opener: Ask which film or director they'd recommend to someone who has never watched Nollywood. You'll get a considered answer and usually a strong opinion.
2
Afrobeats is reshaping global popular music — and it started in Lagos
The international dominance of Afrobeats in the 2020s — Burna Boy winning a Grammy, Wizkid collaborating with Beyoncé, Davido selling out arenas across Europe and North America — did not come from nowhere. The sound draws on Fela Kuti's Afrobeat (distinct from Afrobeats, and an important distinction to Nigerians), highlife, fuji, and a generation of Lagos producers who built something new from it. Fela himself — musician, activist, and one of the most singular figures in 20th century African culture — is a figure your Nigerian colleagues have strong opinions about. Asking about him usually opens something immediate and interesting.
Conversation opener: Ask a Nigerian colleague about Fela — what he meant, what they think of his legacy. The distinction between Afrobeat and Afrobeats is worth knowing before you ask.
3
The Yoruba, Igbo, and Hausa-Fulani are not interchangeable — and Nigerians know it
One of the most common outsider mistakes is treating Nigeria as culturally monolithic. Yoruba culture, concentrated in the southwest and centred on Lagos and Ibadan, has a long urban tradition and a sophisticated trading history. Igbo culture, from the southeast, emphasises individual achievement, entrepreneurship, and a relatively egalitarian social structure. Hausa-Fulani culture, dominant in the north, is shaped by Islam, emirate hierarchy, and a history of large-scale political organisation. These are generalisations — but knowing they exist, and that Nigerians navigate between them constantly, is more useful than treating them as one.
Conversation opener: Ask a Nigerian colleague where they're from and what that region is known for. The answer usually tells you something real about how they see themselves.
4
Nigeria has a Nobel laureate in literature — and he said things that still matter
Wole Soyinka, born in Abeokuta in 1934, won the Nobel Prize for Literature in 1986 — the first African writer to receive it. His work draws on Yoruba mythology, colonial history, and political critique. He was imprisoned without trial for two years during the Biafran War for attempting to negotiate a ceasefire. He has spent much of his life in and out of conflict with Nigerian governments, and has continued writing, speaking, and provoking into his late eighties. Knowing his name — and something about what he stood for — is a signal of genuine attention that Nigerian colleagues, particularly from the southwest, tend to notice.
Read list: Death and the King's Horseman · The Man Died (his prison memoir) · Aké: The Years of Childhood
5
Nigerian football has produced some of the finest players of their generation — and the 1996 Olympics gold is a memory that still runs warm
Jay-Jay Okocha, Nwankwo Kanu, Rashidi Yekini, John Obi Mikel, Vincent Enyeama — and now Victor Osimhen, one of the most expensive African players ever transferred in European football. The Super Eagles won the Africa Cup of Nations in 1994, and the under-23 team won Olympic gold in Atlanta in 1996 — one of the proudest sporting moments in Nigerian memory. The 2018 World Cup kit, designed by Nike, generated queues outside stores in Lagos and sold out globally within hours of release — a reminder that Nigerian football culture runs well beyond results. Ask a Nigerian colleague about the 1996 Olympics team, or about their favourite Super Eagles player growing up. The enthusiasm is usually immediate.
Conversation opener: Ask which player or moment in Super Eagles history they'd want a non-Nigerian to know about. Jay-Jay Okocha tends to come up quickly, and usually with some joy.
Middle East · Arabian Peninsula

Oman

Quieter, more measured, and more anchored in its own long history than its neighbours. A maritime empire, a unique religious tradition, and a diplomatic neutrality that has made it indispensable in some of the most sensitive negotiations of recent decades. Oman moves at its own pace, and it does so on purpose.

Population 4.5M Capital: Muscat Language: Arabic Currency: Omani Rial (OMR)
OMAN

Oman is not like its neighbours, and Omanis know it. While the UAE built itself at spectacular speed into a global commercial hub, while Saudi Arabia carries the weight of being custodian of Islam's holiest sites, and while Qatar punches above its weight through investment and soft power, Oman has taken a different path — quieter, more measured, more anchored in its own long history. That difference is not accidental. It is a deliberate expression of Omani identity, and it is present in the working culture in ways that distinguish an Omani colleague from an Emirati or Saudi one in ways that matter.

Omanis were seafarers and traders long before the modern state existed — the Omani maritime empire at its height in the 18th and 19th centuries stretched from Muscat to Zanzibar and the East African coast. The modern Oman is largely the creation of Sultan Qaboos bin Said, who came to power in 1970 after a palace coup that deposed his father — a deeply conservative ruler who had kept Oman almost entirely closed to the outside world. By the time of Qaboos's death in 2020, after fifty years of rule, he had built a modern state, a functioning bureaucracy, and something rarer: a reputation for genuine political neutrality and diplomatic sophistication that allowed Oman to maintain relationships with Iran, Israel, and its Arab neighbours simultaneously.

What you encounter in an Omani colleague is typically: unhurried warmth, a dignity that is never showy, a deep courtesy that is cultural value rather than performance, and a patience in professional interaction that can initially feel puzzling to colleagues accustomed to faster-moving environments.

Omani working culture is shaped by patience, dignity, and a preference for consensus over speed. Here is what you will likely encounter.

Patience is not passivity — it is a practised value
Omani professional culture operates at a pace that reflects a genuine cultural orientation toward deliberation over speed. Decisions are considered carefully. Relationships are built over time. Rushing — pressing for faster resolution than the relationship or the process supports — tends to produce polite slowdown rather than acceleration. The relationship being built in the meantime is the mechanism through which things will eventually move.
Dignity and courtesy are non-negotiable
Omani professional interaction is characterised by a level of courtesy and dignified conduct that runs across all contexts. Raising your voice, showing visible frustration, or pressing a point in a way that causes embarrassment — to yourself or to anyone else — is a significant social failure. Colleagues who match this register tend to find doors open; those who do not tend to find them politely but firmly closed.
Hierarchy is present but worn lightly
Seniority carries real weight in Omani professional culture — in how rooms are entered, how conversation is structured, whose view shapes the outcome. At the same time, Omani hierarchy tends to be expressed through deference and respect rather than through explicit assertion of authority. A senior Omani figure rarely needs to remind you they are senior; the room adjusts around it.
Consensus matters — and so does the process of reaching it
Decision-making in Omani professional culture tends to be consultative. A decision arrived at without the appropriate people having been heard, even if it is substantively correct, may not carry the legitimacy it needs to be implemented. The consultation is how buy-in is built and how the decision becomes real. Patience with this process is what makes the work stick.
The relationship with frankness is calibrated
Omani communication tends to be indirect around difficult content — criticism, disagreement, bad news — particularly across hierarchical lines and in group settings. The real assessment often comes in private, between people who have established enough trust to speak more plainly. Direct confrontation in public tends to close conversations rather than open them.
Practical tips
→
Slow down. Match the pace of the culture rather than trying to accelerate it. The relationship you are building now is what makes things move later.
→
Accept the coffee and the dates. Qahwa — Omani coffee, flavoured with cardamom and rosewater — and dates are the traditional welcome. Hold the cup with the right hand and tilt it gently when you have had enough.
→
Dress conservatively and consistently. Oman is more conservative than the UAE. Modesty in dress, across genders, is a baseline expectation rather than a specific-context requirement.
→
Never show visible frustration. Whatever is happening, composed and courteous is the correct register. Visible impatience causes lasting damage to a relationship that is very hard to repair.
→
Invest in the one-to-one relationship. The real conversations in Omani professional culture happen between people who know and trust each other, not in formal group settings.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

Sultan Qaboos is a figure of genuine reverence
Qaboos transformed Oman within a single lifetime, and the affection Omanis feel for him is real and deep — not performed loyalty but felt gratitude for what he built. His death in January 2020 was mourned widely. His successor, Sultan Haitham bin Tariq, has sought continuity, and early assessments have been cautiously positive. Treating Qaboos as just another autocrat, or being dismissive about the Omani monarchy in front of Omani colleagues, misreads both the history and the sentiment.
In practice: If the topic of Qaboos arises, listen before you speak. The feelings tend to be personal as well as political.
Ibadi Islam is distinct — and Omanis are proud of it
Most of the Islamic world is either Sunni or Shia. Oman is the only country in the world with a majority Ibadi Muslim population — a branch of Islam that predates the Sunni-Shia split and places particular emphasis on moderation, communal harmony, and the avoidance of religious conflict. This shapes how Omanis think about religious tolerance, how they maintain relationships with Sunni Saudi Arabia and Shia Iran simultaneously, and how they understand their own national character. Omanis are generally proud of this distinction, and acknowledging it shows real attention to who they are.
In practice: If the topic of religion arises, asking about the Ibadi tradition specifically — rather than assuming Sunni or Shia — tends to land as real knowledge rather than generic interest.
Hospitality runs deep and operates by specific rules
Omani hospitality is warm, generous, and structured. Coffee and dates on arrival, food offered before business is conducted, the host's obligation to provide and the guest's obligation to receive graciously. Qahwa — Omani coffee — is served in small handleless cups: hold with the right hand, and when you have had enough, tilt the cup gently from side to side rather than placing it down. Refusing hospitality — particularly on a first visit or in someone's home — creates genuine discomfort that takes time to recover from.
In practice: Learn the coffee cup signal before you need it. Getting it right is a small thing that lands disproportionately well.
Diplomatic caution carries into the workplace
Oman's foreign policy is built on maintaining functional relationships with all sides simultaneously — without being publicly drawn into anyone's camp. This diplomatic instinct has a cultural expression in how Omani professionals navigate internal disagreement and external pressure. Taking strong public positions, forcing colleagues to declare allegiances, or pressing for explicit sides to be taken tends to produce withdrawal rather than engagement. The preference is always for a path that keeps relationships intact.
In practice: Frame disagreements as shared problems to be solved rather than positions to be won. The Omani instinct for finding a route that preserves all relationships is worth working with rather than against.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures is not about bad intentions — it is about different defaults. Here is what is often going on underneath.

Omani culturemeetsfast-moving, output-first cultures
"Everything takes so long — I cannot tell if they are interested or not"
Omani professional pace reflects a genuine cultural orientation toward deliberation, relationship, and consensus rather than speed. The interest is often real; the timeline is just different. Pressing harder does not accelerate things — it tends to produce polite withdrawal. The absence of urgency is not the absence of engagement.
What helps: Build timelines that account for the real pace, not a projected one. Treat each interaction as relationship-building, not just task completion. The pace rewards patience in a way that impatience does not.
Omani culturemeetsdirect-feedback cultures
"I cannot get a straight answer — everything is very diplomatic"
Omani indirect communication around difficult content is a function of how much this culture values face, dignity, and the preservation of the relationship. The real answer is usually present — in what is omitted, in the hesitation, in the change of subject, in the private conversation that follows the formal one. The indirectness is a signal, not an absence of information.
What helps: Create private, low-stakes space for honest conversation. "I want to make sure I am not missing something — is there anything about this that you would approach differently?" asked one-to-one tends to surface what the meeting did not.
Omani culturemeetscolleagues who conflate it with neighbouring Gulf states
"I assumed Oman would be like the UAE — it is quite different"
Oman is frequently grouped with the UAE, Qatar, and Saudi Arabia in ways that obscure meaningful cultural differences. Oman is older, quieter, more anchored in its own history, less shaped by the rapid transformation that oil wealth triggered in its neighbours, and operating from a distinctly different religious and historical tradition. Treating Omani colleagues as interchangeable with Emirati or Saudi ones tends to produce a polite but definite correction, usually nonverbal.
What helps: Ask specifically about what makes Oman distinct. Most Omanis have clear and often proud answers to this question, and asking it signals that you see the difference.
Omani culturemeetsimpatient negotiators
"They agreed in principle but nothing ever gets confirmed"
Omani agreement in principle and formal commitment are different things, and the distance between them is covered by relationship, consultation, and process — none of which can be skipped. Pushing for written confirmation before the relationship and process have run their course tends to produce delay rather than resolution.
What helps: Treat the process as the product, not as the obstacle to the product. Each meeting, each shared coffee, each informal check-in is moving toward the commitment — just not in a straight line.
Omani culturemeetscolleagues unfamiliar with Islamic professional norms
"They disappear several times a day and I am never sure when they are available"
Prayer times — five daily — are a real and non-negotiable feature of a Muslim colleague's working day, and in Oman this is more present and less hidden than in more internationally calibrated workplaces. Scheduling meetings that cut across prayer times, or showing visible irritation at their effect on availability, signals that you have not understood the basic rhythm of the working environment you are in.
What helps: Learn the prayer times, build scheduling around them, and treat them as you would any other fixed calendar commitment. It takes five minutes to look up and saves a significant amount of friction.

Five things about Oman worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your Omani colleagues are coming from.

1
The Omani maritime empire once stretched from Muscat to Zanzibar — and the connection is still alive
At the height of Omani maritime power in the 18th and 19th centuries, Oman controlled a trading empire that extended from the Gulf to the East African coast, with the capital of the empire briefly relocated to Zanzibar under Sultan Said bin Sultan. The Omani presence in East Africa — in trade, in language, in architecture, in the spread of Islam — left marks that are still visible today. Swahili absorbed significant Arabic influence through this contact. The Old Town of Zanzibar retains architectural features that reflect centuries of Omani settlement. For Omanis, this maritime history is a source of real pride in a civilisational reach that the country's current size and quiet diplomacy does not immediately suggest.
Conversation opener: Ask an Omani colleague about the East Africa connection. It tends to produce something vivid and often surprising to people who did not know it.
2
Oman played a secret and significant role in brokering the Iran nuclear talks
Oman's unique diplomatic position — maintaining functional relationships with Iran, the Gulf states, Israel, and Western powers simultaneously — made it an indispensable back channel for some of the most sensitive diplomacy of recent decades. The secret preliminary talks that eventually led to the 2015 Iran nuclear deal were conducted in Muscat, facilitated by Omani officials, with the full knowledge and approval of Sultan Qaboos. This illustrates something real about how Oman understands its role in the world — not as a power that asserts itself loudly, but as one that uses its positioning to do things that louder powers cannot. Asking an Omani colleague about their country's diplomatic tradition tends to produce something quietly proud.
Conversation opener: Ask an Omani colleague what they think makes their country's diplomatic approach distinctive. The answer is usually considered and often specific.
3
Omani traditional dress — the dishdasha and the khanjar — carries specific meaning
The dishdasha — the ankle-length robe worn by Omani men, typically white for everyday wear and coloured for formal occasions — is worn with pride across all professional and social contexts, including the most international business environments. The khanjar — the curved ceremonial dagger worn at the waist — appears on the Omani national emblem and is worn at formal occasions. The tassel (assa) that hangs from the collar of the dishdasha is sometimes dipped in perfume. These are expressions of identity, and treating them with genuine curiosity — asking about the differences, the occasions, the regional variations — tends to land warmly.
Conversation opener: Ask an Omani colleague about the regional variations in how the dishdasha is worn. There are real differences, and most Omanis are happy to explain them.
4
Dhofari frankincense is among the finest in the world — and it has been traded from Oman for three thousand years
The frankincense trees of the Dhofar region in southern Oman produce a resin that has been prized across the ancient world — burned in Egyptian temples, carried along the incense route to Rome, mentioned in the Bible. The trade routes that carried Omani frankincense across the Arabian Peninsula placed Oman at the centre of ancient commerce for millennia, and the ruined city of Sumhuram — a UNESCO World Heritage site in Dhofar — was one of the great frankincense trading ports of the ancient world. Bakhoor, incense burned in the home, remains a daily ritual in many Omani households and is offered to guests as part of the hospitality tradition. Asking an Omani colleague about frankincense or Dhofar tends to unlock something warm and often nostalgic.
Conversation opener: Ask an Omani colleague whether they have been to Dhofar during the khareef season. The monsoon transformation of the landscape tends to produce vivid descriptions.
5
Oman has one of the oldest literary and musical traditions in the Arabian Peninsula — and a living oral poetry tradition
Arabic poetry is one of the oldest and most sophisticated literary traditions in the world, and Oman has been producing poets, scholars, and musicians within that tradition for centuries. Razha — a form of communal poetry and song performed at celebrations — remains a living practice. Omani classical music, drawing on African and South Asian as well as Arab influences — a legacy of the maritime trading past — is distinct from the music of neighbouring countries in ways that specialists find remarkable. Asking an Omani colleague about music or poetry, if the relationship supports it, tends to open something personal and often surprising in its depth.
Conversation opener: Ask an Omani colleague what kind of music is played at weddings or celebrations in their family. The answer usually reveals something about the regional and cultural layering that makes Omani music distinctive.
South Asia · Islamic Republic

Pakistan

A country most people outside it know mainly through the news — which is to say, they mostly know its difficulties and not much else. The working culture beneath the headlines is shaped by deep Islamic faith, hospitality that is among the most generous in the world, and a relationship-first logic that makes complete sense once you understand it.

Population 230M Capital: Islamabad Languages: Urdu, English + regional Currency: Pakistani Rupee (PKR)
پاکستان

Pakistan gained independence in 1947 — simultaneously with India, from the same colonial rupture — and has spent the decades since managing the weight of that founding, the turbulence of repeated political upheaval, and an economy that has delivered both real growth and serious volatility. What this produces in the working culture is a combination of resilience, adaptability, and a relationship-first logic that makes sense once you understand it: in an environment where formal institutions have been unreliable, personal networks are what actually holds things together.

The other essential piece of context is faith. Islam is not kept separate from professional life in Pakistan — it shapes the daily rhythm, the calendar, the ethics of how business is done, and the social norms that govern how colleagues interact. This is not background decoration; it is the operating framework. Understanding even the basics of how Islamic values show up in Pakistani working life changes how you read almost everything else.

Pakistani working culture is shaped by Islamic values, strong family ties, and a relationship-first logic. Here is what you are likely to encounter.

Faith structures the professional day
Five daily prayers — including Zuhr at midday and Asr in the afternoon — will interrupt the working day for many Pakistani colleagues, and this is entirely normal. Ramadan restructures the calendar significantly: fasting colleagues are working on reduced energy, the pace of the afternoon slows, and the evenings become the primary time for socialising and relationship-building. Treating these rhythms as interruptions rather than as the operating reality misreads how things actually work. The colleague who steps away to pray and returns five minutes later is doing exactly what their working environment expects and respects.
Relationships over process
Pakistani business culture runs on personal trust rather than institutional process. A contract matters, but it operates on top of a relational layer that is often more consequential. Before a commitment is made, before a deal moves forward, before a conflict is resolved, the relationship between the people involved is doing most of the work. Introductions through a mutual contact carry significant weight — being vouched for by someone your Pakistani counterpart already trusts compresses months of relationship-building into a much shorter time. Walking in cold, without a shared connection or any relational foundation, is starting at a real disadvantage.
Hierarchy and seniority are taken seriously
Pakistani organisations tend to be clearly hierarchical. Age carries genuine respect — older colleagues and seniors are addressed with appropriate deference, and challenging them directly, especially in front of others, causes real discomfort. Decisions typically sit at the top. Juniors may have strong views and real capability but will often wait for direction from above before acting. The person who chairs the meeting and the person whose opinion determines the outcome are not always the same person — it is worth knowing which is which before you arrive.
Hospitality is a reflex, not a performance
Pakistani hospitality is among the most immediate and generous you will encounter anywhere. Chai will appear within minutes of your arrival at an office or home. Lunch will be offered before it needs to be requested. The host's instinct is to provide, and the guest's expected instinct is to receive. This is not a ritual — it is a deeply held value with real social weight, rooted in both culture and faith. Declining repeatedly, whatever the practical reason, will eventually land as something that needs explaining.
Communication tends to be indirect around difficult topics
Pakistani professional communication is generally warm, relational, and not given to blunt negative feedback — particularly in front of others. Bad news is softened. A direct "no" is often avoided in favour of a more oblique response: the changed subject, the non-committal "we'll see," the agreement that is followed by no movement. This is a system designed to preserve the relationship and avoid public embarrassment for either party. The real answer often lives in the follow-up, not in the meeting.
Practical tips
→
Accept the chai. Always. It is the first gesture of welcome, and declining it — even innocently — starts the relationship on a slightly odd note.
→
Find the shared connection before you make contact. A warm introduction from someone they already trust will do more for the relationship than any amount of cold professional credentials.
→
Build in prayer time. Don't schedule the only available slot for a critical conversation in the five minutes before Zuhr. It will be interrupted, and you will have caused it.
→
Address senior people with appropriate formality until they invite otherwise. Sahib, sir, or the professional title — let them set the register.
→
Patience pays. Decisions take the time they take. Pushing for faster resolution than the relationship and the hierarchy support tends to slow things down rather than speed them up.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

Hospitality is close to a moral obligation
Being welcomed into a Pakistani home or office and declining what is offered creates genuine discomfort. The logic of the host-guest relationship runs deep in both Pashtun and broader Pakistani culture — the obligation is to give generously, and the guest's obligation is to receive with grace. If you genuinely cannot eat something or drink anything, a brief warm explanation is fine. The default should always be to accept. The symbolism of receiving what is offered matters more than the practical question of whether you are actually hungry.
In practice: Accept what is offered. If you have a dietary restriction, name it warmly and early — your host will immediately work around it, and the effort they put in will tell you something about how they understand hospitality.
Ask about family — and be ready to talk about yours
Family is the primary unit of Pakistani social life and will come up quickly and naturally in professional relationships. Questions about your family, your parents, your marital status — these are not intrusive. They are how Pakistani colleagues begin to understand who you are and where you fit. Answering with real warmth and asking back with real curiosity opens the relationship far faster than any amount of professional small talk.
In practice: "How's the family?" is not filler in Pakistan — it is the real check-in. Engage it as such.
Friday prayers restructure the morning
Friday Jumu'ah — the communal noon prayer — is an obligation for Muslim men and a significant weekly observance. It shapes the Friday morning calendar in a way that visitors often underestimate. Scheduling anything consequential for Friday midday will create friction. The working week has a built-in pause that is worth planning around from the start.
In practice: Keep Friday mornings light and avoid locking in critical meetings or deadlines at midday. The adjustment takes about five minutes to build into your planning habits.
The partition is history — and it is also personal
The 1947 partition of British India into India and Pakistan was one of the largest forced migrations in human history — around fourteen million people displaced and somewhere between 200,000 and two million killed in the accompanying violence. Pakistani colleagues whose families were part of the migration carry this history personally. Treating it as a dry historical event, or being casually comparative about India and Pakistan without acknowledging the weight of what happened, will land badly.
In practice: If it comes up, listen before you speak. The personal dimension is almost always closer to the surface than it first appears.
Don't mistake warmth for informality
Pakistani professional culture can be extremely warm — welcoming, friendly, and personal from early in the relationship. This is not an invitation to abandon professional respect for seniority and hierarchy. A colleague who is charming and warm will still expect that you address their senior with the right formality, that you do not challenge the boss in front of the room, and that the relational warmth and the professional structure coexist without contradiction. The warmth is real; the structure underneath it is equally real.
In practice: Match the warmth in tone and the formality in structure. One without the other reads as either cold or presumptuous.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures is not about bad intentions — it is about different defaults. Here is what is often going on underneath.

Pakistani culturemeetsdirect, task-first cultures
"They agreed in the meeting and then nothing moved"
The agreement in the meeting may have been real — in the sense that nobody disagreed — without constituting a firm commitment to action. In a culture where preserving harmony and avoiding public disagreement are structural priorities, the meeting often produces a surface consensus that doesn't always survive contact with the actual obstacles. What moves things is the follow-up conversation — privately, with the person who can actually make it happen. A written summary with clear owners and deadlines does real work here; it turns a social yes into a working one.
What helps: End every substantive meeting with an explicit, warm summary of who is doing what by when. Frame it as helpful clarity, not distrust.
Pakistani culturemeetslow-context, direct cultures
"I can never get a direct answer about what they actually think"
A direct "no" — particularly to a senior person, or in front of others — is socially costly in Pakistani professional culture. The more likely response is a warm non-committal, a shift to a related topic, or an "I'll look into it" that signals the real answer without stating it. This is a system for managing friction without damaging the relationship. Learning to read the indirect register takes time, but the signals are there — the absence of movement is usually more informative than the words.
What helps: Create private, informal space for honest conversation. One-to-one, over chai, with some relationship behind it, the real position usually surfaces. "I want to make sure I'm not missing something — is there anything about this that concerns you?" is a useful frame.
Pakistani diasporameetsnew-country workplaces
"They seem over-qualified for this role — why are they here?"
A significant number of Pakistani professionals working internationally are operating below their original professional level — engineers in technical support, lawyers in administrative positions, doctors in roles that don't yet recognise their qualifications — because credential recognition, language development, or the practical need for immediate income required a step down on arrival. This is not a signal about capability. It is a signal about what it takes to start again in an unfamiliar system.
What helps: Ask about their background directly and with real interest. The gap between what they have done and what they are currently doing is often striking — and worth acknowledging.
Pakistani culturemeetsflat-hierarchy, empowered cultures
"Everything has to go through the top — nobody will make a call"
In a clearly hierarchical culture, making a significant decision without the explicit involvement of the person above you can feel presumptuous — even when the decision technically falls within your remit. The junior colleague who keeps referring upward is correctly reading the system they are operating in. Delegation needs to be explicit and audible. "I want you to make this call — I trust your judgement and you don't need to check with me" is a sentence that needs to be said directly, not implied.
What helps: Be specific about what authority you are delegating and to whom. Implied delegation rarely travels as far as you think it does.

Five things about Pakistan worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your Pakistani colleagues are coming from.

1
The land before the country — five thousand years of civilisation
Pakistan is a new country sitting on some of the oldest urban settlements on earth. The Indus Valley Civilisation — centred on what is now Pakistan, at sites like Mohenjo-daro and Harappa — was one of the four great ancient civilisations, contemporary with Egypt and Mesopotamia, with sophisticated city planning, drainage systems, and trade networks dating to 2,500 BCE. Layers of Persian, Mughal, and Central Asian influence run through the culture in ways that the country's relative modernity as a political entity obscures. When a Pakistani colleague talks about heritage, they have a great deal to draw from.
Conversation opener: Ask a Pakistani colleague what they think is the most underappreciated fact about Pakistan's history. Clear some time.
2
Cricket is not a sport — it is a national emotional event
Cricket in Pakistan operates at a level of cultural intensity that is difficult to convey to someone who didn't grow up with it. The national team's performance is felt personally by millions. A Pakistan vs India match is one of the most-watched sporting events on earth — geopolitical tension, colonial history, and sporting rivalry compressed into one fixture. Imran Khan moved from cricket captain to prime minister, which tells you something about the social capital the sport generates. Knowing a few names — Wasim Akram, Waqar Younis, Shahid Afridi, Babar Azam — will open a conversation that can last an entire working lunch.
Conversation opener: Ask which Pakistan vs India match they remember most vividly. Everyone has one, and the telling is always vivid.
3
Pakistani food is one of the world's great cuisines — with almost no international profile
Karahi, nihari, biryani, haleem, seekh kebab, paya — Pakistani cuisine is robust, intensely spiced, and built around meat cookery and bread traditions refined over centuries. The Lahori food scene in particular has a reputation within Pakistan roughly comparable to Lyon's within France. The relative invisibility of Pakistani cuisine internationally is one of the more puzzling culinary blind spots in the world, given the quality and range of what exists. Asking a Pakistani colleague about regional food differences — Lahori vs Karachi vs Peshawar — will get you strong opinions immediately.
Conversation opener: Ask where to find the best nihari. Be prepared to receive a detailed, passionate answer — and possibly a home-cooked invitation.
4
The Sufi tradition and the side of Pakistan that rarely makes the news
Pakistani Islam has a rich Sufi tradition — mystical, musical, devotional — that coexists with more conservative strands and represents a significant part of how many Pakistanis experience their faith. Qawwali, the devotional music associated with Sufi practice, reached global audiences through Nusrat Fateh Ali Khan — one of the most extraordinary voices of the 20th century in any tradition. The annual Urs festivals at Sufi shrines draw millions of devotees. This dimension of Pakistani religious and cultural life rarely appears in international coverage, which tends to focus on the political and the conflictual. Knowing it exists tends to produce a very different conversation than most outsiders expect.
Conversation opener: Ask a Pakistani colleague about Nusrat Fateh Ali Khan. Even if they are not personally religious, the pride is almost always there.
5
The Pakistani diaspora is one of the most professionally successful in the world
The Pakistani community in the UK is the second-largest diaspora community in the country. Pakistani-origin professionals are prominent in medicine, law, business, technology, and politics in the UK, North America, and the Gulf — in numbers disproportionate to the size of the community. The value placed on education, the relational networks that support new arrivals, and the particular drive that comes from families who made significant sacrifices to move are all part of this story. Understanding the diaspora context is practically useful for anyone working with Pakistani colleagues internationally — many of them carry a dual identity with real fluency, moving between Pakistani family and cultural values and Western professional norms with a skill that often goes unacknowledged.
Conversation opener: Ask a Pakistani colleague abroad what they find hardest to explain about Pakistan to people who have never been. The answer is usually revealing — and rarely what you expected.
Southeast Asia · Archipelago nation

Philippines

Warm, collectivist, and shaped by four centuries of colonial history that left a Catholic faith, the English language, and a love of basketball — but didn't touch the underlying values. Filipino working culture runs on relationships, group harmony, and a loyalty that, once earned, is qualitatively different from anything a contract produces.

Population 115M Capital: Manila Languages: Filipino, English Currency: Philippine Peso (PHP)
PILIPINAS

The Philippines is one of the most genuinely surprising countries to work with — partly because it is so familiar on the surface and so distinct underneath. With over a century of American colonial influence, English as a working language, a largely Catholic population, and a diaspora of around twelve million people spread across global workplaces, Filipino colleagues can seem immediately accessible in a way that occasionally obscures how different the underlying operating logic actually is.

Spain for 333 years, then the United States for nearly fifty. The result is a culture that absorbed Catholicism deeply and American institutional forms, but did not absorb the individualism. Filipino working culture is collectivist to its core — oriented around the group, the family, and the relationship. The country is also an archipelago of over 7,600 islands, with more than 180 languages spoken and real regional diversity beneath the national identity. A colleague from Manila and one from Cebu or Davao are not the same person wearing the same flag.

Filipino working culture is warm, relationship-driven, and shaped by values that don't always announce themselves. Here is what you are likely to encounter.

Hierarchy, but warmly
Filipino organisations are clearly hierarchical — seniority commands real deference, decisions flow from the top, and juniors are unlikely to challenge or contradict their managers directly. But the hierarchy tends to be warmer and more personal than in many equivalents across Asia. A good boss in Filipino culture is not just an authority figure — they are expected to take a personal interest in their people, to show generosity, and to look after those under them. The deference going up and the care coming down are two sides of the same relationship. Managers who deliver only the authority without the warmth tend to find that the formal compliance it generates is real but thin.
Communication: yes, but which kind
Filipino professional communication is high-context and indirect around anything that could cause embarrassment or conflict. A direct "no" is culturally costly. The more common responses are a "yes" that means "I heard you," a "we'll try" that means "this is probably not going to happen," a hesitation that means "I disagree but won't say so," or a warm enthusiasm that doesn't survive contact with the obstacles. Learning to read the register — and creating private space for the honest version — is one of the more important skills for anyone working closely with Filipino colleagues.
Relationships before tasks
Business in the Philippines starts with getting to know each other. Jumping straight to agenda can feel abrupt in a culture where the relationship is not the prelude to the work — it is part of the work itself. This is not small talk for its own sake; it is the process by which trust is built, and trust is the foundation on which everything else rests. Filipino colleagues tend to give their best — real commitment, real effort, real loyalty — to people they feel connected to.
The group over the individual
Filipino working culture is collectivist in a way that shapes how credit is claimed, how decisions are made, and how conflict is handled. Standing out individually at the expense of the group creates discomfort. Consensus and collective ownership of outcomes are preferred over individual heroics, and the instinct to protect group harmony runs deep. This produces teams with real solidarity and mutual support — and occasionally a reluctance to surface problems that might disrupt the collective peace.
Time and the relational variable
Filipino professional culture operates on a more fluid relationship with time than Northern European or American counterparts. Schedules flex, meetings start late, and deadlines carry different weight depending on who set them and what the relationship is. The key variable is usually relational: a deadline tied to a person tends to land with more weight than one tied to a process or a system. If a deadline is hard, explaining why — and making it personal — changes things.
Practical tips
→
Never criticise a Filipino colleague in front of others. The damage to the relationship is real and slow to repair. One-to-one, warm, and private is the only register that works for difficult feedback.
→
Take the social layer seriously. Lunch, small talk, asking about family, remembering what someone told you last week — this is how the trust that makes the work possible gets built.
→
Don't read the yes as a yes until something moves. Calibrate by watching what happens, not by what was said in the meeting.
→
Show personal warmth toward people at all levels. Filipino working culture notices whether a senior person treats everyone with respect. It matters more than most hierarchical cultures.
→
Acknowledge milestones and celebrations. Birthdays, family events, personal news — remembering them signals that you see the person, not just the role.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

Hiya: the social force that shapes almost everything
Hiya is often translated as "shame" but functions more like a deep social consciousness — an acute awareness of how one's behaviour reflects on oneself and on one's group. The avoidance of hiya shapes how Filipino colleagues handle disagreement, feedback, criticism, and failure. Putting someone in a position where they are embarrassed in front of others is one of the most damaging things you can do to a professional relationship in the Philippines. The lengths Filipino colleagues will go to in order to avoid this — including agreeing to things they cannot deliver, or softening bad news until it is unrecognisable — are a direct product of how seriously hiya is felt.
In practice: If something feels like it's being over-softened or the answer keeps coming back vague, hiya is usually part of the explanation. Create private space, reduce the social cost of honesty, and the real answer will usually surface.
Pakikisama: the pull of group harmony
Pakikisama — roughly "group solidarity" — describes the deep instinct to maintain harmony within one's circle, to avoid disrupting relationships, and to prioritise the collective over the individual. In the workplace, this means open disagreement is unusual, the group's comfort takes priority over any individual's candid view, and maintaining smooth relationships is a genuine social obligation. The colleague who never rocks the boat is not being passive — they are being responsible, by their own cultural logic.
In practice: If you want real opinions, reduce the audience. One-to-one, informal, with enough trust established — the candid view is usually there. It just doesn't surface in group settings where pakikisama is in play.
Utang na loob: the debt that doesn't expire
Utang na loob — "debt of the inner self," sometimes translated as debt of gratitude — describes the profound obligation created when someone does something significant for you. When a colleague, boss, or mentor has genuinely helped you, the obligation created goes deeper than a professional thank-you. It becomes a lasting reciprocal bond. This explains both the intensity of Filipino loyalty to people who have invested in them, and the genuine hurt when that loyalty is not acknowledged or is discarded.
In practice: If you invest in a Filipino colleague — genuinely, personally — expect something qualitatively different in return from what professional obligation alone would produce.
Celebrations are taken seriously — and generosity is expected
Birthdays in Filipino working culture often involve the birthday person bringing food for the whole office — not the other way around. Celebrations are communal, generous, and frequent. Not participating — arriving late, skipping, or engaging half-heartedly — is noticed in a culture where showing up for shared moments is part of the social contract. The office birthday, the farewell lunch, the Friday merienda — these are not optional extras. They are the texture of belonging.
In practice: Show up. Bring something if it's your turn. The gesture is the point, and its absence is noticed more than its presence.
Ningas-kugon: the fire that burns fast
Ningas-kugon — literally "cogon grass fire," which blazes intensely and dies quickly — is a Filipino phrase for the tendency to begin something with great enthusiasm and then let it fade. It is acknowledged self-critically within Filipino culture. New projects and new commitments often begin with a burst of warmth and energy that can plateau once the initial excitement passes. Consistent follow-up and visible ongoing interest tend to sustain what the initial burst started.
In practice: Build in regular touchpoints rather than assuming momentum will carry itself. Re-engagement is how ongoing commitment gets expressed and reinforced in this culture.
Regionalism is more significant than outsiders expect
The Philippines contains distinct regional cultures with their own languages, cuisines, and identities. Tagalog-speaking Manileños, Cebuanos, Ilocanos, Bicolanos, and Mindanaoans are not interchangeable. The rivalry between Manila and Cebu over which city is more liveable — and whose food is better — can produce opinions of real intensity. Knowing where a Filipino colleague is from and showing curiosity about that specific place lands very differently from treating the Philippines as a single homogeneous culture.
In practice: Ask where they're from early. The conversation that follows will tell you more about who they are than most other questions you could ask.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures is not about bad intentions — it is about different defaults. Here is what is often going on underneath.

Filipino culturemeetsdirect, task-first cultures
"They said yes in the meeting and then it didn't happen"
The "yes" in the meeting may have been a expression of willingness, or it may have been the most socially comfortable response to a question that felt difficult to deflect. In a culture where saying "no" directly — especially to a senior person — carries real social cost, the agreement in the meeting is not always a reliable indicator of what will actually be delivered. What moves things is the follow-up: a warm, specific, written summary of who is doing what, and a check-in point. The agreement needs to be converted from a social moment into a working one.
What helps: End every substantive meeting with a clear, friendly summary of owners and deadlines. Frame it as helpful clarity, not distrust.
