Filipino culturemeetsdirect feedback cultures
"They're incredibly warm but I can never get a straight answer about problems"
The warmth and the indirectness come from the same source — a deep orientation toward preserving the relationship and avoiding hiya for either party. Problems are often softened, delayed, or communicated so obliquely that the recipient doesn't fully register them. This is honesty being expressed through a different system. The challenge is building enough trust and creating enough private space that the real version surfaces before it becomes a crisis.
What helps: Ask specifically and privately. "What's the hardest part of this right now?" gives permission to name something that the standard check-in usually doesn't.
Direct feedback culturesmeetsFilipino culture
"I gave feedback and now the relationship feels cold"
Feedback delivered directly — particularly in front of others, or without sufficient warmth and relational framing — can land in Filipino culture as a public shaming rather than a professional input. The person on the receiving end may not express the hurt openly, but the relationship changes. The silence, the reduced warmth, the slightly more careful behaviour afterward — these are the signals. Repairing it requires going back privately, acknowledging what happened with warmth, and re-establishing the relational foundation.
What helps: Go back privately and directly — "I want to check in about how I delivered that feedback." The repair conversation is usually well received when it comes from care.
Filipino culturemeetscultures expecting proactive contribution
"They seem very capable but never volunteer an opinion"
In a hierarchical culture shaped by hiya and pakikisama, putting forward an unsolicited opinion — particularly one that challenges the senior person's view — carries real social risk. The Filipino colleague who is visibly capable but consistently quiet in meetings is not lacking in ideas. They are reading the room correctly, by their own cultural logic. The question is whether the environment has been made safe enough for candour — and that usually requires a deliberate and explicit invitation from someone with authority.
What helps: Invite directly and specifically — "I want to hear your take on this before we decide" — and make it clear that a different view is welcome. Then mean it.

Five things about the Philippines worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your Filipino colleagues are coming from.

1
The Philippines has more overseas workers per capita than almost any major country — and the culture is shaped by it
Around twelve million Filipinos live and work abroad — roughly ten percent of the population. Overseas Filipino Workers (OFWs) have been sending remittances home for decades, and this outward migration has become woven into the national identity and economy. Most Filipino families have at least one member who has lived or worked overseas. This means that most Filipino colleagues you encounter internationally are part of a culture that has been processing the experience of working far from home, in unfamiliar systems, for generations. The adaptability, the English fluency, and the particular emotional intelligence around being the outsider in the room — these come from somewhere specific.
Conversation opener: Ask a Filipino colleague abroad whether they have family elsewhere too. The answer almost always opens something real.
2
The Spanish colonial period left a Catholicism that is unlike anywhere else
The Philippines was named after King Philip II of Spain and converted to Catholicism over three centuries of colonial rule. The result absorbed indigenous beliefs and became something distinctly Filipino — the Santo Niño as a national icon, the Black Nazarene procession in Manila drawing millions of barefoot devotees, elaborate passion plays of Holy Week. For many Filipinos, faith is not a private matter — it is a public, communal, emotionally intense part of life that coexists naturally with modernity and a thoroughly contemporary existence. Acknowledging it with curiosity rather than surprise goes a long way.
Conversation opener: Ask about Sinulog in Cebu or the Black Nazarene in Manila. The personal connection is usually immediate.
3
Manny Pacquiao went from poverty to eight world titles to the Senate — and it tells you something real
Manny Pacquiao — eight-division world boxing champion, one of the greatest fighters in the history of the sport — was elected to the Philippine Senate in 2016 and ran for president in 2022. The arc from poverty in General Santos City to global sporting icon to national politician is not just an individual story — it is a Filipino story about what sport means as a channel for pride, identity, and transformation. His career is extraordinary by any measure, and knowing it tends to open a conversation that goes well beyond boxing.
Conversation opener: Ask which Pacquiao fight they remember most. Everyone has one.
4
Filipino food is deeply personal — and still waiting for its international moment
Adobo, sinigang, lechon, kare-kare, halo-halo — Filipino cuisine is rich, complex, and carries the specific emotional weight that home food carries everywhere, amplified for people who are far from home. It has been regularly predicted to break through internationally in the way Thai and Vietnamese food did, without quite having done so yet. For Filipino colleagues abroad, food is often the most direct available form of cultural expression and home-connection. If someone offers you home-cooked Filipino food, real interest and genuine engagement is the appropriate response.
Conversation opener: Ask what their family's adobo recipe is like — every family has its own version and strong feelings about it.
5
Basketball is the national sport — and the passion for it is entirely disproportionate to the country's size
Basketball, not football, is the Philippines' dominant sport — a legacy of American colonial rule that took root so completely it became entirely its own. The Philippine Basketball Association, founded in 1975, is the second-oldest professional basketball league in the world after the NBA. Courts are everywhere — on rooftops, between buildings, in villages with limited electricity. The love of the game is followed and played with genuine passion by people who have no expectation of ever seeing a Filipino in the NBA. Kai Sotto's attempts have been followed with the kind of national attention most countries reserve for World Cup campaigns.
Conversation opener: Ask which PBA team they support, or who their favourite NBA player is. Strong opinions and mild arguments are essentially guaranteed.
Southern Europe · EU member

Portugal

A small country on the Atlantic edge of Europe that once connected the world — and has been living with that history ever since.

Population 10M Capital: Lisbon Language: Portuguese EU member since 1986 Currency: Euro (EUR)
PORTUGAL

Portugal spent a century and a half as one of the great maritime empires in history. From the early 15th century, Portuguese navigators opened sea routes to West Africa, Brazil, India, the Far East, and the Pacific — building a trading empire that connected four continents and left a linguistic and cultural imprint still visible across the world today. The Portuguese language is now spoken by around 250 million people — the long shadow of an empire that punched far above the weight of a small Atlantic country.

What followed that imperial era was long and complicated. Centuries of relative decline, a dictatorship under Salazar that lasted from 1933 to 1974, and a Carnation Revolution in April 1974 that ended it almost without bloodshed — one of the more remarkable peaceful transitions in modern European history. Portugal joined the EU in 1986, underwent rapid modernisation, and has in recent decades built a reputation as one of the more stable, open, and liveable societies in southern Europe.

That arc — imperial ambition, long contraction, quiet reinvention — produces a culture with a particular relationship to time, memory, and the wider world. Saudade — the Portuguese word for a melancholic longing for something loved and lost or absent — is not a cliché invented for tourism. It is a real emotional register that runs through the culture, the music, and the way Portuguese people carry their history.

What to expect when working with Portuguese colleagues — the patterns, defaults, and unspoken rules that shape how things actually get done.

Relationships come before business — and they're built slowly
Portuguese professional culture is relationship-first in a way that is similar to Spain but tends to be quieter in register. The personal connection needs to be established before the professional one can function properly — but the warmth is less demonstrative and takes longer to surface than in some other Latin cultures. Trust is built through consistency, real interest, and showing up reliably over time — not through a single warm meeting.
Hierarchy is real and respected
Portuguese organisations tend to have clear hierarchical structures, and decisions generally sit with senior people. The distance between levels is real — juniors tend to be more deferential in formal settings, and challenging upward publicly is uncommon. This doesn't mean that good ideas from junior people don't travel — it means they travel through the right channels, at the right moment, often via a trusted intermediary rather than directly.
Communication is indirect about conflict, direct about facts
Portuguese colleagues tend to be direct when conveying information or describing a situation — but considerably more indirect when it comes to disagreement, criticism, or delivering unwelcome news. Saying no directly, especially to someone senior, is culturally uncomfortable. What you hear is often a softened version of a harder reality. Learning to read the hedges and the silences is a useful skill.
The work gets done — but the pace is its own
Deadlines in Portuguese professional culture are taken seriously in principle but tend to be understood with some flexibility in practice, particularly for internal work. The relationship between the clock and the quality of what's being produced is weighted differently here. The counterpart is that when something matters, the effort tends to follow — often quietly, often at cost to personal time, without much being said about it.
Formality at the start, warmth over time
The opening register in Portuguese professional settings tends to be more formal than in Spain — titles are used, surnames are common until familiarity develops, and the transition to informality is earned rather than assumed. In more established organisations, formal titles and courteous distance are the baseline. The warmth is real; it just has a longer runway.
Practical tips
→
Invest in the relationship before the work. Coffee, real conversation, asking about family and life — that investment in relationship is the infrastructure on which everything else runs.
→
Start formal and follow their lead. Senhor / Senhora with surname is the safe opening. Let them set the pace toward first names.
→
Read between the lines. A polite hedge or a vague answer is often carrying more information than it seems. Ask follow-up questions gently and you'll usually get closer to the real picture.
→
Don't schedule important things in August. Portugal largely closes. Plan around it.
→
Be patient with decisions. The informal consultation and relationship-building that precedes a formal decision takes time. Pushing for a fast answer before the groundwork is laid tends to produce a yes that doesn't stick.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out — things that seem obvious from the inside and invisible from the outside. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

Saudade is real — and it shapes the register
Saudade is often translated as nostalgia or longing, but it's more specific than either — a bittersweet feeling for something loved that is absent, gone, or perhaps never fully possessed. It runs through fado, through literature, through the way people talk about the sea and about emigration. It doesn't make Portuguese colleagues melancholic in a professional sense — but it explains a certain reflective quality, a tendency to look backward as well as forward, and a relationship with loss that is more articulate and accepted than in cultures that insist on relentless forward motion.
In practice: Don't interpret a moment of wistfulness or a backward-looking comment as pessimism. It's often just the cultural register — a way of holding the present and the past at the same time.
Indirect disagreement is not vagueness — it's the actual communication style
Saying no directly, especially upward or in a group setting, is culturally uncomfortable. Problems get softened, concerns get deferred, unwelcome news travels through intermediaries rather than arriving plainly. A polite hedge is often carrying significantly more weight than it appears. Learning to ask a follow-up question — "and what's the hardest part of that?" — gets you closer to the real picture than a literal reading of the first answer.
In practice: Create private, low-stakes moments for honest input. A one-to-one coffee — "just between us, how is it actually going?" — surfaces what the formal meeting often doesn't.
Formality signals respect, not coldness
The formal opening register — titles, surnames, a certain professional distance — is not a barrier to relationship. It's a signal that the relationship is being taken seriously. Moving too quickly to first names or casual register reads as presumptuous rather than warm. The transition happens, but it's theirs to offer.
In practice: Mirror the register you're given. If they use your surname, use theirs. If they switch to first names, follow.
Criticism of Portugal is a Portuguese privilege
Like Ireland and Lithuania, Portugal has a strong internal critique culture — the bureaucracy, the brain drain, the pace of change. A foreigner who joins in will find the conversation cool very quickly. The critique is internal; the expected response from outside is curiosity, not validation.
In practice: If a Portuguese colleague vents about Portugal, listen with real interest. Ask questions. Don't volunteer your own assessment of the country's problems.
Being a guest carries obligations on both sides
Portuguese hospitality is warm and generous, and accepting it properly matters. Showing up to someone's home empty-handed is noticed. Refusing food or drink repeatedly causes discomfort. The host provides; the guest accepts and shows real interest in what's been offered. The meal is an act of care, not just a social occasion.
In practice: Bring something when invited to a home. Accept what's offered. Ask about the food — it opens warmth that nothing else quite does.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures isn't about bad intentions — it's about different defaults. Here's what's often going on underneath.

Portuguese culturemeetsdirect, task-first cultures
"They're warm in conversation but hard to pin down on decisions"
The warmth in the conversation was real — but it wasn't a commitment. In a relationship-first, hierarchical culture where decisions travel upward and are confirmed informally before they're announced formally, the meeting is often not where the decision happens. "That sounds promising" and "we have a decision" are different things, and treating the first as the second produces frustration in both directions.
What helps: Confirm in writing after the conversation. "Great to talk — just to capture where we landed: we're going with X, confirmed by Y date?" makes the commitment visible without creating confrontation.
Portuguese culturemeetsdirect-feedback cultures
"I never know if there's a real problem — everything sounds fine until it isn't"
Delivering bad news directly — particularly upward, or in a group setting — is culturally uncomfortable. Problems tend to be softened, deferred, or communicated through intermediaries rather than flagged plainly in a meeting. What sounds like a minor concern may be carrying significantly more weight than the delivery suggests. The hedge is often doing more work than it appears.
What helps: Create private moments for honest input. A one-to-one — "just between us, how is it actually going?" — surfaces what the formal meeting often doesn't. Ask specifically about the hardest part, not whether things are fine.
Portuguese culturemeetsfast-moving, low-hierarchy cultures
"They seem cautious and slow to commit — I can't build momentum"
Caution before commitment is not risk-aversion or lack of ambition — it's a considered approach to making sure the relationship, the context, and the informal alignment are in place before something is formalised. Decisions made before the groundwork is laid tend not to stick. The apparent slowness is often the actual process, not a delay to it.
What helps: Invest in the informal conversations that need to happen before the formal decision. Understanding who needs to be comfortable, and speaking to those people early, removes most of the friction.

Five things about Portugal worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your Portuguese colleagues are coming from.

1
The Age of Discovery changed the shape of the world
In the space of roughly a century — from the 1420s to the 1520s — Portuguese navigators mapped most of the world's coastlines, established the first direct sea route from Europe to Asia, reached Brazil, and built a trading network that connected four continents. Vasco da Gama, Bartolomeu Dias, Ferdinand Magellan, Pedro Álvares Cabral — these are not just national heroes; they are figures who altered the course of history. For a country of ten million people on the Atlantic edge of Europe, the scale of that achievement shapes a particular cultural self-image: small in geography, large in reach, accustomed to thinking beyond the immediate horizon.
Conversation opener: Ask a Portuguese colleague which navigator they find most interesting and why. The answer is usually specific, proud, and tells you something about how they see Portugal's place in the world.
2
Fado — a music built from loss
Fado emerged in Lisbon in the early 19th century, likely from the confluence of African rhythms, Brazilian musical influences, and older Portuguese folk traditions. It is built around saudade — longing, fate, the sea, and the people who don't come back. Amália Rodrigues, who brought fado to international audiences in the mid-20th century, is one of the most important cultural figures in Portuguese history. Fado was inscribed as UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage in 2011 — an acknowledgement that it is not merely music but a form of cultural expression that is irreplaceable.
Conversation opener: Ask a Portuguese colleague whether they grew up listening to fado, and what it means to them now. The relationship with it is often complicated and always personal.
3
The Carnation Revolution ended a dictatorship — almost without a shot fired
On 25 April 1974, a group of military officers staged a coup that ended 48 years of authoritarian rule under the Estado Novo regime. The operation was so well-coordinated and so widely supported that it succeeded with minimal violence. Civilians took to the streets and placed red carnations in the soldiers' rifle barrels — a detail so improbable it would seem invented if it weren't documented. April 25th is a national holiday in Portugal and is treated not as a routine civic commemoration but as a living reminder of what changed and what it took.
Conversation opener: Ask a Portuguese colleague over 50 what April 25th means to their family. For those old enough to remember the dictatorship, the answer is rarely abstract.
4
Portugal's cork forests are a remarkable ecological and economic story
Portugal produces around half of the world's cork — the bark of the cork oak (Quercus suber), harvested by hand every nine years without cutting down the tree. The Alentejo region's cork oak forests are among the most biodiverse ecosystems in Europe, supporting rare species of birds and mammals. Cork harvesting is one of the most sustainable industries in the world, and the craft of doing it properly — knowing when the bark is ready, how to strip it without damaging the tree — is passed down through generations. It's an unexpectedly compelling story about a material most people only think about when opening a bottle of wine.
Conversation opener: Ask a Portuguese colleague from the Alentejo about cork. You'll get a depth of knowledge most people from outside Portugal simply don't have.
5
Portuguese football has a depth that goes well beyond Ronaldo
Portugal has produced some of the most technically gifted players in the history of the game — Eusébio, considered one of the greatest forwards of the 1960s, was the top scorer at the 1966 World Cup and remains a figure of deep reverence. Luís Figo won the Ballon d'Or in 2000. Ronaldo has broken almost every record in the sport. But the Portuguese relationship with football is less tribal and more aesthetic than in Spain or Italy — there is a real appreciation for technique, for the quality of the game itself, that runs alongside the club loyalties and national pride. Asking a Portuguese colleague about Eusébio — particularly if they're from Lisbon — usually opens something warm and specific.
Conversation opener: Ask a Portuguese colleague which player — past or present — they think is most underappreciated internationally. Eusébio comes up often, and always with feeling.
Southeast Europe · EU member

Romania

A Romance language in a Slavic sea — and a working culture shaped by a history that is closer than it looks.

Population 19M Capital: Bucharest Language: Romanian EU member since 2007 Currency: Leu (RON)
ROMÂNIA

Romania's position in Europe is one of the more overlooked stories on the continent. The territory has been contested, occupied, and reshaped repeatedly — by the Roman Empire, the Ottoman Empire, the Austro-Hungarian Empire, and then the Soviet sphere — and each left traces that are still visible in the language, the architecture, and the way institutions work. Romanian is a Romance language, an island of Latin in a Slavic sea, a linguistic fact that Romanians are deeply aware of and that shapes a particular sense of civilisational identity.

The 20th century compressed an extraordinary amount into a short time. Romania emerged from WWII on the losing side, was absorbed into the Soviet bloc, and then endured one of the most repressive regimes in Eastern Europe under Nicolae Ceaușescu — a dictatorship that collapsed violently in December 1989, the only Eastern Bloc transition that ended with the execution of a head of state. What came after was a chaotic and painful transition: mass emigration, institutional collapse, slow and uneven reconstruction. Romania joined the EU in 2007, and the gap between where it was in 1990 and where it is now is one of the more striking transformations in European recent history — though it has come at the cost of a significant brain drain, with an estimated three to four million Romanians living and working abroad.

Romanian working culture is shaped by history as much as by any management theory. Here's what you'll likely encounter.

Adaptability is a deeply ingrained working style
Decades of operating in a system where official processes rarely worked as described produced a cultural fluency with improvisation, workarounds, and creative problem-solving under constraint. Improvisation and creative problem-solving under constraint became a real skill — and often still is. Romanian colleagues are often excellent at finding a path when the obvious one is blocked, and at making things work with fewer resources than should be necessary. The counterpart is that rigid process-following for its own sake can feel pointless to people who learned to read which rules actually matter.
Directness varies — but scepticism is consistent
Romanian communication style tends to be fairly direct about problems and obstacles, though the register varies by generation, sector, and individual. What is more consistent is a sceptical baseline: optimistic projections and top-down enthusiasm are received with quiet doubt rather than energetic buy-in. The scepticism is a pattern recognition, developed over time, about the gap between how things are announced and how they actually unfold.
Hierarchy is real, but competence cuts through it
Seniority matters in Romanian organisations, particularly in more traditional or public-sector environments, and decisions tend to sit with senior people. At the same time, demonstrated competence carries independent weight — the person who clearly knows what they're doing earns latitude that pure rank doesn't automatically confer. In tech and startup environments, the register tends to flatten considerably.
Trust is personal, not institutional
The relevant question in a Romanian professional context is usually not "does the system work?" but "do I trust this specific person?" Institutional trust — faith in processes, contracts, official channels — is lower than in Western European contexts, as a legacy of decades in which institutions were at best unreliable and at worst actively harmful. What works instead is personal trust, built through reliability, follow-through, and consistent behaviour over time.
Hard work is taken seriously — and often invisible
There is no culture of performing effort loudly. Work gets done without much commentary about how hard it was. But when something matters and the relationship is solid, the commitment tends to be real and often goes well beyond what was formally required. Don't mistake the quiet for low investment.
Practical tips
→
Follow through on what you commit to. Personal reliability is the primary currency. Promises that evaporate are remembered.
→
Don't oversell. Optimistic framing and enthusiastic promises land with scepticism. Understate and overdeliver.
→
Bring real problems. The improvisational instinct is a real strength. Colleagues who can solve something complex are respected more than those who manage a smooth process.
→
Don't expect process to substitute for relationship. A contract or a procedure is a starting point. The actual working relationship runs through the person, not the document.
→
Be patient with institutional friction. Some of it is cultural legacy, some of it is real structural complexity. Romanian colleagues who navigate it are usually doing something useful.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out — things that seem obvious from the inside and invisible from the outside. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

Hospitality is an obligation — and a test
If a Romanian colleague invites you to their home, the expectation is abundance: food, drink, insistence that you eat more. Refusing repeatedly or treating the hospitality lightly causes real discomfort. Accepting — and showing real pleasure in what's been prepared — is how you signal that you're the kind of person worth knowing. The table is a place where trust gets built or withheld.
In practice: Accept what's offered, eat with interest, and ask about what you're eating. Bring something when invited — wine, flowers, something for the table.
Complaining about Romania is a Romanian right
Romanians can be pointed critics of their own country — the institutions, the corruption, the brain drain, the roads. A foreigner who joins in will find the temperature drop immediately. The critique is internal; the expected response from outside is curiosity, not agreement.
In practice: If a Romanian colleague vents about Romania, listen and ask questions. Don't volunteer your own assessment of the country's problems.
History is closer than it looks
The Ceaușescu period ended in 1989 — within the living memory of most working-age Romanians. Many have parents or grandparents who navigated informers, shortages, and the specific psychological weight of life under that system. For most working-age Romanians, this is family history — and it explains a great deal about the relationship to authority, official information, and institutional trust that doesn't have an obvious explanation otherwise.
In practice: If the communist period comes up, listen more than you speak. Curiosity and respect go further than conclusions from outside.
The gap between what's said officially and what's real is assumed to be large
Decades of a system in which official statements bore little relationship to reality left a cultural habit of reading the gap between the formal version and the actual one. Press releases, official announcements, and top-down communications are received with this frame by default. It isn't cynicism so much as calibrated reading — and it applies to organisations as much as to governments.
In practice: Be straightforward about what is actually happening, even when it's awkward. A Romanian colleague who senses a gap between the official version and reality will trust you less, not more, for maintaining it.
Religion and family are foundational — without being discussed
The Orthodox Church and the extended family are central to Romanian social life in ways that don't always surface in professional conversation but quietly shape a great deal of behaviour: how decisions are made, when people are unavailable, what obligations take precedence. You don't need to engage with either directly — but knowing they're there, and treating them with respect when they surface, matters.
In practice: If a colleague needs time for a family or religious obligation, treat it as a legitimate priority without comment. It is.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures isn't about bad intentions — it's about different defaults. Here's what's often going on underneath.

Romanian culturemeetsoptimism-default cultures
"They're sceptical about everything — it's like they don't want it to succeed"
The scepticism is not about wanting something to fail — it's a default assumption that the gap between how something is described and how it actually unfolds will be significant. Naming problems early is how serious people prepare, not how resistant people resist. Treating the concern-raising as an attitude problem misses what's actually being offered.
What helps: Invite the risk list explicitly. "What do you think is most likely to go wrong?" turns the instinct into a structural contribution rather than ambient doubt.
Romanian culturemeetsprocess-oriented cultures
"They agreed and then worked around the process"
In a culture where official processes have historically been unreliable or counterproductive, the ability to find a path that achieves the result without strictly following the prescribed route is a skill, not a defect. The goal was reached; the method differed from the procedure. The relationship between the rule and its underlying purpose is understood differently here — and that distinction matters.
What helps: Be explicit about which parts of the process are actually non-negotiable versus which are preferred defaults. Romanian colleagues can and will respect hard constraints — they just need to know which ones are real.
Romanian culturemeetsexpressive, high-energy cultures
"I can't tell if they're engaged — there's no visible energy"
Visible enthusiasm and performed engagement are not the Romanian default register. Investment shows up as focus, careful questions, and reliable execution — not energy. The quiet colleague who delivered exactly what was needed by Thursday was probably the most engaged person in the conversation.
What helps: Measure engagement by output and follow-through rather than room energy. Ask directly — "what do you think?" — and take the answer at face value.

Five things about Romania worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your Romanian colleagues are coming from.

1
Romanian is a Romance language — and Romanians know exactly what that means
Romanian is the easternmost Romance language, descended directly from Vulgar Latin brought by Roman colonisation of Dacia in the 2nd century AD. It survived centuries of Slavic, Ottoman, and Hungarian influence without losing its Latin core — a fact that connects Romania culturally to France, Italy, Spain, and Portugal in ways that its geography doesn't obviously suggest. Recognising this, and mentioning it, opens something — it signals that you see Romania as something other than a vaguely Eastern European country.
Conversation opener: Ask a Romanian colleague which other Romance language they find most similar to Romanian. The answer is usually Italians, and they often have a story about being understood in Rome.
2
The Palace of the Parliament is the heaviest building in the world
Ceaușescu's construction project in Bucharest — begun in 1984 and still not entirely finished — is the second largest administrative building in the world by floor area, after the Pentagon, and the heaviest building on earth at approximately 4,098,500 tonnes. To build it, an entire historic district of Bucharest was demolished, displacing around 40,000 people. It now houses the Romanian Parliament and a contemporary art museum, and dominates the city's skyline in a way that is impossible to ignore. Most Romanians have complicated feelings about it — it's too large and too present to pretend doesn't exist.
Conversation opener: Ask a Romanian colleague from Bucharest what it's like to live alongside that building. The answers are rarely simple.
3
Nadia Comaneci scored the first perfect 10 in Olympic gymnastics history
At the 1976 Montreal Olympics, 14-year-old Nadia Comaneci became the first gymnast in Olympic history to receive a perfect score of 10.0 — a result so unprecedented that the scoreboard could only display "1.00" because it wasn't built to show a perfect 10. She scored seven perfect 10s at those Games, won three gold medals, and remains one of the greatest gymnasts of all time. She is still one of the most recognisable Romanians in the world, and asking a Romanian colleague about her is a reliable and warm conversation opener.
Conversation opener: Ask a Romanian colleague what Nadia Comaneci means to them or their family. Almost everyone has something to say.
4
The Danube Delta is one of the largest and best-preserved wetlands in Europe
Where the Danube meets the Black Sea in eastern Romania, it fans out into a vast wetland — the second-largest river delta in Europe, after the Volga — covering over 5,000 square kilometres and home to over 300 species of birds and 45 species of freshwater fish. It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and one of the most biodiverse ecosystems on the continent. It's something Romanians take real pride in — a natural wonder that most of the world has never heard of.
Conversation opener: Ask a Romanian colleague if they've been to the Delta. Those who have usually describe it vividly; those who haven't often say they want to go.
5
The Securitate left a social legacy that lasted well past 1989
The Securitate — Ceaușescu's secret police — had one of the highest ratios of informants to population of any secret service in the Eastern Bloc, with estimates suggesting that one in every 30 Romanians was an informer at some point. The system was designed to make trust between citizens difficult. The archive has been partially opened since 1989, and Romanians have been able to discover whether friends, family members, or colleagues reported on them — a process that has produced revelations still unfolding today. Understanding this is important context for why institutional trust rebuilds slowly, and why personal loyalty — once established — is taken seriously.
Conversation opener: Ask a Romanian colleague over 40 how the country has changed since 1989. The answers tend to be specific, honest, and cover more ground than you'd expect.
Middle East · Arabian Peninsula

Saudi Arabia

The custodian of Islam's two holiest sites, the world's most profitable oil producer, and a country undergoing the most rapid social transformation in the region. Understanding Saudi Arabia in 2025 means holding the traditional framework and the Vision 2030 shift simultaneously — both are real, and neither has finished with the other.

Population 36M Capital: Riyadh Language: Arabic Currency: Saudi Riyal (SAR)
SAUDI ARABIA

Saudi Arabia is changing faster than most outsiders realise, and more slowly than some insiders claim. Understanding the country in 2025 requires holding two things at once: a deeply conservative social and religious tradition that shaped every aspect of professional life for decades, and a top-down transformation project — Vision 2030, driven by Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman — that is deliberately and rapidly dismantling significant parts of that tradition. Both are real. Neither has finished with the other.

The Kingdom was founded in its modern form in 1932, unifying the Arabian Peninsula under Wahhabist Islam and a ruling compact between the Al Saud family and the religious establishment. The discovery of oil in 1938 gave the country a revenue base that made it a central player in global energy markets. The country contains Islam's two holiest sites — Mecca and Medina — which gives it a religious prestige and a set of custodial obligations that no other Muslim-majority country carries.

Vision 2030, announced in 2016, is the most ambitious social and economic transformation programme in the country's modern history. Women can now drive, work across sectors, and attend mixed-gender entertainment. The religious police have been stripped of their arrest powers. The country is actively courting foreign investment and international tourism. The Saudi professional you work with is navigating all of this in real time — and their position on the spectrum from traditional to modernising is something you will not be able to read from the outside without paying attention.

Saudi working culture is built on relationships, hierarchy, and a social intelligence shaped by one of the world's most significant cultural transformations in progress. Here is what you will likely encounter.

Relationships precede transactions — always
Saudi working culture is built on personal connection. Business is conducted between people who know and trust each other, and the investment required to build that trust — shared meals, personal conversation, repeated interaction over time — is not optional groundwork before the real work begins. The relationship is what makes the real work possible. Foreign colleagues who try to move straight to the agenda tend to find the engagement polite but not productive; those who invest in the relationship first tend to find things moving considerably more smoothly.
Hierarchy is fundamental and felt
Saudi professional culture is among the most hierarchical you are likely to encounter in an international business context. Seniority — in age, in rank, in family standing — shapes every dimension of how professional interactions work: who speaks first, whose opinion determines the outcome, how disagreement is expressed and by whom. A foreigner pressing a point in a way that causes a senior Saudi to lose face can end a professional relationship.
The majlis model shapes leadership expectations
The majlis — the open sitting room where a leader traditionally receives visitors and hears concerns — is the deep cultural model for how authority and accessibility should work. A great leader is one who is accessible, who listens, who maintains relationships with people across levels of society. In a professional context, very senior Saudi figures sometimes have a quality of open, warm informality that coexists with their actual authority in ways that can be disorienting if you are reading the informality as flatness.
Vision 2030 has changed the workplace — but unevenly
Mixed-gender working environments, women in senior professional roles, international colleagues across sectors — these are now common in ways that were not true a decade ago. A government ministry, a family-owned traditional business, and the Saudi Aramco headquarters are operating in meaningfully different cultural registers despite all being Saudi workplaces. Reading which context you are in — and not applying the norms of one to another — matters considerably.
Time and pace run differently
Meetings may start late. Conversations may expand well beyond the agenda. A decision expected today may arrive next week. This reflects a culture that prioritises relationship and process over schedule, and where the time spent in the extended conversation is doing real work. Pressing for closure before the relationship and the process support it tends to slow things down rather than speed them up.
Practical tips
→
Invest in the relationship before you need anything from it. The social time is the professional investment.
→
Address seniority carefully. Sheikh for men of standing, titles where they exist — getting this right in early interactions signals competence and respect.
→
Accept hospitality every time. Coffee, dates, food — declining these in a Saudi professional context is a meaningful social signal, and not the one you want to send.
→
Read the room on Vision 2030. Not every Saudi colleague experiences the transformation the same way. Let them set the register rather than arriving with assumptions about whether change is welcome.
→
Never cause a senior person to lose face. Whatever needs to be said, find a way to say it that preserves dignity for everyone in the room.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

The Al Saud family and the state are not clearly separable
Saudi Arabia is a monarchy in which the ruling family occupies a position that goes beyond what most international colleagues are used to thinking of as government. The Al Saud family is very large, and its members are present across business, government, military, and civil society in ways that make the distinction between public and private sector sometimes unclear. Understanding who you are dealing with, and what connections they carry, matters practically as well as socially.
In practice: Before significant meetings or negotiations, understand the relational landscape. Who is in the room, and what does their family name tell you about their standing, is useful context.
Gender norms are shifting — but context still determines everything
The changes of the Vision 2030 era are real and significant: women driving, working across sectors, mixed-gender social and professional spaces. But the pace and extent of these changes vary considerably by region, by family background, by sector, and by individual. Making assumptions in either direction — assuming old restrictions still apply everywhere, or assuming they have been fully lifted — tends to be wrong. Following the lead of your Saudi colleagues and treating questions of gender interaction with ongoing attentiveness is the right approach.
In practice: Observe before you act. Let Saudi colleagues set the register on mixed-gender interaction in each specific context.
Wasta operates here — and more visibly than in most places
Personal connections, family standing, and the ability to call on relationships to navigate systems are central to how things get done in Saudi Arabia. This is not primarily about corruption — it is about a relationship-based system in which who you are connected to, and what respect those connections carry, determines how smoothly things move. The right introduction, from the right person, can change a months-long process into a days-long one.
In practice: Invest in building the right relationships before you need them. Ask trusted contacts who the right person to know is for a given context — and ask for introductions rather than trying to navigate cold.
Ramadan restructures everything
During Ramadan, working hours are reduced by law, the rhythm of the day shifts dramatically, and the social and professional calendar reorganises around iftar — the breaking of the fast at sunset. Attending an iftar invitation from a Saudi colleague is a significant social gesture on their part and warrants a corresponding seriousness on yours. Eating, drinking, or smoking in public during daylight hours is both culturally inappropriate and legally regulated.
In practice: If you are working in Saudi Arabia during Ramadan, treat iftar invitations as you would your most important professional engagements. Showing up matters.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures is not about bad intentions — it is about different defaults. Here is what is often going on underneath.

Saudi culturemeetsfast, task-first cultures
"Decisions take forever and I can never get a straight commitment"
Saudi decision-making reflects a hierarchical, consultative, relationship-respecting process in which the right people need to be in the right conversations before a commitment can be made — and in which commitment without that process tends not to stick. What looks like indecision is often the process being conducted through the channels it needs to move through.
What helps: Map the decision-making landscape before you need a decision. Identify who needs to be on board, invest in those relationships in advance, and build timelines that reflect the real process rather than an optimistic version of it.
Saudi culturemeetsegalitarian cultures
"Senior people are treated completely differently — it feels uncomfortable"
The deference extended to senior figures in Saudi professional culture can read as sycophantic to colleagues from flat-hierarchy backgrounds. What is actually happening is a coherent social logic in which age, standing, and experience carry genuine authority, and in which demonstrating respect for that authority is both a social obligation and a form of real regard. Treating everyone with identical informality, regardless of standing, signals a failure to read the room.
What helps: Match the register the room is operating in. In Saudi professional contexts, erring toward formality and deference with senior figures is always the safer starting point.
Saudi culturemeetsdirect-feedback cultures
"I gave honest feedback and everything went cold"
Face — personal dignity, the avoidance of public embarrassment — is a central value in Saudi professional culture, and it applies with particular force to senior figures. Direct negative feedback delivered in a group setting, or in a way that publicly diminishes someone, causes damage that no amount of substantive correctness repairs. The coldness that follows is a signal that a real and important social norm has been violated.
What helps: Anything critical needs to be delivered privately, with care, framed within genuine respect for the person. The more senior the person receiving it, the more the framing matters relative to the content.
Saudi culturemeetscolleagues with a fixed picture of the country
"I assumed the new Saudi Arabia — turns out it depends entirely on who you are talking to"
Vision 2030 has changed Saudi Arabia significantly and unevenly. The transformation that is real and complete in one context may be partial or absent in another. Arriving with a single fixed picture of what Saudi Arabia is now — whether that picture is the old conservative one or the new modernising one — tends to be wrong about at least half of what you encounter.
What helps: Treat each context and each colleague on its own terms. Observe before you assume. Ask rather than project.
Saudi diasporameetscredential-discounting cultures
"Their background is impressive but I am not sure how it translates internationally"
Saudi universities — King Abdulaziz University, King Fahd University of Petroleum and Minerals, King Abdullah University of Science and Technology — produce graduates in engineering, sciences, and business who compete successfully in international environments. Many Saudi professionals have additionally been educated in the US, UK, or Europe. The assumption that a Saudi credential or career history requires significant discounting is frequently wrong.
What helps: Ask specifically about their training and professional trajectory. The quality tends to be apparent quickly, and the combination of Saudi professional experience with international education is often more substantial than initial assumptions suggest.

Five things about Saudi Arabia worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your Saudi colleagues are coming from.

1
Saudi Aramco is the most profitable company in the history of the world
Saudi Aramco reported net income of $161 billion in 2022, surpassing Apple's record and making it the most profitable company ever measured. It sits on the world's second-largest proven oil reserves and has produced oil at scale since the 1940s. Aramco is not just an economic asset — it is the material foundation of the Saudi state, the source of the wealth that built the infrastructure, the schools, the hospitals, and the welfare system that Saudi citizens have benefited from. Understanding Aramco's centrality — to the Saudi economy, to global energy markets, to the country's sense of itself — is understanding something fundamental about Saudi Arabia.
Conversation opener: Ask a Saudi colleague from an engineering or energy background about their relationship to Aramco — whether they worked there, aspired to, or know people who did. The answer usually reveals something about the company's role in Saudi professional life.
2
Saudi Arabia's custodianship of Mecca and Medina gives it a weight no other country carries
Saudi Arabia is the custodian of Islam's two holiest sites — Mecca and Medina — and for Saudis this is a dimension of national identity that connects directly to their sense of purpose and global significance. The title Custodian of the Two Holy Mosques, used by Saudi kings since 1986, carries a genuine obligation shaping how Saudi leadership understands its role in the world. The most visible expression is the Hajj — the annual pilgrimage drawing 2 to 3 million people in five days, the largest annual gathering of human beings on earth. For the world's 1.8 billion Muslims, Mecca is the most sacred place on earth, and the Saudi custodianship of that access is a geopolitical reality of considerable weight. Treating this dimension lightly, or reducing it to politics, misreads how deeply Saudis feel the responsibility.
Conversation opener: Ask a Saudi colleague what the Hajj season means for their family — whether relatives have made the pilgrimage, what the occasion involves. The personal dimension is usually vivid.
3
Arabic calligraphy reached some of its highest expressions in the service of Islam — and Saudi Arabia sits at the centre of that tradition
The Arabic calligraphic tradition — developed around the writing and decoration of the Quran — is one of the great artistic traditions in history. The mosques of Mecca and Medina contain some of its finest examples. That tradition feeds into a contemporary Saudi art scene growing under the Vision 2030 programme. Asking a Saudi colleague about contemporary Saudi art, or about the calligraphic tradition, tends to open something that most professional interactions do not reach.
Conversation opener: Ask a Saudi colleague whether they follow the contemporary art scene in Riyadh or Jeddah. The answer tends to be more engaged than the question usually expects.
4
The Empty Quarter is the largest continuous sand desert on earth — and Saudis carry it in their identity
The Rub' al Khali — the Empty Quarter — covers approximately 650,000 square kilometres, the largest continuous body of sand on earth. For Bedouin Saudis, the desert carries a cultural memory of navigational skill, endurance, and the hospitality codes that emerged from the necessity of welcoming strangers in a landscape where refusing shelter could mean death. These roots still run through Saudi culture, even in Riyadh high-rises. Asking a Saudi colleague about their family's tribal origins tends to open something personal and vivid.
Conversation opener: Ask a Saudi colleague whether their family has Bedouin roots, or whether they have spent time in the desert. The connection to tribal and desert heritage is often closer than urban professional life suggests.
5
Saudi Arabia has produced a significant contemporary art scene — faster than most people expected
The Diriyah Biennale, launched in 2021, announced Saudi Arabia's arrival as a serious player in the international contemporary art world. The work of Saudi artists — Maha Malluh, Ahmed Mater, and Manal AlDowayan among them — has been shown in major international institutions and addresses Saudi identity, transformation, memory, and modernity with a directness and sophistication that surprises audiences expecting something more decorative. The investment in cultural infrastructure reflects a deliberate effort to build cultural production alongside economic diversification. Asking a Saudi colleague about the contemporary art scene, or about what cultural transformation means to them personally, tends to produce a more complex and interesting answer than the question usually gets.
Conversation opener: Ask a Saudi colleague whether they have been to the Diriyah heritage site, or what they think of the cultural projects being built around it. The restored city of Diriyah — the original Al Saud capital — tends to produce strong feelings.
Southeast Asia · City-state

Singapore

One of the most deliberate national projects in modern history — a city-state that went from expulsion and poverty in 1965 to one of the wealthiest, best-governed places on earth within a generation. The working culture reflects exactly that founding story: performance-oriented, meritocratic, efficient, and carrying a very specific relationship with pressure and achievement.

Population 6M Capital: Singapore Languages: English, Mandarin, Malay, Tamil Currency: Singapore Dollar (SGD)
SINGAPORE

In 1965, Lee Kuan Yew appeared on television and wept as he announced Singapore's separation from Malaysia. The country he was about to lead had no army, no water supply of its own, no natural resources, and no obvious path to viability. Within thirty years it had become one of the wealthiest countries in the world by per capita income. That founding story is not just history — it is the animating logic of Singaporean working culture.

The population is roughly 74% Chinese Singaporean, 13% Malay, and 9% Indian, with Eurasian and other communities making up the rest. These communities have distinct cultural defaults, but they share a Singaporean identity that is real and has its own characteristics: pragmatic, internationally oriented, highly educated, and shaped by a meritocratic national ethos that runs from the school system into the boardroom. One more piece of context: Singaporeans are sharply aware of what people outside Singapore think about them. The caricatures — the fine city, the chewing gum ban, the sterile efficiency — are known, are found mostly tiresome, and tend to close conversations rather than open them.

Singaporean working culture is direct, performance-oriented, and shaped by a meritocratic national ethos that runs from the school system into the boardroom. Here is what you are likely to encounter.

Direct communication — with cultural nuance underneath
Singaporean professional communication, particularly in international and corporate settings, tends toward directness — especially around tasks, expectations, and outcomes. Feedback is generally more explicit than in surrounding Southeast Asian cultures, and ambiguity around deliverables is not tolerated well. That said, the directness has limits around hierarchy and face, particularly in more traditional Chinese Singaporean contexts. Challenging a senior person in a group setting, or delivering feedback in a way that causes public embarrassment, still carries real social cost. The directness is genuine; it operates within relational constraints that are worth knowing about.
Performance and results are the primary currency
Singapore's meritocratic founding principle runs through its working culture in ways that are unusually explicit. Performance matters, credentials matter, and the expectation that good work will be recognised — and that underperformance will be named — is more present here than in many Asian contexts. This produces colleagues who are highly capable, outcome-focused, and generally comfortable being assessed on results. It also produces a particular relationship with pressure and competition that begins in school and does not really let up.
Hierarchy is present but functional
Singaporean organisations are hierarchical, but the hierarchy tends to be more functional and less ceremonial than in many equivalents across Asia. Seniority is respected and decisions flow from the top, but the logic is usually meritocratic rather than purely positional — authority is associated with capability as much as with rank. Juniors will still be unlikely to challenge their managers directly in a group setting, but the distance between levels is generally smaller than in neighbouring countries, and the willingness to engage across levels in informal settings is real.
Efficiency is a value, not just a preference
Time is taken seriously in Singapore. Meetings start on time, agendas are followed, and the expectation that professional interactions will be purposeful and well-prepared is genuine. The cultural low tolerance for inefficiency — a direct product of the national story — means that meandering meetings, vague objectives, and unclear outcomes create real frustration. Showing up prepared, structured, and with a clear purpose signals professional respect in a way that lands distinctly here.
The kiasu dimension
Kiasu — the Hokkien term for "fear of losing" or "afraid to miss out" — carries a particular intensity in a city built on the idea that position must be earned and defended. The competitive instinct runs through education, career, and professional life, and is acknowledged — often with self-deprecating awareness — by Singaporeans themselves. It produces high performers who work hard and prepare thoroughly. It also occasionally produces a guardedness about information, credit, and positioning that is worth being aware of.
Practical tips
→
Be on time and be prepared. Punctuality and preparation are signals of respect here in a way that is more pointed than in most of the region.
→
Be direct about expectations and outcomes. Vagueness frustrates. Clear, specific, and honest — about what you need, what the deadline is, and what good looks like — works considerably better than diplomatic softening.
→
Don't lead with the clichés. Fines, chewing gum, sterile efficiency — Singaporeans have heard all of it, find most of it reductive, and will warm to you faster if you skip it.
→
Respect the meritocratic logic. Credentials, track record, and demonstrated capability carry real weight. Establish yours early, clearly, and without false modesty.
→
Read the ethnic context. Your Chinese Singaporean, Malay Singaporean, and Indian Singaporean colleagues may have meaningfully different cultural defaults beneath the shared Singaporean identity. Pay attention to the individual.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

The rules are real — and that is not entirely a joke
Singapore has a reputation for rules, regulations, and enforcement that its residents are thoroughly aware of. Littering, jaywalking, eating on the MRT, not flushing a public toilet — these carry actual fines, and the social norm around compliance is genuine. This is not timidity or rigid conformity; it is the expression of a national compact that says public order and civic cleanliness are things worth maintaining. Coming in with an ironic attitude toward the rules, or performing a studied indifference to them, reads as both disrespectful and naive. The rules are part of what makes Singapore work, and most Singaporeans know it.
In practice: Follow them. The adjustment is trivial and the signal it sends is not.
Racial harmony is a managed project — and the management is visible
Singapore's multiracial social compact is not the product of spontaneous goodwill. It is the result of deliberate policy: racial quotas in public housing, four official languages, school curricula that teach national cohesion, legislation against racial and religious hatred. Singaporeans are generally aware that this balance is constructed and requires maintenance. Commenting on racial dynamics — even sympathetically, even analytically — is one of the faster ways to create discomfort in a Singaporean professional setting. It is a subject Singaporeans manage carefully among themselves, and that visitors are generally not well-positioned to pronounce on.
In practice: Observe and follow the lead of your colleagues. The dynamics are real; the commentary is not yours to offer.
Education pressure is a lived reality, not a stereotype
The Singaporean education system is among the most rigorous in the world, and the pressure it applies begins young. PSLE scores at age twelve effectively stream children into different secondary school paths. Tuition is a massive industry. Understanding this gives context to the particular relationship with achievement, competition, and performance that Singaporean colleagues tend to carry — and to the parenting conversations that come up frequently with colleagues in their thirties and forties. The stress of the system is real, acknowledged, and the subject of ongoing national debate.
In practice: If a Singaporean colleague with children mentions school pressure, they are not exaggerating. The empathy is warranted.
NS: national service is a shared male experience
All male Singaporean citizens and permanent residents serve two years of national service — in the army, navy, air force, or civil defence. This is a formative shared experience for every Singaporean man, and it shapes the social fabric in ways that are quietly pervasive. Shared NS units and periods of service are a common source of connection between Singaporean male colleagues of different backgrounds. Asking about it with genuine curiosity tends to open a warm and candid conversation — it is a subject most Singaporean men have strong feelings about and rarely get asked about by foreigners.
Conversation opener: Ask a Singaporean male colleague which branch they served in. The stories are usually vivid and the opinions strong.
Singlish is not broken English — it is its own thing
Singlish — the creole blending English with Mandarin, Malay, Tamil, and Hokkien — is the informal spoken language of Singaporean daily life. "Can lah," "chope," "kiasu," "alamak," "shiok," "makan" — it has its own grammar, its own register, and emotional textures that standard English cannot replicate. Singlish is a marker of belonging and shared identity. A foreigner who picks up a word or two and uses it appropriately is received very differently from one who mocks it or imitates it clumsily.
In practice: Learn a few words with genuine interest. Ask what they mean rather than attempting to deploy them prematurely. The curiosity is what lands well.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures is not about bad intentions — it is about different defaults. Here is what is often going on underneath.

Relationship-first culturesmeetsSingaporean culture
"They seem efficient but cold — there's no real relationship there"
Singaporean professional culture can move directly to task and deliverable in a way that feels transactional to colleagues from more relationship-oriented cultures. The warmth is present — it is just expressed differently, often through reliability, competence, and directness rather than through personal disclosure or extended social time. The relationship in Singaporean working culture is often built through doing good work together, not through conversation about personal life before the work starts. Getting to know a Singaporean colleague through shared professional experience tends to work better than expecting the personal layer to arrive first.
What helps: Invest in the informal layer — hawker centre lunches, asking about NS or school — but don't expect it to precede the work. It tends to develop alongside it.
Singaporean culturemeetsdirect cultures (face dimension)
"They say they're fine with the plan and then nothing changes"
The Singaporean directness has real limits around hierarchy and face. In settings where challenging a senior person or naming a problem publicly would cause embarrassment, the more comfortable response is a quiet assent that doesn't reflect the real view. The gap between what is said in a formal setting and what is actually thought is smaller here than in much of Southeast Asia, but it is not zero. Private conversations, one-to-one, will consistently surface more than the formal meeting does.
What helps: Create private space for the real check-in. "Is there anything about this that concerns you — I want to make sure we're not missing something" is a more reliable route to the honest view than a group sign-off.
Relationship-first or high-context culturesmeetsSingaporean culture
"The feedback is brutal — they just say what's wrong without any framing"
Singaporean professional communication, especially around performance and task, can feel blunt to colleagues from cultures where feedback is more carefully wrapped. The directness is the expression of a meritocratic logic that says honest assessment is more respectful than diplomatic softening. What sounds harsh from the outside often sounds like taking you seriously from the inside. The adjustment usually goes both ways: Singaporean colleagues in international teams often moderate their directness once they understand the context; colleagues from more indirect cultures often find that Singaporean feedback, once calibrated for, is actually easier to act on.
What helps: Name the cultural gap early. "I'm used to more framing around critical feedback — can we find a register that works for both of us?" is a conversation most Singaporean colleagues will engage with directly and productively.
Relationship-based culturesmeetsSingaporean culture
"Everything has to be by the book — there's no flexibility"
Singaporean professional culture tends to follow process, procedure, and agreed frameworks with a consistency that can frustrate colleagues who expect relational factors — who you know, what the relationship warrants — to create flexibility. The process-orientation is not bureaucratic indifference; it is a cultural expression of fairness. If the rule applies to everyone, it cannot be manipulated by the well-connected. Understanding the logic — rather than trying to find the workaround — tends to be the more productive approach.
What helps: Work within the system rather than around it. If an exception is genuinely warranted, make the case on its merits through the right channel — not through the relationship.

Five things about Singapore worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your Singaporean colleagues are coming from.

1
The founding story is extraordinary — and Singaporeans know it
Lee Kuan Yew wept on television in 1965. The country he was about to lead had no army, no water supply, and no obvious path to viability. Within thirty years it had become one of the wealthiest countries in the world. The housing policy, the education investment, the port, the corruption crackdown, the careful management of ethnic relations — the speed and deliberateness of the transformation is one of the most studied governance stories of the 20th century. Most Singaporeans carry a version of this founding story with them, and it explains a great deal about the national character that followed.
Conversation opener: Ask a Singaporean colleague over 50 what Singapore was like when they were growing up. The before-and-after contrast is usually vivid and sometimes staggering.
2
The hawker centre is the great social equaliser
Singapore's hawker centres — open-air food courts with dozens of stalls serving Chinese, Malay, Indian, and fusion dishes — are one of the more remarkable social institutions in Asia. Eating there is not a budget choice; it is what everyone does, from construction workers to bankers. The UNESCO inscription of hawker culture on its Intangible Cultural Heritage list in 2020 was received with real national pride. Asking a Singaporean colleague where to find the best chicken rice, laksa, or char kway teow is one of the more reliable conversation openers available — the opinions are strong and the recommendations are specific.
Conversation opener: Ask for the definitive hawker centre recommendation. Be prepared for a passionate and very specific answer.
3
The language situation is more complex than it appears
Singapore has four official languages — English, Mandarin, Malay, and Tamil — but the actual linguistic reality is more layered. Most Singaporeans speak English as their primary working language; Mandarin was actively promoted from the 1980s as a unifying language for Chinese Singaporeans; Singlish runs underneath everything as the informal mother tongue of daily life. The linguistic history of Singapore — including what was lost when dialect communities were encouraged to converge on Mandarin — is a subject of ongoing quiet discussion among Singaporeans themselves.
Conversation opener: Ask an older Singaporean colleague what their first language was growing up. The answer is often different from what you'd expect, and the story behind it usually says something real about the country.
4
Singapore produces writers and artists who are almost entirely unknown outside the region — and very good
Singaporean literature in English has produced writers of real quality — Kevin Kwan (Crazy Rich Asians), Alfian Sa'at, Cyril Wong — who are read seriously within Singapore and largely invisible outside it. The visual arts, theatre, and independent film scenes are more active than Singapore's international image as a financial hub suggests. Asking a Singaporean colleague about local arts or literature tends to open a conversation that neither of you expected to have.
Conversation opener: Ask a Singaporean colleague what Singaporean book, film, or artist they think deserves more international attention. Strong opinions are reliably available.
5
The relationship with Malaysia is the one you have with the sibling you were separated from
Singapore and Malaysia share history, geography, food culture, and hundreds of thousands of people who cross the causeway daily. They also share a complicated post-separation relationship — shaped by water agreements, economic competition, and the particular intimacy of people who grew up together and then had to negotiate being different countries. The jokes they make about each other carry the specific texture of a family argument. The relationship is close, loaded, and fundamentally affectionate in its friction.
Conversation opener: Ask a Singaporean colleague whether they go across the causeway often, and what for. The answer is usually revealing about the relationship.
Southern Africa · 11 official languages

South Africa

Thirty years out of apartheid — long enough for a generation to have grown up after it, not nearly long enough for its consequences to have worked themselves out. A country of profound complexity, extraordinary resilience, and a sociability that surprises almost everyone encountering it for the first time.

Population 60M Capital: Pretoria / Cape Town / Bloemfontein Languages: 11 official including Zulu, Xhosa, Afrikaans, English Currency: Rand (ZAR)
SOUTH AFRICA

South Africa is 30 years out of apartheid. That's long enough for a generation to have grown up after it, and not nearly long enough for its consequences to have worked themselves out. Understanding South African working culture without that framing is like trying to understand Polish working culture without knowing about the partitions — you can do it, but you'll keep misreading things.

The country has eleven official languages and contains within it several fairly distinct cultural worlds: black South African cultures with their own distinctions between Zulu, Xhosa, Sotho, Tswana, Venda, and others; white South African cultures, divided between Afrikaner and English-speaking; Indian South African communities concentrated largely in KwaZulu-Natal; and Coloured communities, predominantly in the Western Cape, with their own distinct history and culture. These communities live alongside each other, work alongside each other, and remain in many respects culturally separate in ways that are not always visible to outsiders.

What cuts across much of this is a particular quality of resilience — a culture that has repeatedly had to absorb enormous change and find ways to function inside it — and a sociability and warmth that South Africans bring to personal interaction with a frequency that surprises many people encountering the country for the first time.

South African working culture is shaped by history, diversity, and a resilience built in demanding conditions. Here's what you'll likely encounter.

Race is present, even when nobody's naming it
South African workplaces carry the legacy of apartheid in their demographics, their power structures, and in the unspoken dynamics between colleagues. Who holds which roles, who defers to whom, who gets heard in a meeting and who doesn't — these patterns often reflect histories that predate the organisation. Acknowledging these dynamics honestly tends to make the room more comfortable, not less.
Ubuntu shapes how collaboration actually works
Umuntu ngumuntu ngabantu — a person is a person through other people. The Ubuntu philosophy runs through much of black South African professional culture. Decision-making that consults broadly, credit that's shared rather than claimed, concern for a colleague who's struggling — these reflect a different model of what the individual is in relation to the group. Working with it rather than around it produces better outcomes.
Directness varies significantly by background
There is no single South African communication style. Afrikaner professional culture tends toward directness — plain speaking, getting to the point. English-speaking white South African culture tends to be somewhat more diplomatically coded, closer to British norms. Black South African professional culture varies significantly by region and language group but often places high value on relational warmth and reading context. Knowing which register you're in, and being attentive enough to read it, matters more than having a single approach.
Hierarchy coexists with warmth
In many South African professional contexts — particularly those with roots in black South African cultural norms — seniority carries real weight. Older colleagues and senior figures expect appropriate deference. This coexists with genuine warmth and informality; the two are not in conflict. A senior figure might share a meal with everyone, make jokes, call people by nickname — and still expect to be addressed respectfully and have their decisions treated as decisions.
The braai is serious social infrastructure
The braai — the South African barbecue — is one of the primary social rituals through which relationships are built and maintained across communities and cultures. Being invited to someone's braai is a meaningful gesture. Showing up, staying, and participating — rather than treating it as an obligation to discharge and leave — signals genuine interest in the relationship.
Practical tips
→
Don't treat race as invisible. You don't have to perform discomfort about it, but pretending the dynamics aren't present reads as either naïve or evasive to most South Africans.
→
Learn at least the greeting in the home language of your colleagues. Sawubona (Zulu), Molo (Xhosa), Hoe gaan dit (Afrikaans) — the effort is disproportionately noticed.
→
Don't assume English fluency means cultural alignment. Most South African professionals are fluent in English and navigating multiple cultural frames simultaneously.
→
Accept the braai invitation. And stay for a proper portion of it.
→
Read the hierarchy correctly. Starting too casual with a senior figure is harder to recover from than starting too formal and relaxing when invited.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out — things that seem obvious from the inside and invisible from the outside. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

"Previously disadvantaged" is a live category, not a bureaucratic one
Broad-Based Black Economic Empowerment (B-BBEE) policy shapes hiring, procurement, and corporate structure in South African business life. For many South Africans, this is a necessary and legitimate attempt to correct structural exclusion that persisted for decades. For others, it is a source of frustration and resentment. Wherever a colleague sits on this, it is likely to be a considered position shaped by real experience. Outsiders who wander into this conversation without having thought about it carefully tend to offend someone immediately or reveal that they don't really understand the country.
In practice: Listen before you speak on this one. Ask questions with genuine curiosity rather than offering comparisons or verdicts.
The concept of face operates — and the private conversation matters
Avoiding public embarrassment — for yourself and for others — is a value across much of South African professional culture, and particularly in contexts shaped by black South African norms. Contradicting someone senior in a group setting, or delivering feedback in a way that humiliates, creates damage that the substance of the point doesn't justify.
In practice: If a South African colleague pulls you aside after a meeting, take it seriously. That's where the real conversation often lives.
Humour is a release valve — and it runs on edge
South African humour tends to be dark, self-aware, and willing to go to places that make outsiders uncomfortable. Apartheid, current dysfunction, load-shedding, the general improbability of daily life — all of it is fair game, and the ability to laugh at it is partly how South Africans manage living inside it. Trevor Noah is a useful cultural reference point: the tone, the willingness to find absurdity in painful material, the sharp social observation — these are not exceptional qualities in a South African context, they're fairly standard.
In practice: You don't have to match the humour. But not flinching at it, and showing you understand what it's doing, signals that you get the culture.
Load-shedding is not a punchline
Eskom's rolling power blackouts — which for extended periods in the early 2020s ran to eight or more hours a day — reshaped how South African businesses operate. Generators, inverters, solar panels, carefully timed work schedules — the adaptation has been extensive and costly. For many South Africans, this represents a serious, ongoing failure of the state, and they carry real frustration about it.
