Someone referencing load-shedding in passing is usually describing a genuine daily constraint, not making small talk.
In practice: Acknowledge it as the real operational challenge it is. Asking how a colleague manages it practically tends to open something candid.
Crime is real and shapes daily life
South Africa has one of the highest murder rates in the world and significant rates of violent crime more broadly. This shapes where South Africans live, how they move, what they drive, and what they consider normal precaution. Outsiders who express surprise at security arrangements, gated communities, or the routines South Africans have built around personal safety are revealing that they don't yet understand what daily life actually involves.
In practice: Ask about it with genuine curiosity rather than alarm, and most South Africans will give you a frank and considered account.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures isn't about bad intentions — it's about different defaults. Here's what's often going on underneath.

South African culturemeetscultures unfamiliar with its complexity
"They seem guarded — I can't get a read on them"
South African professionals — particularly black South Africans in majority-white or international workplaces — often operate in environments where their cultural default is not the dominant one. The guardedness some outsiders read is frequently the calibration happening in real time: working out which set of norms is operating, whether the space is safe for honesty, how much of themselves to bring. It tends to ease significantly once there's a demonstrated track record of reliability and real interest.
What helps: Show up consistently. Follow through. Ask real questions about people as people. The calibration resolves faster when the answer to "can I trust this person?" becomes clear.
South African culturemeetscultures uncomfortable with directness
"They were very blunt — it felt aggressive"
Afrikaner professional directness in particular can land hard on colleagues from more diplomatically coded cultures. The intention is usually clarity and efficiency, not hostility — but the gap between intent and impact is real. At the same time, South Africans across backgrounds have generally navigated environments that required them to be straight about hard things, and they tend to respect the same in return.
What helps: Name the style difference rather than absorbing the discomfort. "I tend to need a bit more framing before the conclusion" is a useful piece of self-knowledge to share.
South African culturemeetscultures with little context for apartheid
"Why does race keep coming up — we're just trying to get the work done"
Colleagues from countries without South Africa's specific history sometimes find the salience of race in South African workplaces surprising, and occasionally frame this as South Africans being "stuck in the past." This tends to land very badly. The consequences of apartheid are structural and ongoing — in wealth distribution, in organisational demographics, in access to education and networks. They are not a mindset that people have chosen to maintain.
What helps: Ask rather than assert. "Help me understand how this works" produces much better outcomes than "why does this still matter."
South African culturemeetsprocess-driven cultures
"They always seem to have a workaround — why not just follow the system?"
South Africans have lived through extended periods in which the official system was either hostile, unreliable, or both. The instinct to route around it — to find the person who can actually make something happen, rather than the process that theoretically should — is a rational response to a real history. It produces creativity and network-building that formal systems don't, and it transfers well in international contexts when the formal system lets people down.
What helps: Acknowledge the constraint rather than defending the system. Working alongside the instinct rather than against it tends to produce better results.
South African diasporameetscredential-unfamiliar workplaces
"Their experience is from Africa — I'm not sure how it translates"
South Africa has well-regarded universities — UCT, Wits, Stellenbosch, among others — a sophisticated financial sector, a serious legal tradition, and a complex business environment that has produced professionals with capabilities that map directly onto international contexts. The assumption that "African experience" needs translating or discounting is, in the case of South African professionals, frequently simply wrong.
What helps: Ask specifically about what they've built and what they've navigated. The complexity of the environments many South African professionals have operated in is usually considerably greater than the environments their international counterparts have encountered.

Five things about South Africa worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your South African colleagues are coming from.

1
South Africa has the most progressive Constitution in the world — and it was written in two years
The post-apartheid Constitution, adopted in 1996, is widely considered the most comprehensive in the world — enshrining rights to housing, healthcare, food, water, and social security, and explicitly protecting against discrimination on grounds including sexual orientation at a time when almost no other constitution did. It was negotiated between parties who had been at war with each other, under the shadow of potential civil conflict, in roughly two years. The gap between the Constitution and the lived reality of many South Africans is real and persistent, but the document itself is a remarkable achievement — and most South Africans know it.
Conversation opener: Ask a South African colleague what they think the Constitution got right — and what the gap between it and reality looks like from where they stand.
2
South African literature contains some of the 20th century's finest writers — most of whom were writing under censorship
Nadine Gordimer won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1991. J.M. Coetzee won it in 2003 — the only writer to have won the Booker Prize twice. Alan Paton's Cry, the Beloved Country (1948) was one of the first works to bring apartheid's human reality to international attention. Athol Fugard's plays were being written and performed under a government actively trying to suppress what they were saying. Asking a South African colleague what they read growing up tends to open something interesting.
Read list: Cry, the Beloved Country · Disgrace (Coetzee) · July's People (Gordimer) · Sizwe Banzi is Dead (Fugard)
3
The Springboks went from apartheid symbol to three-time World Cup winners — and the arc spans three decades
For most of their history, the Springboks were a symbol of white South African nationalism — the team the townships did not claim. The 1995 Rugby World Cup changed that: Nelson Mandela presenting the trophy to captain Francois Pienaar, wearing Springbok jersey number 6, was an act of political theatre so precise it changed the meaning of a symbol in real time. South Africans across communities remember where they were. The arc continued in 2019, when the World Cup was won by a team captained by Siya Kolisi — the first black Springbok captain — whose journey from poverty in the Eastern Cape to lifting the trophy in Japan is one of the more remarkable stories in world sport. The 2023 World Cup followed. Asking a South African colleague what the Springboks mean today versus what they meant before 1994 usually produces something worth hearing.
Conversation opener: Ask a South African over 40 where they watched the 1995 final. The answer is usually specific and never casual.
4
South African music runs much deeper than what's exported
Miriam Makeba — Mama Africa — took South African music to the world while living in exile, banned from returning to her own country. Hugh Masekela's trumpet carried political weight that the apartheid government found threatening enough to exile him too. Ladysmith Black Mambazo brought isicathamiya choral music to a global audience through Paul Simon's Graceland. More recently, amapiano — a distinctly South African sound developed in the townships, blending house music with jazz-inflected basslines — has become one of the most influential sounds in global dance music over the past decade. Asking a South African colleague what they're listening to tends to reveal a musical culture considerably richer than the international coverage suggests.
Conversation opener: Ask which South African artist they think the world is sleeping on. The answer is rarely Ladysmith Black Mambazo.
5
South Africa hosts the oldest ultramarathon in the world — and ordinary people run it
The Comrades Marathon — 89 kilometres between Durban and Pietermaritzburg, alternating direction each year — was first run in 1921. Around 25,000 people run it annually. It is not a professional event populated by elite athletes; it is a mass participation race in which ordinary South Africans — teachers, accountants, nurses — train for months to cover nearly 90 kilometres on foot in under twelve hours. The culture around it tells you something real about South African endurance, stubbornness, and the particular satisfaction of doing something hard. Ask a South African colleague if they've run Comrades. Many have, or know someone who has, and the answer tends to come with a story.
Conversation opener: Ask a South African colleague about Comrades — whether they've run it, whether they'd want to, what it represents. The answers tend to be revealing.
Southern Europe · EU member

Spain

A country that runs on relationships, passion, and a deep resistance to the idea that efficiency should come at the cost of living well. Spanish working culture is warmer, louder, and more flexible than northern European norms — and once you understand what's actually driving it, it makes complete sense.

Population 47M Capital: Madrid Languages: Spanish + regional Currency: Euro (EUR)
ESPAÑA

The Spanish workplace is relationship-first, and almost everything flows from that: decisions, trust, timelines, and the willingness to go the extra mile for someone. Get the relationship right and the work follows. Skip the relationship and build the most efficient process in the world — and it will quietly not function. Spain operates on different priorities from northern European or American defaults — not lesser ones, just different ones — and understanding that distinction is the key to working well here.

Time works differently here too. The famous late lunch, the long evening, the meeting that starts fifteen minutes after it was supposed to — these aren't bugs, they're features of a culture that treats rigidity about schedules as mildly antisocial. Spain also contains multitudes: Catalonia, the Basque Country, Galicia, and Andalucía all have distinct cultures, languages, and identities that matter deeply to the people who live there. Treating Spain as a single uniform culture will lose you points quickly.

The post-Franco generation rebuilt a democracy, an economy, and a cultural identity in about thirty years. The country that produced Picasso, Lorca, Almodóvar, and Ferran Adrià is not a country short on creative ambition.

What to expect when working with Spanish colleagues — the patterns, defaults, and unspoken rules that shape how things actually get done.

Relationships before business — and that's not a preamble, it's the point
In Spain, the personal relationship is not a warm-up before the real meeting begins — it is the meeting. Time spent on coffee, on asking about family, on finding out who someone actually is, is not wasted time. It's the foundation on which every subsequent work interaction will be built. Colleagues who skip this and go straight to agenda items come across as cold, transactional, and hard to trust.
Flexibility with time is a feature, not a flaw
Meetings rarely start on the dot. Deadlines are often understood as approximate targets rather than hard commitments, particularly for internal work. The priority is the quality of what's happening, not its punctual completion. The counterpart is that Spanish colleagues will often work later, stay longer, and put in extra effort when the relationship and the cause merit it.
Communication is expressive and direct — at the same time
Spanish communication style is warm, demonstrative, and not especially shy about disagreement. Debate is normal and healthy; a lively argument in a meeting isn't a crisis — it's people being engaged. Silence in a Spanish meeting is often the worse sign. That said, criticism of someone's work is usually delivered with warmth and framed constructively — the relationship cushions the directness.
Hierarchy exists — and so does informality
Spanish organisations often have clear seniority structures, and decisions can sit with senior people. But the day-to-day register is often surprisingly informal — first names, open doors, lunch together across levels. The hierarchy shapes who decides; the warmth shapes how everyone interacts getting there. Both things coexist without much tension.
Lunch is serious
The long lunch is not a cultural relic — it's still very much alive, especially outside the most internationalised sectors. Lunch is where relationships are built, business is discussed informally, and trust is cemented. Being the person who eats a sad sandwich at their desk and emails instead is a missed opportunity in a culture where the table is a genuine place of work.
Practical tips
→
Invest in the personal before the professional. Ask about the weekend, ask about family — genuinely. It's not small talk in Spain; it's the actual relationship.
→
Build buffer into timelines. Not because Spanish colleagues are unreliable, but because flexibility is mutual and expected to go both ways.
→
Say yes to the lunch. The long lunch is an institution. Turning it down routinely signals that you don't value the relationship.
→
Learn a few words of the local language. In Catalonia or the Basque Country especially, even a small effort in the regional language is noticed and appreciated.
→
Don't schedule things for August. Spain largely shuts down. Plan around it.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out — things that seem obvious from the inside and invisible from the outside. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

Regional identity is not a detail
Calling a Catalan person simply "Spanish" without acknowledgement is an error with real social weight. The Basque Country, Catalonia, Galicia — these are not just administrative regions, they are cultures with their own languages, histories, and in some cases active independence movements. Getting this wrong is a quick way to signal that you haven't done your homework. Getting it right — showing awareness, curiosity — lands disproportionately well.
In practice: Ask where someone is from specifically. "Are you from Barcelona — or somewhere else in Catalonia?" signals awareness. Referring to everything as "Spain" flattens something people care about deeply.
Food is identity
Spanish food culture runs deep. Paella is from Valencia — not a generic Spanish dish. Each region has its own cuisine, its own wines, its own ways of doing things at the table that carry genuine pride. Being an enthusiastic and curious eater is a social asset in Spain. Showing indifference to what's been prepared or recommended will be noticed.
In practice: Show interest in what you're eating. Ask what it is, where it's from, how it's made. Asking what something is, where it's from, how it's made is the right instinct.
The day runs later than you expect
Dinner at 10pm is normal. Meetings at 7pm are not unusual. The working day often starts later and ends later than northern European norms, and evening social life begins where most northern Europeans are already in bed. Expecting Spanish colleagues to match a rigidly 9-to-5 schedule tends to create friction in both directions.
In practice: If you're scheduling cross-border calls, mid-morning works well for both sides. Early morning calls will be met with quiet suffering.
Noise is normal
Spain is a loud country — and that's not a complaint, it's a feature. Bars are loud, families are loud, conversations overlap. In a professional context, animated discussion is normal and expected. Interpreting volume or interruption as aggression is a mistake. It usually means the opposite — people are engaged and comfortable.
In practice: Don't wait for a gap in the conversation before speaking — there often won't be one. Jumping in is normal and expected.
Fútbol is not optional small talk
Football in Spain is not a hobby — it's a social institution and, in many cases, a matter of tribal identity. Real Madrid and FC Barcelona carry political and regional weight that goes well beyond sport. Knowing which city you're in and having even a passing familiarity with the local club is real social currency.
In practice: Asking about the match — with real interest, not performatively — opens doors. Knowing that Barça vs. Real is not just a football match but a proxy for Catalonia vs. Castile makes you a more interesting person to talk to.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures isn't about bad intentions — it's about different defaults. Here's what's often going on underneath.

Spanish culturemeetstime-precise cultures
"They're never on time and deadlines don't mean anything"
Spain operates on polychronic time — relationships and quality of engagement take precedence over the clock. A meeting that starts late because someone was finishing an important conversation isn't disrespectful; it's prioritising correctly by local values. What looks like unreliability from outside often reflects a different hierarchy of what matters.
What helps: Be explicit about which deadlines are hard. "This one has an external dependency — I need it by Thursday end of day" lands differently than a soft internal target.
Spanish culturemeetsreserved professional cultures
"They're too emotional — it's hard to have a rational conversation"
Expressiveness, raised voices, and visible passion in a meeting are not signs that the conversation has gone wrong — they're signs that people are invested. Spanish professional culture is comfortable with emotion in a way that northern European norms are not. A meeting that looks like an argument to a Finnish observer might just be a normal Thursday to everyone else in the room.
What helps: Don't interpret passion as conflict. If you're uncertain, watch whether participants seem distressed or energised afterwards — that's the real signal.
Spanish culturemeetslow-context, direct cultures
"I can't get a straight answer — everything is maybe"
Vagueness in Spanish communication sometimes reflects real flexibility, sometimes a reluctance to disappoint, and sometimes a sense that committing firmly is presumptuous until the relationship is more established. "We'll see" and "probably" can mean very different things depending on the context and the relationship.
What helps: Ask for specifics gently and directly — "just so I can plan — are we thinking Thursday or Friday?" A concrete question usually gets a concrete answer.
Spanish culturemeetstask-focused, accountability cultures
"They say yes and then do something different"
"Yes" in Spain can mean agreement, acknowledgement, or simply the socially correct response in a meeting. Like many relationship-first cultures, the unwillingness to disappoint someone face-to-face can produce affirmative answers that don't necessarily survive the walk back to the desk. Commitment here means genuine intent in the moment; whether the conditions for delivery are in place is a separate question.
What helps: Follow up warmly in writing. "Great — just confirming we're aligned on X" makes the commitment visible without creating confrontation.

Five things about Spain worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your Spanish colleagues are coming from.

1
Spain had a civil war in living memory — and the silence around it is only now lifting
The Spanish Civil War (1936–39) and Francisco Franco's dictatorship (1939–75) left wounds that were officially suppressed for decades under a post-transition "pact of forgetting." It's only in the 2000s and 2010s that Spain has begun seriously excavating this history — identifying mass graves, debating memory laws, relitigating what happened. For many families, the war and the dictatorship are not history — they're the grandparents' lives. This context shapes a particular sensitivity around political division, authority, and collective memory.
Conversation opener: Ask a Spanish colleague over 50 what their family's experience of the Franco era was. The answers are often surprising and always revealing.
2
The rhythm of Spanish life was designed by the climate
The late dinner, the long lunch, the evening paseo, the after-work drinks that go until midnight — these aren't arbitrary cultural choices, they're adaptations to a climate where the middle of the day is too hot to be outside or productive for much of the year. Understanding the rhythm of Spanish life as a rational response to the Mediterranean climate makes it immediately less mysterious. The social life moves to the edges of the day because that's when the day is actually pleasant.
Conversation opener: Ask a Spanish colleague what they think of northern European working hours. The response is usually equal parts bafflement and pity.
3
Spanish art and cinema are more subversive than the sunny stereotype suggests
Buñuel, Dalí, Picasso, Goya — Spain's artistic tradition runs toward the strange, the dark, and the political. Pedro Almodóvar's films deal with trauma, identity, and desire in ways that have no equivalent in most national cinemas. The culture that produced the surrealists and Goya's Black Paintings is not a simple one. The lightness and warmth of everyday Spanish life coexists with a deep artistic tradition of confronting difficult things head-on.
Watch list: All About My Mother (Almodóvar, 1999) · The Others (Amenábar, 2001) · Pan's Labyrinth (del Toro, 2006)
4
The economic crisis of 2008–2014 left a generational mark
Spain's unemployment hit 27% in 2013 — and youth unemployment peaked above 55%. An entire generation of highly educated Spaniards left for Germany, the UK, and Latin America. The people who stayed often built remarkable things in adversity — a startup ecosystem, new cultural institutions, a generation of chefs and designers and engineers who built careers on ingenuity rather than inheritance. Understanding this shapes how you read the Spanish work ethic: for many, it was forged in a period when nothing was guaranteed.
Conversation opener: Ask a Spanish colleague in their 30s or 40s about their experience of the crisis. Almost everyone has a story.
5
La Roja and the golden decade — sport as national healing
Between 2008 and 2012, the Spanish national football team won the European Championship, the World Cup, and the European Championship again — an unprecedented treble. For a country still processing economic collapse and regional tension, it was a rare moment of uncomplicated national pride. Real Madrid and Barcelona's rivalry maps onto deeper political divisions — Castilian Spain vs. Catalan identity — making football in Spain more politically loaded than almost anywhere else.
Conversation opener: Ask any Spanish colleague over 30 where they watched the 2010 World Cup final. Almost everyone remembers exactly where they were.
Northern Europe · EU member

Sweden

At peace since 1814 — and a working culture built on the confidence that produces.

Population 10.5M Capital: Stockholm Language: Swedish EU member since 1995 Currency: Krona (SEK)
SVERIGE

Sweden has been at peace since 1814 — one of the longest uninterrupted periods of non-belligerence of any European country. That fact is not incidental. While much of Europe spent the 19th and 20th centuries being reshaped by wars, occupations, and revolutions, Sweden had the unusual luxury of building its institutions largely without interruption. The result is a country with deep confidence in its systems: the rule of law, the welfare state, the labour market model, and the particular relationship between individual rights and collective responsibility that defines Scandinavian social democracy.

The Swedish model — developed across the 20th century through negotiation between employers, unions, and the state rather than through conflict — produced a working culture shaped by consensus, egalitarianism, and a strong belief that good decisions are made through proper process rather than by whoever has the most authority in the room. These aren't just workplace norms; they're the expression of a broader social contract that Swedes have thought carefully about and tend to take seriously.

That said, the culture is considerably more layered than the stereotype of reserved, conflict-averse consensus-seekers suggests. Sweden has produced globally competitive companies, sharp designers, and a popular music export disproportionate to its population of ten million. The flatness is real; so is the ambition and the edge beneath it.

Swedish working culture is shaped by a social contract as much as by management theory. Here's what you'll likely encounter.

Lagom and the culture of calibration
The Swedish concept of lagom — roughly "just the right amount," neither too much nor too little — shapes professional behaviour in ways that are easy to miss. Modesty, measured expression, and the avoidance of standing out are not false humility. They're a coherent value system in which the collective register matters and individual performance of superiority is considered mildly uncomfortable for everyone. This doesn't mean ambition is absent — it means it tends to be expressed through results rather than declaration.
Consensus is the actual process, not a preamble to it
Decisions in Swedish organisations tend to be made through a structured process of broad consultation — förankring — in which key stakeholders are brought along before any formal decision is made. This takes time. Walking into a meeting expecting to make a case and leave with a decision often doesn't work, because the meeting is rarely where the decision is made. The decision is the outcome of a process that ran through conversations and informal alignment well before anyone sat down formally.
Hierarchy is flat — and that's not a pose
Swedish organisations are genuinely flat in ways that surprise many international colleagues. Senior people are on first-name terms with everyone, challenge from junior colleagues is expected and welcomed, and the org chart rarely reflects the actual distribution of influence. The person with the most authority is often the last to speak — and will expect others to have contributed substantively before they do.
Silence is not discomfort — it's thinking
Swedish professional culture is comfortable with silence in meetings in a way that colleagues from more conversationally dense cultures can find disconcerting. A pause after a proposal is not a sign that something has gone wrong — it's a sign that people are considering it. Rushing to fill the silence tends to be counterproductive. Let the room think.
Punctuality and preparation are baseline signals
Sweden runs on linear time. Meetings start when they're supposed to. Coming unprepared, or arriving late without advance notice, signals a disrespect for other people's time that is hard to recover from quickly. Both are read as signals about how seriously you take the work and the people you're working with.
Practical tips
→
Don't self-promote loudly. Results speak; declarations about them tend to make the room uncomfortable. Let the work be visible, not the announcement of it.
→
Do the consultation before the meeting. If a decision matters, speak to the key people informally first. Förankring — making sure people are aligned before you sit down together — is how things actually move.
→
Be on time and be prepared. Both are baseline professional signals, not extras.
→
Sit with the silence. Don't interpret quiet as opposition or confusion. Give people time to think before asking for a reaction.
→
Push back substantively. Swedes expect colleagues to challenge ideas on the merits. Polite non-engagement is less useful than a well-reasoned objection.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out — things that seem obvious from the inside and invisible from the outside. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

Jantelagen — the norm against standing out
Jantelagen (the Law of Jante) named something already deeply recognisable across Scandinavia: the social norm that says you should not consider yourself better than others, or act as though you are. It's not a rule anyone follows consciously, but it shapes the room. Visible self-promotion, talking yourself up, or claiming credit loudly produces a social discomfort in Sweden that is hard to describe but impossible to miss once you've felt it.
In practice: When your work goes well, frame it as a team outcome. Acknowledge what others contributed. Acknowledging what others contributed and avoiding self-promotion is the correct register here.
Fika is not a coffee break — it's social infrastructure
Fika — the Swedish practice of taking a break together with coffee and something to eat — happens at regular times during the working day and is not optional in the way a casual coffee invitation might be elsewhere. It is the primary social infrastructure of the Swedish workplace: where relationships are maintained, where informal information travels, where the real temperature of the team is taken. Consistently skipping fika signals that you're not really part of the group, even if your work output is excellent.
In practice: Show up for fika. You don't have to talk much — presence is what matters.
Work-life balance is a structural value, not a personal preference
Swedish working culture draws a clear line between work time and personal time, and the expectation runs in both directions. Working conspicuously long hours is not a sign of dedication — it can read as poor planning or an implicit critique of colleagues who work normal hours. Emailing outside working hours and expecting a response is a cultural mismatch. Parental leave — taken by both parents — is normal, expected, and not a career impediment.
In practice: Respect the boundary. Don't send messages expecting replies in the evening. Don't signal virtue through visible overwork.
Directness about tasks, indirectness about conflict
Swedish professional communication tends to be clear and direct about tasks, information, and expectations. But when it comes to interpersonal conflict or difficult personal feedback, the instinct is considerably more indirect — problems are softened, unpleasant messages are carefully wrapped, and direct confrontation is avoided where possible. The same culture that is straightforward about work can be surprisingly oblique about friction between people. Learning to read the diplomatic register is a separate skill from understanding the work register.
In practice: If something feels off in a relationship, it probably won't be said directly. Ask gently and privately — "is there anything that would be useful to talk through?" — and take the answer seriously.
Nature is a serious part of life
Sweden has allemansrätten — the right of public access, meaning anyone can walk, camp, or forage on almost any land. Swedes take this seriously. Time outdoors — skiing, hiking, picking berries, going to a summer cottage — is not recreational in a casual sense; it is restorative in a way that is culturally fundamental. Asking a Swedish colleague about their summer house or their outdoor life opens a dimension of conversation that work talk rarely reaches.
In practice: Ask about the summer cottage or the winter skiing. Almost everyone has somewhere specific they go, and the attachment is usually real and detailed.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures isn't about bad intentions — it's about different defaults. Here's what's often going on underneath.

Swedish culturemeetsdecisive, low-process cultures
"Nothing ever gets decided — we just keep consulting"
The consultation process is the decision process. Skipping it doesn't produce faster decisions — it produces decisions that don't stick, because the people who weren't consulted don't feel ownership of the outcome. The time spent on förankring is not delay; it's how Swedish organisations build the implementation commitment that makes decisions durable.
What helps: Map the stakeholders before the formal decision point and speak to them individually. By the time the meeting happens, the decision should already be made.
Swedish culturemeetscultures where meetings produce commitments
"They agreed in the meeting and then nothing changed"
Polite engagement in a meeting is not the same as commitment to action. Swedish professional culture can produce a meeting tone that is constructive and collaborative without constituting a firm agreement to do anything specific. Follow up in writing with clear owners and deadlines. That step turns conversation into commitment in a way the meeting itself often doesn't.
What helps: End every substantive meeting with an explicit summary of who is doing what by when. Frame it as good practice, not distrust.
Swedish culturemeetsexpressive, high-feedback cultures
"They're hard to read — I don't know if they're happy with the work"
Swedish professional feedback tends to be measured — neither effusive nor harsh. The absence of visible enthusiasm is not disappointment, and a calm "that works" is often high praise. Waiting for American-style positive reinforcement will leave you consistently under-informed about where you actually stand. Ask directly, and read the answer literally rather than trying to calibrate it upward.
What helps: Ask specifically — "is there anything you'd change about this?" rather than "what do you think?" The first question gives permission for honest input that the second sometimes doesn't.

Five things about Sweden worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your Swedish colleagues are coming from.

1
Sweden's welfare state was built through negotiation, not revolution
The Swedish model — a comprehensive welfare state combined with a market economy and strong labour rights — was constructed through the Saltsjöbaden Agreement of 1938, a landmark deal between employers and unions that avoided the class conflict tearing apart much of Europe at the time. It wasn't imposed by a government; it was negotiated. This history explains a great deal about Swedish working culture: the faith in process, the belief that good outcomes come from bringing the right people together, and the instinct to consult broadly before deciding. The working culture is a workplace expression of a national settlement.
Conversation opener: Ask a Swedish colleague what they think makes the Swedish model work — or not work. The answer is usually considered and specific.
2
IKEA, H&M, Spotify, Minecraft — Sweden's global consumer output is disproportionate
IKEA was founded by Ingvar Kamprad in 1943. H&M opened its first store in 1947. Spotify launched in 2008. Minecraft was released in 2011. For a country of ten million people, the concentration of globally dominant consumer brands is hard to explain by coincidence. What connects them is a specifically Swedish sensibility: practicality, clarity, democratic design — the idea that good products should work well and be accessible, without pretension. It's the same instinct that built the welfare state, applied to commerce.
Conversation opener: Ask a Swedish colleague which Swedish company or product they think is most underappreciated internationally. The answer is rarely IKEA.
3
After the US and UK, Sweden is the world's third largest exporter of popular music
ABBA, Roxette, Ace of Base, The Cardigans, Robyn, Swedish House Mafia, Avicii — the list spans decades and genres. Behind the artists is an industry: the songwriter-producer model pioneered by Max Martin, who has written more US number one singles than any living songwriter, has its roots in Stockholm. A country of ten million people has shaped the sound of global pop music for fifty years. Asking a Swedish colleague about the music scene usually opens something immediate.
Conversation opener: Ask a Swedish colleague which Swedish artist or producer they think deserves more international recognition. Avicii's legacy comes up often, and never casually.
4
Astrid Lindgren changed a government
Pippi Longstocking's creator is one of the most translated authors in the world. In 1976, Lindgren published a satirical fairy tale in a Swedish newspaper attacking the country's marginal tax rates — which had reached over 100% of income for some self-employed people due to a technical flaw. The piece triggered a national debate that contributed directly to the Social Democrats losing power for the first time in 44 years. An author of children's books changed a government. That tells you something about the relationship between cultural voice, public argument, and civic life in Sweden.
Conversation opener: Ask a Swedish colleague what they know about the Lindgren tax affair. It's a story many Swedes know, and it usually surprises people from outside.
5
The Swedish Vikings went east — not west
When people think Vikings, they tend to think of raids on England and Ireland — those were mostly Norwegian and Danish. The Swedish Vikings, known as the Varangians, went east: through Russia, down the great rivers to Constantinople and Baghdad, establishing trading routes that connected Scandinavia to Byzantium and the Islamic world. They founded Novgorod and Kiev, served as the personal bodyguard of the Byzantine emperors — the Varangian Guard — and left their marks in runestones across Sweden that record journeys to places most Europeans at the time had never heard of. The Swedish Viking story is one of trade, exploration, and extraordinary geographic reach — less about raiding, more about connecting a continent. Asking a Swedish colleague about this usually surprises them with how little of it is commonly known outside Sweden.
Conversation opener: Ask a Swedish colleague if they know about the Varangian Guard. Most do, and the detail that they served in Constantinople tends to land well.
Southeast Asia · Kingdom of Thailand

Thailand

The only country in Southeast Asia never colonised by a European power — a fact that shapes the culture in ways that run deeper than historical trivia. Thai working culture is centred on harmony, face, and warmth, where the smile is real but layered, hierarchy is respected with genuine sincerity, and the avoidance of open conflict is not weakness but a considered value.

Population 72M Capital: Bangkok Language: Thai Currency: Thai Baht (THB)
ประเทศไทย

Thailand negotiated the modern world on its own terms — the diplomatic agility that kept the colonial powers at bay, the emphasis on hierarchy and face that kept internal order, the deep reverence for the monarchy that provided continuity. These are all connected, and they shape the working culture in ways that show up at work.

The other essential context is Buddhism. Theravada Buddhism is not a Sunday practice in Thailand — it is woven into the daily texture of life in ways that shape attitudes toward conflict, hierarchy, suffering, and time. The monk at the early-morning alms round, the spirit house outside the office building, the amulet worn under the work shirt — these are not decorative. They reflect a worldview in which equanimity in the face of difficulty is an aspiration rather than a coping mechanism. The Thai smile is one of the most famous and most misread things about the country. It can mean happiness, embarrassment, apology, discomfort, and the wish that the current situation would resolve itself — often simultaneously. Learning to read which one you are receiving is one of the more useful skills available.

Thai working culture is shaped by Buddhist values, a deep respect for hierarchy, and a strong orientation toward harmony. Here is what you are likely to encounter.

Hierarchy is sincere, not just structural
Thai organisations are clearly hierarchical and the deference to seniority is felt, not merely performed. The concept of kreng jai means that juniors will go to considerable lengths to avoid causing discomfort to seniors — including withholding bad news, avoiding disagreement, and framing things more positively than the situation warrants. Decisions flow from the top, and the most senior person shapes the direction of a conversation simply by being in it. Understanding the hierarchy is not just about knowing who to address first — it is about understanding that the whole communication system operates differently depending on where you sit within it.
Sanuk: work should have some joy in it
Sanuk — fun, enjoyment, the quality of making things pleasant — is a genuine Thai value that extends into professional life. Thais tend to bring lightness, humour, and a preference for good atmosphere to their work. An environment that is relentlessly serious, tense, or joyless creates a kind of cultural friction that is hard to name but real. Colleagues who bring warmth and occasional levity to the work tend to build better relationships here. This is not about avoiding seriousness when it matters — it is about understanding that the quality of the working atmosphere is itself a professional consideration.
Indirect communication and the loaded smile
Thai professional communication is high-context and face-oriented. Direct disagreement, open criticism, and blunt feedback — particularly upward or in front of others — are avoided with real consistency. The "yes" in a Thai meeting is often a signal that the message was received, not that it was agreed to. Problems surface obliquely: through tone, through absence, through the third party who quietly lets you know that something is wrong. The smile that greets a request you cannot fulfil is not deception — it is the face-saving mechanism doing its job. Learning to read beyond it takes time and a willingness to ask privately rather than publicly.
Relationships and the slow build of trust
Thai business culture rewards patience and relational investment. Trust is built through repeated contact, shared meals, demonstrated reliability, and the accumulation of goodwill over time — not through a single impressive first meeting. Showing up consistently, following through on small things, and being personally warm across multiple interactions is what actually moves the relationship forward. A well-maintained relationship in Thailand is one of the more durable foundations you can build professional work on.
Time as a flexible resource
Thailand operates on a more fluid relationship with time than Northern European or East Asian equivalents. Meetings may start late, schedules shift, and the pace of decision-making can feel slow to counterparts used to faster-turning cultures. The relationship and the atmosphere matter more than the clock, and disrupting either in pursuit of a tighter schedule tends to be counterproductive. That said, international business contexts in Bangkok run considerably closer to stated times than more traditional settings.
Practical tips
→
Never raise your voice or show anger. Loss of emotional control is a serious loss of face in Thai culture — for both parties. Whatever the frustration, maintaining calm composure is both practically more effective and culturally essential.
→
Treat the wai correctly. A foreigner who receives a wai and returns it with a genuine nod of acknowledgement is doing fine. Initiating it indiscriminately or doing it badly is worse than not doing it.
→
Never touch someone's head. The head is spiritually the highest part of the body in Thai culture. Touching it — even affectionately — is a real transgression.
→
Find out who actually holds the relationship. The person who formally heads a project and the person whose personal connection makes it move are often different. Both matter; the second usually matters more.
→
Build in extra time around key decisions. The consultation, the internal alignment, the need for the senior person to be briefed before anything is committed — these are the process, not delays to it.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

Kreng jai: the instinct not to impose
Kreng jai — often translated as "consideration for others' feelings" — describes a deep Thai instinct to avoid causing inconvenience, discomfort, or embarrassment to others. In practice this means colleagues will often not ask for help they need, will not raise problems that might burden you, and will not disagree with a position that seems important to you. The avoidance of open conflict is a deeply held social consideration. The challenge it creates for working relationships is that it can make it hard to know when something is wrong until it has been wrong for a while.
In practice: Create regular, low-stakes check-ins that make it easy to raise small things. "Is there anything that would be useful to talk through?" asked privately and genuinely is more productive than waiting for problems to surface on their own.
The monarchy is not a topic
Thailand has strict lèse-majesté laws — criticising the monarchy is a criminal offence, and the law is enforced. But beyond the legal dimension, reverence for the monarchy among many Thais is deeply sincere and personal. King Bhumibol Adulyadej, who reigned for seventy years until his death in 2016, is regarded by many Thais with genuine love and grief that is still felt. Any commentary on the monarchy — however mild, however academic — in front of Thai colleagues is a serious social error and potentially a legal one.
In practice: If the topic comes up, listen respectfully and say nothing critical. The appropriate response to reverence is acknowledgement, not debate.
Shoes off — and the logic behind it
Removing shoes before entering a home, a temple, and sometimes an office is the default expectation in Thailand. The feet are the lowest and least spiritually pure part of the body; bringing the outside world's dirt into a space of significance is a real transgression. Being the person who doesn't notice the pile of shoes at the door and walks straight in is a more consequential mistake than it might seem.
In practice: Look for the shoes before you enter any space that might follow this convention. When in doubt, ask.
Don't point with your feet — or your finger
Pointing the soles of your feet toward another person, toward a Buddha image, or toward an altar is disrespectful — feet are the lowest part of the body and this is a real affront. Similarly, pointing at a person with a single finger is considered rude; gesturing with the whole hand, palm down, is the accepted alternative. These are small physical habits that take almost no effort to adjust and that signal whether you have made the effort to understand where you are.
In practice: Sit cross-legged or with feet tucked away in traditional settings. When indicating direction or people, use the whole hand.
Monks occupy a specific and protected social space
Buddhist monks in Thailand are highly respected and subject to specific social rules. Women may not touch a monk or hand anything directly to one. On public transport, seats near monks are generally left for them. Treating a monk with casual disregard, or not noticing the social space they occupy, will be noticed by Thai colleagues with more discomfort than they will express directly.
In practice: If you're unsure how to behave around a monk, err toward formality and distance. A respectful nod is always appropriate.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures is not about bad intentions — it is about different defaults. Here is what is often going on underneath.

Direct culturesmeetsThai culture
"They always seem happy but I never know what's actually going on"
The Thai smile and the warm, forward-looking communication style can make it hard to tell when something is wrong, when a commitment is not going to be met, or when a relationship has cooled. The maintenance of pleasant atmosphere is a structural priority — which means the signals of difficulty tend to be subtle: a slightly less warm greeting, a slower response, a task that moves at half the previous pace. What you get in the meeting is often the socially optimised version of the situation, not the operational one.
What helps: Create regular one-to-one check-ins in informal settings. Ask specifically — "what's the hardest part of this right now?" — rather than the general "how's it going?" that usually produces a fine.
Task-first culturesmeetsThai culture
"They agreed and then nothing moved — and nobody told me there was a problem"
The agreement in the meeting may have been genuine in the social sense — nobody wanted to disrupt the harmony by saying that the timeline was unrealistic or the resource unavailable. Kreng jai means that the cost of raising a problem out loud, in front of others, often feels higher than the cost of managing it quietly — until it becomes unmanageable. By the time the problem surfaces explicitly, it has often been visible to everyone except the person it most needed to reach.
What helps: Build in mid-point check-ins with specific questions. "What do we need to make this work that we don't currently have?" is more useful than "are we on track?" — the first question invites the problem; the second invites a yes.
Direct culturesmeetsThai face culture
"They're great when things are good but hard to reach when there's a problem"
In a face-oriented culture, problems carry a social weight that makes them harder to raise the more serious they become. A small issue can be mentioned obliquely; a significant failure is much harder to name directly because naming it means someone loses face. The colleague who goes quiet when something has gone wrong is trying to figure out how to surface the problem without causing more damage than the problem itself.
What helps: Make it easy to raise bad news by receiving it well when it arrives. The colleague who responds to bad news with calm, practical problem-solving will consistently get earlier warnings than the one who doesn't.
Flat-hierarchy culturesmeetsThai culture
"They don't push back — I don't know if they actually have a view"
In a hierarchical culture shaped by kreng jai, expressing a view that contradicts a senior person's position — especially in a group setting — is not something most Thai colleagues will do spontaneously, regardless of whether they have strong opinions. The opinion is often there; the social conditions for expressing it in the meeting simply aren't. The candid view tends to surface in private, informal settings, between people who have established enough trust that honesty doesn't feel dangerous.
What helps: Ask directly and privately, and make it explicit that a different view is welcome. "I want to know if you see this differently — I find it genuinely useful" is an invitation that tends to produce a real answer from someone who has earned a degree of trust.

Five things about Thailand worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your Thai colleagues are coming from.

1
Thailand was never colonised — and the pride in this runs quietly through everything
While every neighbouring country fell under French, British, or Dutch colonial rule, Thailand — then Siam — maintained its independence through skilled diplomacy, strategic territorial concessions, and the particular abilities of its 19th-century kings, especially Rama IV and Rama V. This is a source of deep, quiet national pride that shapes the culture's relationship with foreign influence, its sense of civilisational self-confidence, and its attitude toward outside criticism. Thailand did not need to be saved or developed by a colonial power — and this history is present in the national identity in ways that show up at work.
Conversation opener: Ask a Thai colleague what they think is the most underappreciated fact about Thai history. The answer tends to be specific and proud.
2
Muay Thai is more than a sport — it is a living tradition
Muay Thai — the striking art that uses fists, elbows, knees, and shins — is Thailand's national sport and one of the most effective martial arts in the world. But it is also a ritual and a spiritual practice. Fighters perform the wai kru — a pre-fight ceremony honouring teachers and ancestors — before every bout. For many Thai men, particularly from rural areas, Muay Thai represents a path out of poverty, a source of community identity, and a connection to something very old. Asking a Thai colleague about Muay Thai — who their favourite fighter is, whether they've trained — opens a conversation that touches something real about Thai identity and pride.
Conversation opener: Ask whether they've ever trained Muay Thai or watched fights live. Almost everyone has a story.
3
Thai food is extraordinary — and the version most of the world knows is a small fraction of it
Pad thai, green curry, som tam — these are real, and they are good. They are also roughly equivalent to judging Italian food by spaghetti bolognese. Regional Thai cuisine varies enormously: the fiery, herb-heavy cooking of the north, the coconut-rich cuisine of the south, the Isan food of the northeast — papaya salad, grilled meats, sticky rice — that is actually the most widely eaten cuisine in the country. Asking a Thai colleague where they're from and what the food is like there will typically produce an animated, specific, and passionate response. Regional food pride in Thailand is real and deep.
Conversation opener: Ask which region produces the best food. Be prepared for a strong opinion and a detailed argument.
4
The king is more than a constitutional figurehead — understanding this matters
King Bhumibol Adulyadej — Rama IX — reigned for seventy years until his death in 2016, and the grief that accompanied his passing was not performed. For many Thais, particularly older generations, he represented stability, moral authority, and a personal investment in the country's development. The reverence for the institution of the monarchy — and for Rama IX specifically — is real and should be treated as such. Understanding this is separate from having an opinion about it, which is not a thing to have out loud in Thailand.
Conversation opener: Ask an older Thai colleague what they remember about Rama IX's development projects in rural Thailand. The personal gratitude is usually real and specific.
5
The Thai calendar runs on its own logic — and so does the spirit world
Thailand uses the Buddhist Era calendar, which runs 543 years ahead of the Gregorian — 2025 CE is 2568 BE. Astrologically auspicious dates are consulted for weddings, business openings, and major decisions. Spirit houses — small shrines outside homes and businesses where offerings are left for the spirits of the land — are not superstition but active religious practice. Amulets are worn for protection, and their provenance and spiritual power are serious topics. The visible, modern Bangkok of glass towers and BTS trains exists alongside a rich invisible world that most visitors don't see and most Thais take entirely seriously. Engaging with this dimension of Thai culture with curiosity rather than scepticism opens a part of the country that the tourist version rarely reaches.
Conversation opener: Ask a Thai colleague about the spirit house outside their office or home. The explanation is usually both practical and genuinely felt.
Middle East / Europe · Transcontinental

Turkey

The only city in the world that straddles two continents, and a country that has spent most of its modern history negotiating between orientations — toward the West and toward the East, toward secularism and toward Islam, toward a European future and toward a deeper Ottoman past. That negotiation is unresolved, ongoing, and present in Turkish working culture.

Population 85M Capital: Ankara Language: Turkish Currency: Turkish Lira (TRY)
TÜRKİYE

Turkey sits at the intersection of Europe and Asia in ways that go well beyond geography. Istanbul is the only city in the world that straddles two continents, and the country as a whole has spent most of its modern history negotiating between orientations — toward the West and toward the East, toward secularism and toward Islam, toward a European future and toward a deeper Ottoman past. That negotiation is unresolved, ongoing, and present in Turkish working culture in ways that show up before you expect them to.

The Ottoman Empire lasted six centuries and at its height stretched from Vienna to the Persian Gulf. Its collapse after World War I, and the subsequent founding of the Turkish Republic by Mustafa Kemal Atatürk in 1923, was one of the most radical state transformations of the 20th century — a deliberate, top-down project to build a modern secular nation out of the ruins of an empire. Atatürk remains a figure of almost sacred status for many Turks, and his image appears in nearly every government building, school, and many offices.

What cuts across much of this is a culture of strong hospitality, intense family loyalty, a deep pride in Turkish history and civilisation, and a working style that combines warmth with a formality around seniority that surprises people expecting something more casual.

Turkish working culture is shaped by relationships, hierarchy, and a pride in civilisational heritage that runs through even the most international professional settings. Here is what you will likely encounter.

Relationships before transactions
Turkish business culture is relationship-first. Before substantive work can really begin, there needs to be some basis of personal connection — shared conversation, shared food or tea, a sense that the people involved know each other as people rather than just as role-holders. Jumping straight to the agenda without this groundwork tends to produce polite engagement but not real investment. The time spent building the relationship is not separate from the work — it is what makes the work possible.
Hierarchy is visible and respected
Turkish workplaces tend to be clearly hierarchical. Seniority determines who speaks first, whose opinion shapes the room, and how decisions are made and communicated. Titles carry weight. A junior colleague openly contradicting a senior in a group setting would be unusual and likely uncomfortable for everyone present. Dissent tends to travel through different channels, at different moments, and between people with the right relationship to carry it.
The boss decides — but relationships shape what the boss decides
Decision-making authority typically sits at the top, and expecting consensus-building as a default will often lead to confusion. At the same time, the relationships and informal conversations that precede a decision — who has the ear of the senior person, who surfaced the right information at the right moment — matter enormously. The formal decision and the informal process that shaped it are two different things — and both matter.
Communication is warm, high-context, and face-conscious
Turkish communication combines genuine expressiveness and warmth with a high-context approach to difficult content. Disagreement, criticism, and bad news tend to be delivered indirectly, softened, or deferred — particularly in group settings and across hierarchical lines. Direct, unmediated bluntness tends to land as rude rather than clear.
Tea is the medium through which everything happens
Turkish tea — çay, black, in small tulip-shaped glasses, served constantly — is the ambient liquid of Turkish professional and social life. Offering tea is an act of hospitality; accepting it is how you signal that you are comfortable and that the interaction is going well. Meetings run on tea. Negotiations run on tea. The rhythm of the tea is part of the social texture of the interaction.
Practical tips
→
Invest in the relationship before you need it. Coffee, lunch, or simply time spent in informal conversation is part of how the work gets done.
→
Accept the tea. Every time. The gesture matters more than the caffeine.
→
Address seniority correctly. Bey (Mr) and Hanım (Ms) after the first name is the standard respectful form — so "Mehmet Bey" or "Ayşe Hanım." Getting this right in early interactions lands well.
→
Do not mistake warmth for informality. Turkish colleagues can be warm and still expect real formality around seniority and hierarchy.
→
Be patient with the pace. Decisions take time. Pushing for faster resolution than the relationship supports tends to slow things down rather than speed them up.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

Hospitality is close to a moral obligation
Turkish hospitality is not casual generosity — it is a deeply held value with real social weight. Being welcomed into a Turkish colleague's home, office, or circle and declining what is offered creates genuine discomfort. The host's obligation is to provide; the guest's obligation is to receive. Refusing food, drink, or the invitation itself repeatedly will be felt as a rejection, whatever the practical reason behind it.
In practice: Accept what is offered. If you genuinely cannot eat or drink something, a brief warm explanation is fine — but the default should always be to receive.
Atatürk is not a historical figure — he is a present one
Expressing criticism of Atatürk in front of Turkish colleagues — even mild or academic criticism — is likely to produce a very uncomfortable silence or a sharp response. For a large portion of the Turkish population, Atatürk represents the founding of the modern state and a personal pride that goes well beyond politics. It is also, notably, illegal to insult his memory under Turkish law. This is one of the clearer cases where an outsider's intellectual curiosity bumps hard into a living cultural reality.
In practice: Curiosity about Atatürk is fine and usually welcomed. Critical framing is a different matter entirely.
Family is the primary unit — everything else follows
Turkish family structures tend to be close, multi-generational, and mutually obligated in ways that shape professional life directly. A colleague who needs to attend a family event, support a parent, or manage a family crisis is operating within a system of obligations that takes genuine priority. Understanding this — and treating it as legitimate rather than as a distraction from professional reliability — will serve you considerably better than the alternative.
In practice: Ask about family with real interest. History is not a distraction from getting to know your Turkish colleague — it is often the most direct route.
Pride in Turkish history and civilisation is real and easily bruised
Turks carry a deep awareness of Ottoman and pre-Ottoman history and a corresponding sensitivity to framings that reduce Turkey to a peripheral or lesser civilisation. Comments that position Turkey as not-quite-European, or as a developing country needing to catch up to Western norms, tend to land very badly. Showing knowledge of and respect for Turkish history is one of the faster routes to real warmth.
In practice: Ask about history, culture, and cuisine with genuine curiosity. The depth of what's there to discover is usually a surprise, and Turks tend to notice when someone has made the effort.
The evil eye is taken more seriously than outsiders expect
The nazar — the blue eye amulet — is ubiquitous in Turkey: hanging in offices, homes, cars, worn as jewellery. The belief that envy or admiration from others can cause harm runs through Turkish culture at a level that coexists comfortably with modernity and education. Treating it as a quaint superstition, or being visibly amused by it, misreads how genuinely it is held.
In practice: Acknowledge it with curiosity rather than scepticism. Asking about it tends to open a warm conversation rather than a defensive one.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures is not about bad intentions — it is about different defaults. Here is what is often going on underneath.

Turkish culturemeetslow-context, direct cultures
"I can never tell if they actually agree or are just being polite"
Turkish communication is calibrated to avoid public disagreement, preserve face, and maintain warmth in the relationship even when the substantive answer is no. What registers as evasion from a low-context perspective is often a clear enough signal within the system — the hesitation, the changed subject, the enthusiasm that is notably absent — but these signals require calibration to read. Pressing for a direct yes or no in a group setting often produces a yes that is not one.
What helps: Create private, informal space for the real conversation. One-to-one, over tea, with some relationship behind it, the honest assessment usually surfaces. "I want to make sure I am not missing something — is there anything about this that concerns you?" is a useful frame.
Turkish culturemeetsflat-hierarchy cultures
"Nobody will make a decision without checking with the boss"
In a clearly hierarchical culture, decisions that fall within someone's technical responsibility may still be referred upward as a matter of course — not because the person lacks capability, but because the culture around authority means that deciding unilaterally on things that touch the hierarchy feels presumptuous. This can look like lack of initiative from a flat-hierarchy perspective.
What helps: Be explicit about what authority you are delegating and to whom. "I want you to make this call — I trust your judgement here" needs to be said directly, not implied.
Turkish culturemeetstime-precise cultures
"Meetings never start on time and the agenda is always abandoned"
Turkish professional time, particularly in social and relationship-building contexts, tends to be more fluid than Northern European or American defaults. A meeting that starts fifteen minutes late and spends the first half on personal conversation before reaching the agenda is functioning normally. The relationship maintenance happening in that time is doing real work.
What helps: Build buffer into schedules. Treat the pre-agenda conversation as part of the meeting, not as delay. In formal external contexts, things tend to run closer to stated times.
Turkish culturemeetscultures uncomfortable with strong emotion
"They seem very intense — everything feels high-stakes"
Turkish communication tends toward expressiveness. Passion, animation, raised voices in disagreement, demonstrative enthusiasm — these are normal registers, not signs that something is wrong. Colleagues from cultures that associate emotional restraint with professionalism can misread this expressiveness as aggression or instability.
What helps: Read the relationship, not just the register. If the warmth is present and the relationship is intact, the expressiveness is usually a sign of engagement, not a warning signal.
Turkish diasporameetscredential-discounting cultures
"Their background is from Turkey — I am not sure how transferable it is"
Turkey has well-regarded universities — Boğaziçi, Middle East Technical University, Bilkent, Istanbul Technical University — producing graduates in engineering, computer science, economics, and the humanities who are competitive internationally. The assumption that a Turkish qualification or career history needs significant discounting is frequently wrong.
What helps: Ask specifically about their training, their projects, their professional trajectory. The quality tends to be apparent quickly.

Five things about Turkey worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your Turkish colleagues are coming from.

1
Istanbul was the capital of three empires — and it shows
Constantinople was the capital of the Eastern Roman (Byzantine) Empire for over a thousand years, then the capital of the Ottoman Empire for nearly five centuries. The city contains layers of civilisation that are literally stacked on top of each other — the Hagia Sophia alone has been a Greek Orthodox cathedral, a Roman Catholic cathedral, an Ottoman mosque, a secular museum, and a mosque again. Istanbul is the only city in the world that has served as the capital of a Christian empire and a Muslim empire, and the architecture, the culture, and the collective memory of the city reflect all of it. For Turks, Istanbul is a statement about the depth and reach of Turkish and Ottoman civilisation.
Conversation opener: Ask a Turkish colleague from Istanbul which layer of the city they find most fascinating — Byzantine, Ottoman, or modern. The answer tells you something about how they relate to Turkish history.
2
Turkish literature has a Nobel laureate who was nearly imprisoned for what he said about his own country
Orhan Pamuk won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 2006. His novels — Snow, My Name is Red, The Museum of Innocence, Istanbul — explore Turkish identity, memory, East-West tension, and the weight of history. In 2005, he told a Swiss newspaper that "thirty thousand Kurds and a million Armenians were killed in these lands, and nobody but me dares to talk about it" — a statement that led to criminal charges under Turkish law before they were eventually dropped under international pressure. The case illustrated something real about the tensions in Turkish public life that his novels often explore. Knowing his work signals a kind of engagement with Turkish culture that tends to be noticed.
Read list: Istanbul (start here for the city and the culture) · Snow · My Name is Red
3
Turkish football is more than a sport — it is a primary arena of city identity and regional pride
Galatasaray, Fenerbahçe, and Beşiktaş divide Istanbul in ways that go well beyond sport — they map onto different neighbourhoods, different social identities, and a rivalry that is some of the most intense in European football. Galatasaray's victory in the 2000 UEFA Cup and Super Cup — the first Turkish club to win a European trophy — is still remembered as one of the proudest moments in Turkish football history. Asking a Turkish colleague which club they support is a reliable opener — and the answer tells you something real about where they are from and who they are.
Conversation opener: Ask a Turkish colleague from Istanbul which of the three big clubs they support, and why. Be prepared for strong opinions delivered with equal warmth and certainty.
4
Turkish cuisine is one of the great underrated food cultures of the world — and Turks know it
The argument that Turkish cuisine, alongside French and Chinese, is one of the three foundational cuisines of the world is not an unreasonable one. The Ottoman kitchen at its height was drawing on ingredients and techniques from three continents. Meze, kebab, börek, baklava, manti, pide, lahmacun — these are not variations on a single tradition but a vast and regionally differentiated food culture in which every city, every region, and every family has its own specific versions and strong opinions about them. Asking a Turkish colleague about food — where their family is from, what the best regional dish is, what their grandmother made — tends to unlock an enthusiasm that runs well past appetite.
Conversation opener: Ask a Turkish colleague which city has the best food in Turkey and why. Clear your schedule.
5
The Turkish bath — hammam — is a social institution that has been running continuously for centuries
The hammam tradition, inherited from Byzantine and Roman bathing culture and developed through the Ottoman period, has been a central social institution in Turkish life for hundreds of years. The neighbourhood hammam was historically where people gathered, news travelled, and the social fabric was maintained. Major historical hammams in Istanbul have been in continuous operation for five hundred years. While the hammam has become more associated with tourism in international perception, for many Turks it remains a cultural institution — a place of relaxation, social ritual, and a specific kind of ordinary luxury. Asking a Turkish colleague whether they still go to a hammam tends to open something warm and sometimes nostalgic.
Conversation opener: Ask a Turkish colleague about the hammam tradition in their family or city — whether it is something they grew up with. The answers usually reveal something about how Turkish cultural continuity actually works in daily life.
Middle East · Gulf

United Arab Emirates

A federation of seven emirates that transformed from pearl-fishing settlements into one of the world's most internationally connected countries in roughly fifty years. The most important thing to understand: Emirati nationals make up around 11% of the population. The working culture is genuinely trilateral — Emiratis, Arab and South Asian expats, and Western expats, all operating by somewhat different rules in the same workplace.

Population 10M (10% nationals) Capital: Abu Dhabi Language: Arabic (English widely used) Currency: UAE Dirham (AED)
UAE

The UAE is one of the most unusual working environments on earth. A federation of seven emirates — Abu Dhabi, Dubai, Sharjah, and four smaller ones — it has transformed from a collection of pearl-fishing and trading settlements into one of the world's wealthiest and most internationally connected countries in roughly fifty years.

The most important thing to understand about working in the UAE is the demographic structure. Emirati nationals make up roughly 11% of the total population. The remaining 89% are expatriates: South Asians make up the largest group, followed by other Arabs, Filipinos, and a smaller but disproportionately visible Western expat population. This means that a typical UAE workplace is not one culture navigating itself — it is multiple cultures navigating each other, with Emiratis as the host culture whose norms and values set the framework within which everyone else operates.

The Emirati colleague you meet is a citizen of a country where citizens are a small minority, where the economy has been built largely by imported labour, and where the question of national identity — what it means to be Emirati, what is being preserved and what is changing — is both complex and deeply felt. The country is also the regional headquarters for a remarkable number of multinational companies, a global aviation hub, and an increasingly significant financial and tech centre. The professional culture that has developed is international — but it operates inside a framework of Emirati values, Islamic customs, and social norms that are real, not merely decorative.

UAE working culture is shaped by the Emirati host culture, a international expat workforce, and an Islamic framework that sets the ambient norms. Here is what you will likely encounter.

The host culture sets the frame
Even in the most international-seeming workplace, Emirati cultural values — hospitality, respect for seniority, Islamic norms around working hours and social interaction, the importance of face and dignity — set the ambient framework. Non-Emirati colleagues who treat the UAE as a culturally neutral international environment tend to find out relatively quickly that it is not.
Relationships and wasta matter
Wasta — the use of personal connections and influence to navigate systems, secure opportunities, and get things done — is a real and significant feature of how the UAE works, particularly in interactions involving government or semi-government entities. Understanding that who you know, and the respect attached to the name you are operating under, has a tangible effect on how quickly and smoothly things move is practically useful.
Hierarchy is clear and respected
UAE workplaces tend toward clear hierarchy, and seniority — in age, in rank, and in social standing — carries real weight. In Emirati culture particularly, the senior person present shapes the direction and tone of a conversation in ways that do not require explicit assertion. This is more pronounced in government and family business contexts; international private sector environments run somewhat flatter, though hierarchy remains more present than in Northern European or Anglo-American workplaces.
Gender dynamics vary by context and employer
The UAE is one of the more liberal environments in the Gulf for professional women — women work across sectors, in senior roles, and in mixed-gender settings that would be unusual elsewhere in the region. At the same time, conservative norms around gender interaction are present and real, particularly with Emirati nationals and in more traditional settings. Reading the room — and being attentive to the specific norms of each context — is more useful than arriving with fixed assumptions in either direction.
Ramadan restructures the professional calendar
During Ramadan, working hours are reduced, the rhythm of the day shifts significantly, and the evenings — when the fast is broken at iftar — become the primary time for hospitality, relationship-building, and informal professional socialising. Navigating Ramadan well — showing genuine respect for what it means rather than treating it purely as a scheduling inconvenience — makes a visible difference.
Practical tips
→
Learn the basic Islamic greetings and use them. As-salamu alaykum / wa alaykum as-salam is a small thing that signals real respect. In Emirati cultural contexts it matters more than most expats realise.
→
Dress conservatively, especially outside the most international environments. This matters more in Abu Dhabi than in Dubai, more in government contexts than in private sector ones, but erring toward conservative is always the right default.
→
Accept Arabic coffee and dates. Gahwa and dates are the traditional Emirati welcome; declining them is a small but real social misstep.
→
Be especially attentive during Ramadan. Respecting the fast, attending an iftar if invited, acknowledging the significance of the month — these are noticed and appreciated.
→
Understand that the expat experience and the Emirati experience of the same workplace can be very different. Reading both is more useful than assuming they are the same.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

Sheikh Zayed is a founding father in the deepest sense
Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan — who unified the seven emirates in 1971 and led the country until his death in 2004 — is revered by Emiratis with a genuine depth of feeling that goes beyond political respect. His values — generosity, consultation, the care of citizens, the development of the nation — are the stated foundation of Emirati national identity. Treating him as just another political leader, or being dismissive about the ruling family in any way in front of Emirati colleagues, is a significant social error.
In practice: If his name comes up, listen. The feelings tend to be personal as well as political, and the gratitude is usually real.
The Emirati national is not the typical employee
