Emiratisation — the government policy requiring private sector companies to employ minimum percentages of Emirati nationals — means that in many workplaces, Emiratis occupy specific roles as part of a deliberate national development programme. Emiratis working in private sector roles are often aware of the perceptions that come with this. Treating an Emirati colleague's position as presumptively a function of policy rather than capability is an assumption that tends to be both wrong and, when perceived, badly received.
In practice: Ask about their background and experience with genuine curiosity. The assumption of the alternative is both unfair and frequently incorrect.
Friday is the day of rest — and the weekend runs Friday-Saturday
The UAE weekend is Friday and Saturday, with Sunday the first working day of the week. Friday prayers are a significant social and religious event. Scheduling anything that requires Emirati attendance on a Friday morning, or treating Friday as a normal working day, signals that you have not understood the basic calendar of the place you are working in.
In practice: Build your weekly planning around the Friday-Saturday weekend from day one. It takes about five minutes to internalise and prevents a significant amount of friction.
Public behaviour has real legal dimensions
The UAE operates under a legal framework that gives social and religious norms the force of law in ways that many expats underestimate. Public displays of affection, drunkenness in public, certain types of online content, and criticism of the ruling family or government are all legally regulated. Understanding that the social rules of the country have legal backing changes how you think about them.
In practice: Do the basic research before you arrive. Know what the legal sensitivities are. Apply the same social intelligence you would apply in any unfamiliar environment: observe, ask, and follow the lead of people who know the place.
The majlis is where real conversations happen
The majlis — literally "a place of sitting" — is the traditional Emirati setting for open, consultative conversation: a room or gathering where the leader receives visitors, hears concerns, and conducts informal business in an accessible way. The majlis tradition shapes expectations about how leadership should function — accessible, consultative, relationship-based — in ways that persist into modern professional contexts. An Emirati senior figure who wants to have a real conversation will often set it in a context that approximates this informality.
In practice: When a senior Emirati suggests something informal — a coffee, a walk, a casual setting — treat it as a significant professional opportunity, not as a social aside.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures is not about bad intentions — it is about different defaults. Here is what is often going on underneath.

Emirati culturemeetsfast-moving, task-first cultures
"Nothing moves without multiple layers of approval — it is incredibly slow"
Decision-making in Emirati organisational culture, particularly in government and semi-government entities, tends to be consultative, consensus-building, and respectful of hierarchy in ways that produce a pace that can frustrate colleagues accustomed to flatter, faster-moving structures. The relationship maintenance and face-preservation happening inside the process is doing real work — building the consensus that makes implementation durable — but it does not look like that from the outside.
What helps: Build timeline assumptions that account for the real pace, not the theoretical one. Establish relationships at multiple levels rather than relying on a single point of contact. The process goes faster when the relationships are already in place.
UAE working culturemeetscolleagues unfamiliar with the expat hierarchy
"There seems to be an informal pecking order that nobody acknowledges"
The UAE workplace often contains an unspoken hierarchy among expatriates that maps onto nationality, passport, and perceived economic status — Western expats on certain contract structures, Arab expats, South Asian expats — that operates alongside the formal organisational hierarchy and sometimes in tension with it. This is rarely explicit, often felt, and frequently a source of real friction that nobody names directly.
What helps: Be attentive to it. Treating all colleagues with equivalent professional respect — regardless of nationality or contract type — is both ethically straightforward and, in a workplace where these dynamics are well understood, tends to be noticed positively.
Emirati culturemeetsdirect-feedback cultures
"I gave honest feedback and the relationship completely changed"
Face — personal dignity, the avoidance of public embarrassment — is a central value in Emirati professional culture. Direct negative feedback, delivered in a group setting or in a way that places someone in a position of public inadequacy, causes damage that the substantive content of the feedback does not justify. The relationship change is a signal that a real social norm has been violated.
What helps: Deliver anything critical in private, with care, framed within genuine respect for the person. The more senior the person receiving the feedback, the more care the framing requires.
UAE working culturemeetscolleagues who treat Ramadan as inconvenience
"Everything grinds to a halt for a month — it is very frustrating"
Ramadan is the most important month in the Islamic calendar — a period of spiritual reflection, community, and discipline that carries real meaning for Muslim colleagues. Framing it primarily as a scheduling problem, eating or drinking in front of fasting colleagues without consideration, or visibly treating the month as an obstacle to productivity signals a failure to understand where you are and who you are working with.
What helps: Treat Ramadan as a professional calendar event that requires adaptation, not reluctant accommodation. Attend an iftar if invited. Show that you understand what the month means.
Western expat culturemeetsUAE legal and social norms
"I assumed it worked like back home — it does not"
Many Western expats arrive in the UAE with an implicit assumption that the social and legal framework will be broadly familiar. The UAE has made a deliberate effort to be internationally welcoming and commercially open, and in many respects it has succeeded. But the underlying legal and social framework is different in ways that become apparent at specific moments. Understanding that you are a guest operating under someone else's rules — and adjusting accordingly — is a better frame than assuming equivalence.
What helps: Do the basic research before you arrive. Know what the legal sensitivities are. Apply the same social intelligence you would apply in any unfamiliar environment.

Five things about the UAE worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your Emirati colleagues are coming from.

1
Dubai went from a small fishing and trading port to a global city in a single generation
In 1960, Dubai's population was around 40,000 people. Today it has over 3.5 million and a skyline recognised worldwide. The Burj Khalifa — at 828 metres — was the tallest building in the world when completed in 2010. The Palm Jumeirah added 78 kilometres of coastline. This pace of transformation has no real parallel in modern urban history, and understanding it changes how you read both the ambition of Emirati leadership and the cultural dislocation that can come with living inside rapid change.
Conversation opener: Ask an Emirati colleague what Dubai or Abu Dhabi looked like when their parents were young. The contrast tends to be vivid and personally felt.
2
The UAE's tolerance and coexistence model is a deliberate and carefully managed national project
In a region often marked by religious and sectarian tension, the UAE has made a conscious effort to practice tolerance — permitting churches, temples, and Hindu mandirs alongside mosques, and hosting the Abrahamic Family House, a mosque, church, and synagogue on a shared site in Abu Dhabi. This is both a genuine value and a deliberate strategic positioning. Understanding it as both — rather than reading it as purely cynical or purely idealistic — gives you a more accurate sense of how Emirati leadership thinks about the country's role in the world.
Conversation opener: Ask an Emirati colleague what they think of the Abrahamic Family House and what it represents. The answers tend to be thoughtful and reveal something about how Emiratis understand their country's international positioning.
3
Arabic coffee and the hospitality ritual that surrounds it deserves more attention than it usually gets
Emirati Arabic coffee — gahwa — is made with lightly roasted coffee beans, cardamom, saffron, and rosewater, and served in small handleless cups that are refilled until you indicate you are finished by gently shaking the cup from side to side. The ritual of offering, pouring, and receiving gahwa is one of the oldest and most codified hospitality practices in Gulf culture. Understanding how it works — that you keep accepting until you signal you are done, that the host pours for the guest first — is a small piece of cultural knowledge that lands disproportionately well in Emirati contexts.
Conversation opener: Ask an Emirati colleague about the gahwa tradition in their family — when it is served, what occasions it marks. The ritual tends to carry personal memory.
4
Falconry is a connection to the deepest layer of Emirati identity
Falconry has been practised by the people of the Arabian Peninsula for over two thousand years and is classified by UNESCO as an Intangible Cultural Heritage. For Emiratis, the falcon — which appears on the national coat of arms — connects directly to the Bedouin heritage of the desert, to a way of life that the oil wealth replaced within living memory, and to a set of values — patience, skill, the relationship between human and animal — that older Emiratis particularly feel a real bond with. Asking an Emirati colleague about falconry, with curiosity, tends to open something warm and personal that most professional interactions do not reach.
Conversation opener: Ask an Emirati colleague whether their family keeps falcons, or whether they grew up around falconry. The connection to Bedouin heritage tends to surface quickly.
5
The UAE has become one of the world's most significant contemporary art markets — faster than almost anyone expected
Abu Dhabi's Saadiyat Island hosts the Louvre Abu Dhabi — a museum with a real collection — and the planned site of what will be the world's largest Guggenheim. Art Dubai has become one of the most significant art fairs outside Basel and Miami. The Sharjah Biennial has been running for four decades. There is an art scene, a collecting culture, and a deliberate effort to position the UAE as a centre of cultural production as well as commerce. Asking a UAE-based colleague about the local arts scene tends to reveal an engagement that most outsiders do not expect.
Conversation opener: Ask a colleague based in Abu Dhabi whether they have been to the Louvre Abu Dhabi, and what they thought of it. The reactions are usually more nuanced than a simple yes or no.
Northern Europe · EU-adjacent

United Kingdom

A country that invented the rules of modern diplomacy, then built an entire culture around not having to state them. Brilliant, eccentric, class-conscious, and self-deprecating — understanding British working culture means learning to read between the lines, because what's said and what's meant are rarely the same thing.

Population 67M Capital: London Language: English Currency: Pound Sterling (GBP)
UK

Britain's great export isn't the English language or the common law — it's indirectness. A culture shaped by centuries of aristocratic understatement, boarding school restraint, and the social catastrophe of making anyone feel uncomfortable has produced a communication style that baffles even native English speakers from other countries. Americans think British colleagues are cold. Australians think they're passive-aggressive. Germans think they're evasive. They're not, exactly — they're just operating on a frequency that takes time to tune into.

The UK is also not one culture. Scotland, Wales, Northern Ireland, and England have meaningfully different characters. London is a world city that has more in common with New York and Singapore than it does with Birmingham or Glasgow. Class — though officially denied — still shapes voice, opportunity, and workplace dynamics in ways that are uncomfortable to discuss. And Brexit left a fracture line through the culture that is still very much live.

Understanding British working culture means getting comfortable with irony, understatement, and the queue as a moral institution.

What to expect when working with British colleagues — the patterns, defaults, and unspoken rules that shape how things actually get done.

The art of the "quite good"
British praise is calibrated very differently from American praise. "Not bad" means impressive. "Quite good" means very good. "Interesting" might mean "I have serious concerns." "We'll have to agree to disagree" means "you're wrong." The scale runs about two notches below what a literal reading would suggest. Calibrate accordingly, or you'll consistently underestimate how well — or badly — things are going.
Indirectness as policy
Criticism is almost never delivered directly, especially in formal settings. "That's one way to approach it" means "I don't think this will work." "You might want to consider..." is not a suggestion — it's a flag. Learning to read the hedge is a critical skill. Directness — particularly blunt, American-style feedback — often lands as aggressive even when it isn't meant that way.
Irony is the native language
British humour runs deep through professional life. Self-deprecation, dry wit, and the ability to make a joke at your own expense are not signs of low confidence — they're social currency. Someone who takes themselves very seriously in a British workplace makes others uncomfortable. Laughing at yourself is not weakness; it's the entry ticket.
Hierarchy — it's there, it's just politely hidden
British organisations often have clear hierarchies that nobody acknowledges explicitly. Seniority still shapes whose opinion shapes the room. But it's usually expressed through deference and tone rather than titles and formality. A very senior person might dress like everyone else and make you tea — the power is real, the display of it is not.
Meetings and the decision theatre
Meetings in British organisations often function as a place to confirm decisions made elsewhere — through corridor conversations, pre-meetings, and careful lobbying. Arriving at a big meeting expecting to make the case from scratch is often too late. The actual decision tends to live in the conversations that happened before the meeting.
Practical tips
→
Don't match American energy in a British room. Enthusiasm is welcome; performance of enthusiasm is not.
→
Listen for the hedge. "With respect..." and "I wonder if..." are polite signals that something is about to go wrong for you.
→
Small talk is mandatory, not optional. The weather, sport, the weekend — this is not filler, it's the social lubrication that makes the actual conversation possible.
→
Be on time — but not conspicuously early. Punctuality is expected. Showing up ten minutes early to a casual meeting can feel as odd as being ten minutes late.
→
Don't ask someone what school they went to. The class implications in the UK are significant enough that it's a social minefield, especially early in a relationship.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out — things that seem obvious from the inside and invisible from the outside. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

The queue is a moral institution
Queue-jumping is not a minor social faux pas in the UK — it is a real transgression. The queue represents fairness, patience, and civilised behaviour. Cutting in, even accidentally, will be noticed, silently judged, and occasionally muttered about. Nobody will say anything directly. That's not the point.
In practice: If you accidentally step ahead of someone, acknowledge it immediately. A quick "sorry, after you" repairs the damage entirely.
Self-deprecation is compulsory, boasting is forbidden
British social norms are extremely hostile to visible self-promotion. Talking about your own achievements, salary, or status in positive terms is considered deeply uncomfortable. The acceptable alternative is to achieve remarkable things and then make a small, dry remark about how it all went rather better than expected. This confuses Americans. It confuses everyone. It's still the rule.
In practice: When presenting your own work, let the quality speak. Frame successes as team efforts or happy accidents rather than personal triumphs.
Class is the conversation nobody is having
The British class system is alive, pervasive, and officially invisible. Accent, vocabulary, the school you attended, and how you hold a fork still carry social information that shapes how people are perceived. Most British people are acutely aware of class signals while simultaneously insisting they don't matter. They do.
In practice: Don't ask about someone's educational background early in a relationship. It's a minefield. If it comes up naturally, follow their lead on how much weight to give it.
The pub is an institution, not just a venue
The pub is where workplace relationships are cemented, confidences are exchanged, and hierarchies are briefly relaxed. Being the person who never comes to the pub — whether for social or religious reasons — is worth acknowledging early, so colleagues don't keep asking. Declining once is fine. Declining every time, without context, can make you seem distant in a culture where this ritual carries real weight.
In practice: You don't have to drink. Showing up with a soft drink and staying for an hour is entirely acceptable and goes a long way.
Tea is not about the tea
Tea in Britain carries an emotional weight that has almost nothing to do with the drink itself. Bad argument? Have a tea. Difficult news? Someone puts the kettle on before anything else is said. Medical emergency — someone's partner has had an accident, the hospital rush is imminent — and still, somehow, there's a tea first. It's a ritual that says: before we face this, let's mark it together. The tea creates a pause, a small act of care, a signal that you're not alone in it. Offering it is how British people say they're there for you. Accepting it is how you let them be.
In practice: Accept the tea. Even if you don't want it. Especially if things are hard. The gesture is the point — and understanding that changes a lot about how British emotional support actually works.
"Sorry" doesn't mean sorry
The British use "sorry" as punctuation — to soften a request, to get someone's attention, to fill a pause, to acknowledge an awkward situation without admitting fault. If someone bumps into you and you both say sorry simultaneously, nobody is taking blame. Treating every British "sorry" as an apology or admission of fault will confuse the relationship considerably.
In practice: Match the register. A reflexive "sorry" usually just needs a nod or a "no worries" — not a detailed response about who was at fault.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures isn't about bad intentions — it's about different defaults. Here's what's often going on underneath.

British culturemeetsdirect cultures
"They're so passive-aggressive"
What reads as passive-aggression is usually indirect communication — a deeply embedded default, not a personal strategy. The British colleague who says "that's certainly one approach" isn't being sarcastic to hurt you; they believe they.ve communicated their concern. The gap is in the encoding, not the intent.
What helps: Ask directly — "what would you change about this?" and you'll usually get an honest answer. The indirectness drops significantly in one-to-one conversations.
British culturemeetswarm, expressive cultures
"I can never tell if they actually like me"
British warmth is real but expressed through reliability, dry humour, and inclusion in small rituals — not through emotional disclosure or effusiveness. If a British colleague makes a dry in-joke at your expense, that's affection. They're teasing you because they like you. If they're excessively formal and polite, that's actually the colder signal.
What helps: Look for inclusion in the informal — being invited to the pub, being copied into things you didn't need to be, being told the gossip. These are the warmth signals.
British culturemeetsdirect decision-making cultures
"Nothing ever gets decided in the meeting"
British decision-making happens in the pre-meeting — in corridor conversations, informal chats, and carefully placed emails. The formal meeting is often there to ratify. Coming in with a fresh argument expecting to shift the outcome is usually too late. The work happens before the room fills up.
What helps: Invest in relationships outside the meeting room. Coffee with the right person beforehand is worth ten minutes of brilliant argument in the meeting.
British culturemeetslow-context cultures that separate humour from substance
"They keep making jokes — I can't tell if they're serious"
British humour is not a layer on top of the professional relationship — it's woven through it. A British colleague making a dry remark about a serious topic isn't trivialising it; they're processing it the only way that feels comfortable to them. Not laughing, or looking confused, breaks a small but real social contract.
What helps: You don't have to match the humour. Smiling and acknowledging the joke is usually enough. What doesn't work is treating every meeting like it needs to be humour-free to be serious.
British culturemeetshigh-context cultures
"Their 'yes' never seems to mean yes"
A British "yes" in a meeting can mean anything from real agreement to polite acknowledgement that you've finished speaking. "That's something we could look at" is not a commitment. "Leave it with me" may or may not result in action. Follow up in writing. A confirmation email after a verbal agreement isn't pushiness — in British working culture, it's normal practice.
What helps: Summarise agreed actions at the end of a meeting and send a brief follow-up. "Just confirming what we discussed..." is a completely acceptable — and very common — British professional move.

Five things about Britain worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your British colleagues are coming from.

1
The NHS is not a policy position — it's an identity
The National Health Service was founded in 1948 and is, for a large portion of the British population, the most cherished institution in the country — more so than Parliament, the monarchy, or any sports team. It was celebrated in the London 2012 Olympic opening ceremony. Criticising the NHS in a British workplace is a faster way to make enemies than almost any other topic. The fact that it is struggling and has real problems does not change the emotional weight it carries.
Conversation opener: Ask a British colleague what the NHS meant to their family. The personal stories are often powerful.
2
The class system produced the greatest class comedy in the world
Fawlty Towers, Downton Abbey, Keeping Up Appearances, Peep Show, The IT Crowd — British comedy is largely a sustained meditation on class anxiety, social embarrassment, and the gap between aspiration and reality. Knowing a little about the comedy tells you a lot about the culture. Basil Fawlty is not just a funny character; he's a precise social archetype — the person desperate to move up, terrified of being seen as common, treating everyone below him badly and everyone above him obsequiously.
Watch list: Fawlty Towers · Peep Show · The IT Crowd · Fleabag
3
Britain invented the modern world and is still working out how to feel about it
The Industrial Revolution, the railway, the World Wide Web, the jet engine, the structure of DNA, the vacuum cleaner — a disproportionate share of the foundational technologies of modern life came from a small island in Northern Europe. And simultaneously, the British Empire caused extraordinary suffering across four continents. Both things are true, and British culture is in a long, unresolved argument about how to hold both at once.
Conversation opener: Ask a younger British colleague what history they remember being taught about the Empire. The gap between generations is striking.
4
The music is a real export — and it's stranger than it looks
The Beatles, the Rolling Stones, David Bowie, Led Zeppelin, The Clash, Joy Division, Radiohead, Adele, Amy Winehouse. Britain's pop music output is extraordinary and wildly disproportionate to its size. The stranger story is why — a particular combination of art school culture, working-class ambition, BBC infrastructure, and fierce regional identity (Manchester, Liverpool, London, Bristol all producing distinct sounds) created the conditions for something exceptional. It wasn't an accident.
Conversation opener: Ask a British colleague which era they think was the peak. Strong opinions and mild arguments are guaranteed.
5
"Blitz spirit" is a real cultural reference — and it gets invoked constantly
The Second World War, and specifically the 1940–41 bombing of British cities, produced a mythology of stoic endurance, community solidarity, and cheerful resilience under extreme pressure. "Blitz spirit" is invoked in everything from sporting defeats to public health campaigns to Brexit arguments. Whether it's an accurate description of how the British actually are, or a flattering national story, is contested. Either way, it tells you something real about what the culture values.
Conversation opener: Ask a British colleague over 50 what their grandparents told them about the war. Almost everyone has a story.
South America · Caribbean coast

Venezuela

Warm, expressive, fiercely proud, and shaped by a depth of contradiction that most outsiders don't expect. Venezuelan working culture is relationship-driven and emotionally intelligent — and the Venezuelan colleagues you're most likely to meet have usually navigated more disruption, loss, and reinvention than their composure suggests.

Population 28M Capital: Caracas Language: Spanish Currency: Bolívar (VES)
VENEZUELA

A note on context that matters: Venezuela has experienced one of the most severe economic and political collapses of any modern country not at war. Since roughly 2013, hyperinflation, food and medicine shortages, institutional breakdown, and political repression have driven an estimated seven to eight million people out of the country — one of the largest displacement crises in the Western Hemisphere. The Venezuelan colleagues you're most likely to encounter in an international workplace are, in many cases, people who made the difficult decision to leave — who rebuilt careers, often from scratch, in a new country and a new context.

This matters not because it should define how you relate to them, but because it shapes who they are: resilient, resourceful, often highly adaptable, and carrying a complicated relationship with a homeland they may love deeply and be unable to return to. Treating that context with curiosity and respect — rather than ignorance or pity — is the starting point.

Beneath the crisis is a culture of real richness. Venezuela sits at the cultural intersection of Indigenous, Spanish colonial, and African influences, with a Caribbean temperament that is warmer, more expressive, and more musically alive than the South American average. Venezuelan identity is rooted in a deep pride in that cultural heritage — in the music, the food, the landscape, the way people come together — a cultural confidence that the current political situation has not extinguished. The Venezuelans working alongside you today come from a wide range of backgrounds and stories; what most of them share is the texture of that culture, and a relationship with home that is complicated, loving, and very much alive.

What to expect when working with Venezuelan colleagues — the patterns, defaults, and unspoken rules that shape how things actually get done.

Warmth is immediate, real, and not a professional strategy
Venezuelan professional culture is warm from the first moment. Greetings are effusive, eye contact is direct, physical closeness is normal, and the personal relationship is established before — and alongside — the professional one. The warmth is extended without calculation. It is simply how Venezuelans engage with people they're working with, and the warmth tends to be durable once given. Colleagues from cooler professional cultures sometimes mistake it for superficiality; it is usually the opposite.
Relationships carry the work — not the process
As in much of Latin America, the personal relationship is the infrastructure on which professional commitments are built. What gets done, how quickly, with how much goodwill and extra effort — all of this runs through the quality of the relationship, not the quality of the contract. A Venezuelan colleague who likes and trusts you will move mountains. A process without a relationship behind it is just paperwork. Invest in the person and the work tends to follow.
Expressiveness and emotion in professional settings are normal
Venezuelans communicate with their whole selves — voice, gesture, facial expression, volume. A passionate disagreement in a meeting is not a sign that the relationship is damaged; it is a sign that people are engaged and that the topic matters. Silence and restraint in a Venezuelan professional context can actually signal discomfort or disengagement, rather than the thoughtfulness they might signal in northern European cultures. If the room goes quiet, that's sometimes the more concerning sign.
Hierarchy exists — but warmth cuts across it
Venezuelan organisations can have real hierarchies, and seniority is respected. But the social register across levels tends to be warm and informal in a way that surprises colleagues expecting Latin formality. A senior person may be warm, jokey, and personally invested in junior colleagues' lives — while still being clearly in charge. The authority is real; the distance is not. This is a culture where the boss might ask about your family and mean it, while also expecting their decisions to be implemented without extensive pushback.
Flexibility with time is cultural — and chronic uncertainty has deepened it
Venezuelan culture has long had a relaxed relationship with punctuality and scheduling. Decades of instability — power cuts, shortages, bureaucratic unpredictability — have layered onto this a deep pragmatism about plans: things change, you adapt, rigidity doesn't survive contact with reality. This produces colleagues who are often excellent under pressure and brilliant at improvising — and who may need a nudge when fixed deadlines are immovable.
Practical tips
→
Invest in the greeting. Don't rush past the personal opening of a conversation. Ask about family, about how things are going — with real interest. The five minutes you spend there pays off in everything that follows.
→
Read energy, not just words. Venezuelans communicate a great deal through tone, expression, and body language. What someone says and how they say it are both data.
→
Be direct about hard deadlines. Not because Venezuelan colleagues are unreliable — they often aren't — but because flexibility is the default assumption. Making the non-negotiable ones explicit is fair to everyone.
→
Don't lead with the crisis. The political situation in Venezuela is real and heavy. Your Venezuelan colleague knows this better than you do. Let them bring it up; don't make it the first thing you associate them with.
→
Match the warmth. A flat, transactional register in a relationship-first culture signals that you're not really present. You don't have to be effusive — just human.

Every culture has rules that nobody spells out — things that seem obvious from the inside and invisible from the outside. These are some of the ones worth knowing before you find out the hard way.

The diaspora is enormous — and "Venezuelan" now includes everywhere
An estimated seven to eight million Venezuelans live outside the country — in Colombia, Peru, Chile, Spain, the US, and beyond. This means that "Venezuelan working culture" increasingly describes people operating in contexts far from Venezuela, often code-switching between two or more cultural registers simultaneously. Your Venezuelan colleague may be deeply Venezuelan in some respects and deeply adapted to their new context in others. The most useful thing you can do is ask — not assume.
In practice: "How long have you been here?" and "how did you find the transition?" are warm questions that open real conversation — and signal that you see them as a whole person, not just a job title.
Pride in Venezuela's culture runs deep, separate from politics
The economic and political crisis is not what Venezuelans think about when they think about being Venezuelan. They think about the food, the music, the landscape, the warmth of the people, the way parties work, the way families gather. Venezuela produced some of the most celebrated music, cuisine, and natural landscapes in South America. When a Venezuelan colleague's face lights up talking about home, they're not being delusional about the political situation — they're accessing something real that the political situation hasn't taken from them.
In practice: Ask about the culture, not the crisis. "What do you miss most?" or "what should I know about Venezuelan food?" opens warmth that political conversation rarely does.
Food is emotional territory
Venezuelan food — arepas, pabellón criollo, cachapas, tequeños, hallacas at Christmas — carries the specific emotional weight that home food carries everywhere, amplified for people who are far from home and may not be able to return easily. A Venezuelan colleague who makes you an arepa or insists you try their food is offering something real. The appropriate response is real interest, not polite nibbling.
In practice: Accept the food. Ask what it is and what makes it theirs specifically. The conversation that follows is usually worth more than whatever else you were planning to talk about.
The crisis doesn't define them — but it has shaped them
Venezuelans who have navigated the last decade have, almost without exception, developed a resourcefulness and tolerance for uncertainty that is unusual. Many have rebuilt careers from zero in unfamiliar countries, learned new languages, and maintained family bonds across borders. The colleague who seems remarkably calm under pressure, who adapts quickly, who finds creative solutions to resource constraints — often, this comes from somewhere specific.
In practice: Don't treat resilience as a given or take it for granted. Acknowledging what someone has navigated — when they've shared it — is not pity. It's respect.
Nicknames are affection, not informality
Venezuelans use nicknames — apodos — with an ease that can surprise colleagues from more formal cultures. Gordo (chubby), Flaco (skinny), Negro (dark), Mono (blond) — these are terms of endearment, not insults. Someone who would be offended by a stranger commenting on their appearance will happily answer to their apodo from a colleague for their entire working life. Being given a nickname is a signal of closeness. If a Venezuelan colleague gives you one, you've been accepted into something real.
In practice: Don't correct the nickname or look uncomfortable — that breaks the warmth the gesture was offering. And don't try to assign nicknames yourself until you know each other well; the giving is earned, not claimed.

Most friction between colleagues from different cultures isn't about bad intentions — it's about different defaults. Here's what's often going on underneath.

Venezuelan culturemeetsstructured, deadline-driven cultures
"They're great in person but hard to pin down on deliverables"
The warmth and enthusiasm in the relationship are real — and so is the cultural default toward flexibility on timelines. These are not in contradiction; they're just different variables. Someone who is committed to you and relaxed about when the thing arrives is not being inconsistent — they're operating two separate systems simultaneously.
What helps: Separate the relationship conversation from the deadline conversation. "I really want this to work — and I need to be honest that the deadline is the 14th and I can't move it" is clearer and more effective than hoping the relational commitment will translate automatically into on-time delivery.
Venezuelan culturemeetslow-context, task-focused cultures
"They take everything personally"
In a relationship-first culture, the professional and the personal are not kept in separate boxes. Criticism of work can land as criticism of the person, because the person has invested themselves in the work and the relationship. The same emotional investment that makes feedback feel personal is what makes Venezuelan colleagues give real personal commitment to things they care about. The same emotional investment that makes feedback feel personal is what makes Venezuelan colleagues give real personal commitment to things they care about.
What helps: Frame feedback within the relationship. "I really value what you bring to this — and I want to flag something I think we should fix" lands very differently than the same substantive point delivered cold.
Venezuelan culturemeetslow-context, bottom-line cultures
"They take forever to get to the point"
Venezuelan communication is storytelling-first. Before sharing a fact or a decision, a Venezuelan colleague will typically lay out the full context first — the background, the relationships involved, the sequence of events. The context is doing real work: without it, the conclusion doesn't fully land. In cultures that expect a headline followed by supporting detail, this can feel like burying the point. It helps to give it the space it needs, especially early in a relationship.
What helps: Give it space, especially in a relationship you're building. When time is short, a warm and direct signal works well: "I want to hear the full story — can you give me the headline first so I can ask the right questions?" That's respectful, not dismissive.
Venezuelan culturemeetsdirect-signal cultures
"I never know what's actually going on — they're always positive"
Venezuelan communication tends to stay warm and forward-looking even when things are difficult. Saying that something is a serious problem, that a relationship has broken down, or that you're struggling — these things are often softened or deferred in a culture that values keeping the relational atmosphere positive. What you get in the meeting may be more optimistic than the reality behind it.
What helps: Create private, low-stakes moments for honest check-ins. One-to-one conversations in an informal setting — coffee, a walk — surface what the formal meeting often doesn't. Ask specifically: "What's the hardest part of this right now?" gives permission to name it.
Venezuelan diasporameetsnew-country workplaces
"They seem over-qualified and under-challenged — why are they in this role?"
A significant number of Venezuelan professionals in international workplaces are operating well below their original professional level — doctors working as nurses, lawyers in administrative roles, engineers in junior technical positions — because their qualifications weren't recognised, their language skills needed time to develop, or they needed an income quickly after arriving somewhere new. This is not a signal about their capabilities. It's a signal about the circumstances that brought them to this role.
What helps: Ask about their background directly and with real interest. Most Venezuelan professionals will tell you — and the gap between what they've done and what they're currently doing is often both striking and something they're actively working to close.

Five things about Venezuela worth knowing — good conversation starters, and better still for understanding where your Venezuelan colleagues are coming from.

1
Venezuela was one of the wealthiest countries in Latin America — and the speed of the collapse is historically unusual
In the 1970s, Venezuela had the highest per capita income in Latin America, a growing middle class, and oil revenues that funded real development. The country that collapsed into hyperinflation and mass emigration in the 2010s is the same country — within living memory of many working-age Venezuelans. This is not a country with no memory of better times. The particular quality of Venezuelan grief about the current situation comes from knowing, in detail, what was lost and when.
Conversation opener: Ask a Venezuelan colleague over 40 what Venezuela was like when they were growing up. The contrast with the present is often the most honest window into how they carry the situation.
2
El Sistema — music as social transformation, and one of Venezuela's most remarkable exports
El Sistema — a publicly funded classical music education programme founded in 1975 — trained hundreds of thousands of children from poor communities as classical musicians and produced Gustavo Dudamel, one of the most celebrated conductors in the world. El Sistema became a global model for using music as social intervention, replicated in dozens of countries. For a country known internationally mainly for oil and crisis, it is a remarkable legacy — and one that most Venezuelans are quietly very proud of.
Conversation opener: Ask a Venezuelan colleague if they know El Sistema or Gustavo Dudamel. The pride is usually immediate.
3
The landscape is extraordinary — and Venezuelans know it
Venezuela contains the world's highest waterfall (Angel Falls / Salto Ángel), the Orinoco Delta, Caribbean beaches, Andean peaks, the Gran Sabana's ancient tepui plateaus, and the Los Llanos wetlands — one of the most biodiverse ecosystems on earth. For Venezuelans, the natural beauty of the country is not a tourist-brochure abstraction — it's personal. The fact that much of it is inaccessible to most Venezuelans right now makes the attachment more acute, not less.
Conversation opener: Ask a Venezuelan colleague about a place in Venezuela they miss or would want you to see. Almost everyone has one, and the answer is usually vivid.
4
The arepa is not just food — it's a daily ritual and a cultural anchor
The arepa — a simple round corn cake, griddled or baked, split and filled — is eaten at breakfast, lunch, and dinner across Venezuela. Every family has its fillings, its technique, its arguments about the right cornmeal. For Venezuelans living abroad, making arepas is often the most direct act of cultural continuity available — the thing that most immediately brings home into the present. Understanding this — that what looks like a simple snack is actually a daily act of cultural maintenance — tells you something real about how Venezuelans carry their identity.
Conversation opener: Ask a Venezuelan colleague what goes in their ideal arepa. Clear twenty minutes and be prepared to have opinions you didn't know you needed.
5
Baseball is the national sport — and it surprises almost everyone
Unlike most of South America, Venezuela's dominant sport is baseball — a legacy of the American oil industry presence that took root so completely it became entirely its own. Several Venezuelan players are MLB stars. Knowing any of them — Miguel Cabrera, Johan Santana, Pablo Sandoval — is a fast way to establish unexpected common ground. Asking a Venezuelan colleague about béisbol and watching the reaction tells you something real about how sport functions as identity here.
Conversation opener: Ask a Venezuelan colleague which team they support or whether they follow any Venezuelan players in MLB. The enthusiasm is usually immediate and generous.
Cultural Atlas
